They managed to damage the transport Deucalion which was leading the port wing column. More bombs fell close to several other ships.

Finally, at 1345 hours, the two Reggiane fighters approached HMS Victorious as if to land on. They looked like Hurricanes and HMS Victorious was at that time engaged in landing her own fighters. They managed to drop their bombs and one hit the flight deck amidships. Fortunately the bomb broke up without exploding. By the time HMS Victorious could open fire both fighters were out of range.

The Deucalion could no longer keep up with the convoy and was ordered to follow the inshore route along the Tunisian coast escorted by HMS Bramham. Two bombers found these ships late in the afternoon, but their bombs missed. At 1940 hours, however, near the Cani Rocks, two torpedo aircraft attacked and a torpedo hit the Deucalion. She caught fire and eventually blew up.

The convoy passed some 20 miles north of Galita Island and spent the afternoon avoiding enemy submarines which were known to be concentrated in these waters. There were innumerable reports of sightings and Asdic contacts and at least two submarines proved dangerous. At 1616 hours, HMS Pathfinder and HMS Zetland attacked one on the port bow of the convoy and hunted her until the convoy was out of reach. HMS Ithuriel, stationed on the quarter, then attacked, forced the enemy to surface and finally rammed her. She proved to be the Italian submarine Cobalto. Meanwhile HMS Tartar, on the starboard quarter, saw six torpedoes fired at close range at 1640 hours, and the next destroyer in the screen, HMS Lookout sighted a periscope. Together they attacked the submarine, continuing until it was no longer dangerous. There was no evidence this submarine was sunk.

At 1750 hours, HMS Ithuriel, which was on her way back to the convoy after sinking the Italian submarine Cobalto was attacked by a few dive-bombers, when still a dozen miles astern of the convoy. At this time the convoy came under attack by aircraft stationed on Sicily. This force numbered nearly 100 aircraft. Ju.87 dive-bombers as well as Ju.88’s and SM-79’s all with a strong escort of fighters. The enemy started attacking at 1835 hours, the bombers attacking from both ahead and astern which last was the direction of the sun. The torpedo aircraft came from ahead to attack on the starboard bow and beam of the convoy.

The Italian SM-79’s torpedo bombers dropped their torpedoes from ranges of about 3000 yards outside the destroyer screen, and once again the convoy turned away to avoid them. However the destroyer HMS Foresight was hit by a torpedo and disabled. The bombers chose HMS Indomitable as their main target. She was astern of HMS Rodney at the time on the port quarter of the convoy. Four Ju.88’s and eight Ju.87’s came suddenly out of the sun and dived steeply towards HMS Indomitable from astern. Some of the Ju.87 came down to 1000 feet and the carrier received three hits and her flight deck was put out of action. Her airborne fighters eventually had to land on HMS Victorious. HMS Rodney meanwhile had a narrow escape when a bomber attacked from ahead. One enemy aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by AA fire from the ships while the fighters claimed nine more although there were about twice as much enemy fighters in the air then British.

HMS Tartar took the damaged HMS Foresight in tow and proceeded westward for Gibraltar. Next day, as they were shadowed by enemy aircraft, and enemy submarines were known to be in the area, it was decided to scuttle the cripple before both ships might be lost. HMS Tartar then torpedoed HMS Foresight a few miles from Galita Island.
Passage through the narrows, 12-13 August 1942, and the loss off HMS Manchester.

These last air attacks took place about 20 nautical miles west of the Skerki Channel and at 1900 hours, when the attacks were clearly over, Vice-Admiral Syfret turned away with ‘Force Z’. It was now up to Rear-Admiral Burrough with ‘Force X’ to take the convoy to Malta.

At 2000 hours, when the convoy was changing it’s formation from four to two columns, the convoy was attacked by Italian submarines. The submarine Dessiè attacked a freighter with four torpedoes and claimed three hits. The sound of the torpedo hits was however not caused by her attack but by an attack by the Axum which hit three ships, HMS Nigeria, HMS Cairo and the tanker Ohio.

HMS Nigeria had to turn back to make for Gibraltar escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Derwent, HMS Wilton and HMS Bicester. Rear-Admiral Burrough transferred his flag to the destroyer HMS Ashanti. The stern of HMS Cairo had been blown off and she had to be sunk as she was beyond salvage with both engines also out of action. She was scuttled by HMS Pathfinder. The Ohio meanwhile managed to struggle on.

At this time the convoy was still trying to form up the the submarine attacks messed things up and right at thus time the convoy was once more attacked from the air in the growing dusk at 2030 hours. About 20 German aircraft, Ju-88’s made dive bombing and torpedo attacks, hitting the Empire Hope with a bomb and the Clan Ferguson and Brisbane Star with torpedoes. The first of these ships had to be sunk (by HMS Bramham, the second blew up but the last eventually reached Malta. Soon after this attack, at 2111 hours, HMS Kenya was torpedoed by the Italian submarine Alagi. She was able to evade three of the four torpedoes but was hit in the bow by the fouth. She was however able to remain with the convoy.

The situation was then as follows. HMS Kenya and HMS Manchester with two merchant ships, and with the minesweeping destroyers HMS Intrepid, HMS Icarus and HMS Fury sweeping ahead, had passed the Skerki Channel and were steering to pass Zembra Island on the way to Cape Bon. HMS Ashanti, with Rear-Admiral Burrough on board was fast overhauling these ships. The other two destroyers HMS Pathfinder, HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury, were rounding up the remaining nine merchant ships. The escort destroyer HMS Bramham was also catching up after having escorted the single Deucalion until she sank.

On learing about the fate of HMS Nigeria and HMS Cairo, Vice-Admiral Syfret detached HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali to reinforce Rear-Admiral Burrough. It would take these ships several hourse to catch up with the convoy.

The main body of the convoy passed Cape Bon around midnight. Fourty minutes later enemy Motor Torpedo Boats appeared and started to attack. Their first victim was HMS Manchester which was torpedoed at 0120 B/13 by the Italian MS 16 or MS 22. She had to be scuttled by her own crew. Many of her ships company landed in Tunisia and were interned by the Vichy-French but about 300 were picked up by destroyers (first by HMS Pathfinder, and later by HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. These last two destoyers then set off towards Gibraltar.)

Four and possibly five of the merchant ships were also hit by the Motor Torpedo Boats. These were the Wairangi, Rochester Castle, Almeria Lykes, Santa Elisa and probably the Glenorchy. They were attacked between 0315 and 0430 hours about 15 nautical miles south-east of Kelibia whilst taking a short cut to overhaul the main body of the convoy. Four were lost, only the Rochester Castle survived and she managed to catch up with the main body of the convoy at 0530 hours. The Glenorchy was sunk by the Italian MS 31, the other four, of which the Rochester Castle survived as mentioned earlier, were hit by the German S 30 and S 36 as well as the Italian MAS 554 and MAS 557.

Shortly before 0530 hours HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali had joined the main body of the convoy making the force now two cruisers and seven destroyers with the transports Rochester Castle, Waimarama and Melbourne Star. The damaged tanker Ohio was slowly catching up. With her was the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury. Astern of the main body was the Port Chalmers escorted by the destroyer HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Bramham. The destroyers recued the crew of the Santa Elisa when the passed by the abandoned ship which was afterwards finished off by a German bomber. The Dorset was proceeding without escort and lastly the damaged Brisbane Star was still keeping close to the Tunisian coast independently, intending to steer towards Malta after nightfall.

At 0730 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, sent back HMS Tartar and HMS Somali to Kelibia to assist HMS Manchester and then go to Gibraltar. When they arrived they found out that the Manchester had been scuttled several hours earlier so they rescued those of her crew that had not reached the shore yet and then made off to Gibraltar as ordered. Besides crew of the Manchester they also picked up survivors from the Almeria Lykes and Wairangi.

The next encounter with the enemy was an air attack on the main body of the convoy at 0800 hours by German bombers. About 12 Ju.88’s made a shallow diving attack coming down from 6000 feet to 2000 feet to drop their bombs. Two dived on the Waimarama hitting her several times and she blew up immediately, one of the bombers was even destroyed in the explosion. HMS Ledbury saved some of her crew out of the blazing sea. At 0925 hours, when the Ohio, Port Chalmers and Dorset where with the main body again, a few Ju.87’s escorted by Italian fighters attacked. They dived down to 1500 to 1000 feet. HMS Kenya leading the port column, and the Ohio last ship but one in the starboard column, had narrow escapes. One of the enemy aircraft crashed on board the Ohio just after having released it’s bomb after being damaged by gunfire from the Ohio and HMS Ashanti. Another aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by fighters from Malta that had been patrolling overhead since daybreak.
Arrivals at Malta 13-15 August 1942.

At 1050 hours, about 20 bombers, mostly Ju.88’s with a few Ju.87’s, came in to attack. Target was the Ohio and she received four or five near misses and her engines were disabled. At the same time the Rochester Castle in the port column was near-missed and set on fire but she continued with the convoy. The Dorset which was astern of her was hit and stopped. The convoy went on leaving the Dorset behind with the Ohio and two destroyers.

At 1125 hours the last air attack on the main body took place. Five Italian SM.79’s attacked with torpedoes and almost hit the Port Chalmers as the torpedo got stuck in the paravane. Further attacks on the main body were held of by fighters from Malta. At 1430 hours, four minesweepers from Malta joined the main body of the convoy, these were HMS Speedy (Lt.Cdr. A.E. Doran, RN, with the group’s commander A/Cdr. H.J.A.S. Jerome, RN on board), HMS Hebe, HMS Rye and HMS Heyte. Also with them were seven Motor Launches; ML 121, ML 126, ML 134, ML 135, ML 168, ML 459 and ML 462. HMS Rye and two of the ML’s were sent towards the damaged Ohio which was ‘vital for Malta’, according to A/Cdr. Jerome.

At 1600 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, set course to the west with his two cruisers and with five destroyers. The Port Chalmers, Melbourne Star and Rochester Castle arrived in Grand Harbour around 1800 hours with the force of A/Cdr. Jerome. The Rochester Castle was by that time very low in the water, she had just made it into port on time.

Out were still the Ohio, Dorset and the Brisbane Star. The valuable Ohio had been helpless with HMS Penn and HMS Bramham. When HMS Rye arrived at 1730 hours, HMS Penn took the Ohio in tow. Meanwhile HMS Bramham was sent to the Dorset but soon afterwards German bombers came again and the ships were attacked repeatedly until dark. Both merchantman were hit around 1900 hours and the Dorset sank.

At daylight on the 14th HMS Ledbury arrived to help bringing the Ohio to Malta. HMS Speedy also soon arrived on the scene with two ML’s. The rest of his force he had sent to search for the Brisbane Star. At 1045 hours, enemy aircraft made their last attempt, causing the parting of the tow. Fighter from Malta shot down two of the attackers. The tow was passed again and the slow procession went on and in the morning of the 15th the vital tanker finally reached Malta.

The Brisbane Star had by then also arrived. She left the Tunisian coast at dusk on the 13th. Aircraft had attacked her unsuccessfully and one of the attackers was shot down by a Beaufighter escort that had been sent from Malta. She arrived at Malta in the afternoon of the 14th.
Italian surface ships to operate against the convoy ?

The convoy had experienced the violence of the enemy in every shape except that of an attack by large surface ships. Yet Italian cruisers and destroyers had been at sea to intercept and attack it. Two light cruiser had left Cagliari in the evening of 11 August 1942 and the heavy cruisers Gorizia and Bolzano from Messina, and a light cruiser from Naples had sailed on the morning of the 12th. That evening reconnaissance aircraft reported one heavy and two light cruisers with eight destroyers about 80 nautical miles to the north of the western tip of Sicily and steering south. It would have been possible for this force to meet the convoy at dawn on the 13th so the shadowing aircraft was therefore ordered in plain language to illuminate and attack. This apparently influenced the Italians as they had limited air cover and they turned back at 0130 B/13 when near Cape San Vito. At 0140 hours the aircraft reported that it had dropped its bombs but no hits had been obtained. Similar orders were signalled, in plain language, to relief shadowers and to report the position of the enemy force to the benefit of imaginary Liberator bombers in case the Italians would change their minds and turn back. They however held on to the eastward.

The submarine HMS P 42 sighted them around 0800 B/13 off Stromboli and attacked with four torpedoes claiming two hits. She had in fact hit the heavy cruiser Bolzano which was able to proceed northwards and the light cruiser Muzio Attendolo which managed to reach Messina with her bows blown off. The other cruisers went to Naples. Following the attack P 42 was heavily depth charged by the destroyers but managed to escape.

In fact the following Italian ships had been at sea; heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trieste, Bolzano, light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia Raimondo Montecuccoli, Muzio Attendolo. They were escorted by eleven destroyers; Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Fuceliere, Geniere, Legionaro, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani, Grecale and Maestrale.
The return to Gibraltar.

The British ships returning to Gibraltar had better fortune. Having left the convoy off Malta in the afternoon of the 13th, they rounded Cape Bon around 0130 B/14 and from that point until past Zembra Island they successful ran the gauntled of E-boats laying in wait.

at 0450 B/14, near the Fratelli Rocks, a submarine fired torpedoes at HMS Ashanti from the surface. She was nearly rammed by HMS Kenya, which was next astern of the ‘flagship’ (Rear-Admiral Burrough was still in HMS Ashanti). The inevitable shadowers arrived soon after daylight to herald their air attacks that began at 0730 hours. They lasted until around 1315 hours. German bombers came in first with three attemps by a few Ju.88’s. This was followed by a more severe attack with about 30 bombers, Ju-88’s and Ju-87’s between 1030 and 1050 hours. An hour later 15 Savoia high-level bombers attacked followed until 1315 hours by torpedo-carrying Savoia’s. Around 20 aircraft attacking single or in pairs. Also aircraft are though to be laying mines ahead. Several ships were near missed, but no further damage was sustained. After these attacks the British were left alone and in the evening they joined ‘Force Z’.

Vice-Admiral Syfret had gone as far west as 01’E where he ordered the damaged carrier HMS Indomitable to proceed to Malta with HMS Rodney and a destroyer screen made up of HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland. He then turned back to the east to make rendez-vous with Rear-Admiral Burrough. HMS Rodney, HMS Indomitable, HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland arrived at Gibraltar in the evening of the 14th.

A few hours before they arrived the damaged HMS Nigeria and her escort had also entered port, as had HMS Tartar, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. On her way back HMS Nigeria had been attacked by torpedo-bombers and a submarine but she had not been hit.
Conclusion.

Out of the fourteen ships that had sailed only five arrived ‘safe’ at Malta. This was not a very high score also given the very heavy escort that had been provided also taken in mind that an aircraft carrier, a light cruiser, an AA cruiser an a destroyer had been lost and two heavy cruiser had been damaged. But the convoy had to meet very heavy air attacks by over 150 bombers and 80 torpedo aircraft, all in the space of two days. Also these aircraft were protected by fighter in much greater strength that the carriers and Malta could provide. And there were also the enemy submarines and E-boats.

The spirit in which to operation was carried out appears in Vice-Admiral Syfret’s report: ‘ Tribute has been paid to the personnel of His Majesty’s Ships, both the officers and men will desire to give first place to the conduct, courage, and determination of the masters, officers, and men of the merchant ships. The steadfast manner in which these ships pressed on their way to Malta through all attacks, answering every maneuvering order like a well trained fleet unit, was a most inspiring sight. Many of these fine men and their ships were lost. But the memory of their conduct will remain an inspiration to all who were privileged to sail with them.
Movements of the Italian Fleet during operation Pedestal.

The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli (7th Cruiser Division, Divisional Admiral Alberto Da Zara) escorted by the destroyers Ascari, Alfredo Oriani (leader) and Vincenzo Gioberti (10th Destroyer Squadron), sailed from Cagliari at 1605 B/13-1625 B/13. They were sighted by HMS P 211 at 2020 B/13 in position 38°42'N, 10°29'E. They were attacked by HMS P 43 at 1840 B/13 and missed in position 38°46.5'N, 09°49'E. They arrived at Palermo at 0726 B/14-0826 B/14.

At 1910 B/14-2006 B/14, the squadron sailed from Palermo escorted by Alfredo Oriani, Ascari, Ugolino Vivaldi, Vincenzo Gioberti and Lanzerotto Malocello. The destroyer Premuda sailed from Trapani at 1745 B/14 to join them. After midnight, Vincenzo Gioberti had turn back due to defects and returned to Palermo at 0500 B/15.

At 0532 B/15, in position 190° - Punta Sciaccazza (Pantelleria) - 18 miles, The Italian squadron sighted two enemy cruisers and several destroyers of the Tribal and J/K/N-classes, bearing 220°, course 135°, 15 knots, at a range of 22000 metres. At a distance from them was a convoy consisting of a 12000-ton troop transport, a tanker and three merchant ships of about 6,000 tons, escorted by four Tribal-class destroyers. Immediately, Eugenio di Savoia catapulted a RO 43 seaplane and at 0536 B/14 (it later crashed at sea, the pilot was recovered by a MAS boat but the navigating officer was missing), Raimondo Montecuccoli also launched a RO 43 seaplane but it was forced by engine defects to abort the mission and landed in Sicily. The two cruisers increased speed to 28 knot and a couple of minutes later to 32 knots and opened fire with their 152mm/53 guns, with the third salvo straddling the enemy. The British gunfire soon straddled the Italian cruiser. At 0543 B/15, five enemy destroyers closed to attack, the leading destroyer was of the Tribal-class, followed by a J/K/N-class, the other three could not be identified. At 0546 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia opened fire on the leading destroyer with her main armament and as the range has now decreased, the 100mm/47 also joined. The Italian destroyers also opened fire at 0551 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres (their extreme range). Three minutes later, they were ordered to attack the convoy which was observed to turn back while enemy destroyers laid a smoke screen. At 0555 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia was hit by an enemy round on the 70mm armoured belt on the starboard side, two ratings were killed and eight were wounded. In the meantime, at 0559 B/15, the Italian destroyers had closed the enemy convoy at 5800 metres and the enemy destroyers at 5000 metres. Ugolini Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello each launched two torpedoes. At 0605 B/15, a merchant vessel appeared to be hit by a torpedo. Seven minutes later, an enemy destroyer was hit and the explosion was followed by a dense smoke, she withdrew from the action. At 0600 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia fired one torpedo. At 0609 B/15, the Italian cruisers resumed their fire on the enemy cruisers at a range of 17000 metres but observation was difficult due to the smoke from the enemy ships. At 0620 B/15, the destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi was hit by a round in the forward engine room which had to be evacuated, ten men were killed and eight wounded. Admiral da Zara ordered the destroyers to give her assistance. Alfredo Oriani and Ascari were unable to do so due to the enemy fire. Only Lanzerotto Malocello stood by the stricken destroyer, and laid smoke to cover her. At 0645 B/15, the enemy destroyers were now at 4500 metres and Ugolino Vivaldi fired two torpedoes at the destroyer with the letters H 68 clearly seen on her bows [This was HMS Foresight]. Shortly after, she managed to increase speed at 13 knots. The enemy cruiser was firing at a range of 19000 metres at the Italian destroyers which could not reply due to the distance. At 0659 B/15, the leading enemy cruiser appeared to have been seriously hit, and the escorting destroyers covered her with smoke. By 0717 B/15, the enemy formation had completely disappeared from view, but a big detonation was observed. At 0735 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi sighted Premuda, Ascari and Alfredo Oriani and the former joined to escort her. At 0757 B/15, a violent fire developed in Ugolino Vivaldi's forward engine room the forward magazine had to be flooded, and ammunition thrown overboard as a precaution. At 0919 B/15, Lanzerotto Malocello took her damaged sistership in tow and they proceed to Pantelleria. The enemy appeared briefly at 0801 B/15, and the cruisers resumed fire for a minute before contact was definitely lost. At 0935 B/15, an enemy bomber of the Beaufort type attacked Eugenio di Savoia, one torpedo missed astern and the other sank in mid-course. In the meantime, the cruisers were still in action with the enemy and, at 1156 B/15, a large explosion was observed with a white column of smoke of over 1000 metres in height believed to be from an enemy ammunition ship. At 1223 B/15, next to the enemy merchant ship on fire, two destroyers were observed and twelve minutes later, fire was opened on one of them at the range of 19500 metres. It was certainly hit and masked by smoke from the other destroyer. Another two destroyers were then sighted next to another merchant ship on fire and fire was opened on one of them at 1259 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres. The destroyer blew up at 1325 B/15 and sank rapidly. In the meantime, Alfredo Oriani and Ascari opened fire at a tanker at a range of 12000 metres which was at repeatedly hit at 1302 B/15-1304 B/15 and set afire. Italian bombers. At 1343 B/15, Alfredo Oriani launched a torpedo at the tanker, but it was only a muffled hit. At 1423 B/15-1437 B/15, six enemy bombers attacked Alfredo Oriani and Ascari; they were not hit. In the action, Eugenio di Savoia fired 838 rounds of 152mm rounds, 690 rounds of 100mm, 978 rounds of 37mm and 973 rounds of 13.2mm, Raimondo Montecuccoli fired 704 rounds of 152mm rounds, 368 rounds of 100mm rounds and 567 rounds of 37mm. The cruisers came under air attack at 1428 B/15 by enemy bombers and at 1600 B/15 by three torpedo bombers. One of the latter was shot down by Italian fighters. Shortly after, the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Premuda joined the cruisers. Premuda was detached and reached Trapani at 2105 B/15. The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli, escorted by the destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Lanzerotto Malocello and Ascari reached Naples at 1000 B/16-1154 B/16.

The tribulations of Ugolino Vivaldi. She was proceeding to Pantelleria, towed by Lanzerotto Malocello and escorted by Premuda. At 0927 B/15, the towing cable broke and Ugolino Vivaldi ordered Premuda to take over the towing and, nine minutes later, four Swordfish bombers attacked Lanzerotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi, but they managed to avoid their torpedoes. At 0948 B/15, another Beaufort bomber missed astern the destroyer Ascari. At 1003 B/15, the destroyers Ascari and Alfredo Oriani rejoined the cruisers. At 1019 B/15, Premuda managed to pass a tow line to Ugolino Vivavli which was still on fire and were proceeding to Pantelleria, fifteen minutes later, they were sighted at long range by the Italian cruisers. At 1218 B/15, two minesweepers from Pantelleria joined them and were asked to provide all the available fire extinguishers and at 1400 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi dropped anchor at Scauri (Pantelleria). At 1640 B/15, she ordered Premuda and Lanzerotto Malocello to leave the formation and rejoin the 7th Cruiser Division. At 2000 B/15, The damaged destroyer was joined by the tug Salvatore I and the fire was finally extinguished an hour later. At 0728 B/16, Ugolino Vivaldi departed from Pantelleria towed by Salvatore I, but the tug had engine defects and could not proceed at more than 5 knots. They were escorted by the torpedo boats Castore and Polluce and the tug Terracina and reached Trapani at 0154 B/17. (22)
3 Aug 1942 At 1100 B/3, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) made landfall on Capri Island and proceeded to patrol about 6 miles miles south of Campanella Point (to the south of the Bocca Piccola).

At 1415 B/3, sighted ship at a range of 10 miles steering 335° heading for the Bocco Piccola. This was subsequently identified as a hospital ship and was thought to be the Italian Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924).
The hospital ship was the Arno (8024 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Derna at 2015 B/1 and arrived at Naples at 1545 B/3.

At 1630 B/3, P 42 sighted a merchant ship that was escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat and a Cant Z 501 flying boat in position 200° - Carena Point (Capri) - 7 miles. P 42 closed to attack but the convoy passed about 6 miles off track, rounded Carena Point and proceeded towards Naples. The ship was in ballast and was thought to be of the Capo Lena-class of 4800 tons.

This was the Italian transport Amsterdam (8673 GRT, built 1927) carrying PoWs and escorted by the torpedo boat Centauro. They had sailed from Tripoli at 1737 B/1-1830 B/1 and arrived at Naples at 2000 B/3-2025 B/3.
At 2130 B/3, HMS P 42 surfaced and made off to the eastwards to avoid schooner patrol south of the Bocca Piccola.
At 2255 B/3, HMS P 42 dived to avoid a patrol vessel or motor torpedo boat. (19)
6 Aug 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel off Capri Island with three torpedoes. No hits were obtained.
This was the Italian transport Argentina (5085 GRT, built 1907) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa.

They had sailed from Naples at 1724 B/6 for Taranto. At 2027 B/6, Generale Achille Papa observed three torpedo tracks in position 40°25'10"N, 14°19'20"E, and immediately combed them, dropping a minute later, a pattern of four 100-kg depth charges set at a depth of 50 metres. Shortly after, an oil patch was observed, and the submarine was believed to be damaged. The torpedo boat circled and dropped two pairs of 100-kg depth charges set at 75 metres. More oil slicks and bubbles of air were seen and eight 30-kg depth charges were dropped at intervals. At 2045 B/6, four 100-kg depth charges set at 75 metres and Generale Achille Papa left the scene to rejoin Argentina. The next day at 0633 B, Argentina signalled that she had a defective condenser and must proceed to Messina. They arrived there at 1120 B/7.

1850 B/6 - When in position 150°, Campanella Point, 7 nautical miles sighted smoke through the Bocca Piccola. Then the funnel and masts of a ship steering approximately 235°. As she appeared to be going north of Capri diced to run to the west at speed.
1940 B/6 - Having run 3 nautical miles on course 260° the ship was observed to the north-west of Carena Point lighthouse (Capri) at a range of 8 nautical miles steering approximately 220°.

1957 B/6 - The ship altered course to 140°, putting P 42 in a good attacking position. The ship was of about 7000 tons. She was later seen to be escorted by a three-funnelled torpedo-boat. Started attack.
2023 B/6 - In approximate position 40°35'N, 14°10'E fired three torpedoes from 2000 yards. No hits were obtained.
2031 B/6 - Counter attacked commenced. 13 Depth charges in all were dropped. None were close and no damage was caused.
2047 B/6 - The last depth charge was dropped.
2050 B/6 - Altered course to the westward.
2138 B/6 - Surfaced, made off to the south-west but sighted a schooner on bearing 135° at a range of 1 mile. Dived again.
2251 B/6 - Surfaced. Nothing in sight. Made off to the south-west at 10 knots. (19)

8 Aug 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) makes a torpedo attack on the Italian transport Algerino (1370 GRT, built 1921) off Capri. The target was not hit. According to Italian sources she was unescorted.
The torpedo was reported to be on the beach, unexploded, and the fishing vessel B 206 / Lo Bianco was asked to retrieve it.

Following the attack, Algerino took refuge at Vibo Valentia. The transport Amba Alagi (3710 GRT, built 1910), which had also sailed from Naples for Milazzo and Trapani was in the vicinity, and was warned of the submarine. However, she did not acknowledge receipt of the signal and MAS 547 and MAS 556 which had sailed from Messina at 1959 B/8 to hunt the submarine, were asked to contact her. Amba Alagi had engine defects and had already proceeded to Vibo Valentia where she arrived at 1900 B/8. At 0748 B/8, the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz, escorting the transports Albisola (4097 GRT, built 1927) and Castelverde (6666 GRT, built 1921), had sailed from Naples for Cagliari. At 1500 B/8, she was ordered to leave the convoy and proceed to the assistance of Algerino. At 1840 B/8, she was ordered to return to Naples. Aircraft were also sent to the area. The transport Giacomo C. (4638 GRT, built 1920) and Anna Maria (1205 GRT, built 1912), escorted by the auxiliary Francesco Morosini (2423 GRT, built 1928), and proceeding from Naples to Palermo and Trapani, were informed of the presence of a submarine. The transport Romagna (1416 GRT, built 1899) escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa, which had sailed from Messina at 0640 B/8 for Naples, were also warned at 1452 B/8 and took an alternative route. At 0535 B/9, the torpedo boat Centauro sailed from Naples to hunt the submarine and returned at 0638 B/10 without having sighted anything.

Late in the evening HMS P 42 carried out a successful bombardment of a southbound train between Fiumefreddo and Longobardi. According to Italian sources the locomotive and a wagon were confirmed hit, two were wounded.
0855 B/8 - While to the north of Paola, in position 39°25'N, 15°56'E, sighted masts and funnels of two small coasting vessels. Enemy course was 160°. These ships were out of range.
0930 B/8 - In position 39°25'N, 15°56'E, sighted a small coasting vessel with two masts and one funnel proceeding on course 160°. Considered this vessel too small for a torpedo attack.
1120 B/8 - In position 39°25'N, 15°56'E, sighted a modern type of trawler proceeding on course 170°.
1140 B/8, in position 39°23'N, 15°56'E, sighted a 2000-tons merchant vessel, with two masts and one funnel, proceeding on course 175°. Commenced attack.
1223 B/8 - Fired one torpedo. No result was obtained. P 42 took avoiding action, but no counter attack developed as it was at first thought that the trawler seen at 1120 B/8 might be an escort.
In the afternoon the coast was inspected for a suitable place for gun action against the railway.
1915 B/8 - In position 39°14'N, 16°02'E, sighted a small, two-masted, fishing vessel proceeding on course 350°.
2104 B/8 - Surfaced 3.5 miles from the shore and ran into the shore.

2210 B/8 - When some 1500 yards from the shore a train was observed approaching from the north. This train was watched carefully. As it passed on to the southward, the signal lights could be seen changing from red to white and green, and then back red again.

At 2225 B/8, a train passed from south to north. This was also watched. The night was clear without a moon but the objects under the shadow of the hills were difficult to discern. There was no blackout, only an economy in lighting. The sea was calm with a low swell and a slight offshore breeze.
The selected bridge was not easy to identify and may not have been seen at all. A point, thought to be the bridge, was chosen and shown to the gunlayer and guntrainer as being the focus point.
2238 B/8 - A southbound train was observed approaching. The gun was trained on and followed the headlights of the engine along the coast. By this time P 42 was about 800 to 900 from the shore.

2244 B/8 - The engine reached the focus point and fire was opened from 1000 yards. The first round set off an amazing display of electrical fireworks along the track some 50 yards in length. At first it seemed the train was blowing up, but later the flames could be seen to be electrical and very fierce. This display lasted a minute, by which time five rounds had been fire at the inferno. By now the engine had become detached and was still moving slowly to the right. The next minutes were devoted to it with at least one direct hit. After ten rounds had been expended there was nothing left to fire at except a section of burning train.
2248 B/8 - Withdrew from the coast.
2257 B/8 - Set course for Stomboli. (19)

10 Aug 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) is detected and depth charged near Cape Milazzo. P 42 cleared the area and took up another patrol position. A position which later turned out to be a good anticipation on behalf of Lt. Mars. The submarine was hunted by a CANT Z.501 aircraft and then by the auxiliary submarine chasers Quarnaro and Francipane and the motor torpedo boats MAS 547 and MAS 560. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori also participated in the hunt. She used her towing mine and dropped four 100-kg depth charges at 1245 B/10 and more of 50-kg and 100-kg types at 1435 B/10.
2100 B/9 - Surfaced in position 38°35'N, 15°17'E and proceeded towards the ordered patrol position 38°18'N, 15°13'E.
0030 B/10 - In position 333° - Cape Milazzo - 6 miles heard mushy HE from the direction of Cape Milazzo. The bearing remained constant. This got no closer and eventually faded.
0436 B/10 - Dived in position 025° - Cape Milazzo - 7 miles and closed the patrol position.
0900 B/10 - In position 320° - Cape Milazzo - 4 miles, a ship was sighted steering straight towards. P 42 went deep (80 feet) and speeded up to get off track. She appeared to be a naval tug.
0938 B/10 to 0953 B/10 - Five depth charges were dropped but none was close enough to cause damage.
0954 B/10 - Returned to periscope depth. The same ship was in sight bearing 059° going away.
1115 B/10 - When in position 000°, Cape Milazzo, 4 nautical miles, sighted smoke bearing 070°.
1145 B/10 - A Cant.Z.501 aircraft was seen to circle round the smoke which was now seen to be a small ship (one funnel, one mast) zigzagging.
1211 B/10 - The vessel was seen to be a naval craft (similar to Crotone) and closing on a steady bearing, although still zigzagging. The aircraft continued to circle.
1222 B/10 - Went to 80 feet.

1225 B/10 - Depth charging commenced. Went to 120 feet. Attempted to keep stern on. After a pattern of depth charges was dropped burst of speed were used to get clear. Later a second vessel joined in and there may have even been more vessels present.
1448 B/10 - Depth charging ceased. A total of 50 having been dropped in this second attack.
1500 B/10 - Remained at 120 feet while steering 030°.
1821 B/10 - Depth charging recommenced (our position was now 353°, Cape Milazzo, 9.7 nautical miles) and continued until 1914 hours. 15 Depth charges were dropped in this third attack.
2000 B/10 - Altered course to 050°.

2228 B/10 - Surfaced in position 007°, Cape Milazzo, 17 nautical miles. Nothing in sight. In all 70 depth charges had been dropped, many near enough to shake the boat, but none so close that any cork fell down. As far as is known no damage had been done to P 42. (19)

11 Aug 1942 From yesterday's experiences, it was evident that the patrol position of HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) had been compromised and that it would be useless and hazardous to remain on patrol in that area. Lt. Mars therefore decided to move patrol to position 255° - Stromboli peak - 12.5 miles (38°44'N, 14°48'E) [the patrol report gives position 38°44'N, 15°48'E but this is a mistake, probably just a typo].
0518 B/11 - Dived in position 324° - Stromboli - 15 miles and set course for the selected new patrol position.
1230 B/11 - Reached the new patrol position and patrolled within two miles from it. (19)

13 Aug 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian heavy cruiser Bolzano and the Italian light cruiser Muzio Attendolo in the Ionian Sea off the north coast of Sicily in position 38°43'N, 14°57'E.

They were in company with the heavy cruisers Trieste and Gorizia (the latter was just moving away to launch a seaplane) and were escorted by the destroyers Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Fuciliere, Geniere, Grecale and Legionario.

Bolzano was hit in an oil tank and her magazines had to be flooded (nine killed and twenty wounded). She was beached at Panarea Island and refloated about a month later. Attendolo had a large part of her bow wrecked.

To intercept the Pedestal convoy the Italians sent the following units to sea; The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli (Seventh Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Maestrale, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Cagliari at 1900 B/11-2030 B/11.
The light cruiser Muzio Attendolo, escorted by the torpedo boats Circe and Generale Achille Papa sailed from Naples at 0930 B/12-0950 B/12.

The heavy cruisers Bolzano (Third Cruiser Division, flying the flag of Admiral Angelo Parona, former head of Betasom) and Gorizia had sailed from Messina at 0740 B/12-0940 B/12, escorted by the destroyers Geniere, Ascari, Camicia Nera, Aviere, Legionario, Grecale and Corsaro.
The heavy cruiser Trieste, escorted by the destroyer Fuciliere and the torpedo boat Ardito sailed from La Spezia at 0049 B/12.

At 1418 B/12, the Seventh Cruiser Division sighted Muzio Attendolo escorted by the torpedo boats Circe and Generale Achille Papa and joined them. At 1427 B/12, the two torpedo boats were detached, the first to reinforce the screen of a convoy (tanker Nautilus (Italian 2071 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the torpedo boats Enrico Cosenz and Nicola Fabrizi proceeding from Naples to Messina, where they arrived at 2029 B/13-2036 B/13) while the second proceeded to Naples, where she arrived at 1847 B/12.

At 1844 B/12, Eugenio di Savoia sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and merged with them, the Seventh Cruiser Division taking the lead (Gorizia took command of the group). Trieste, Fuciliere and Ardito were sighted shortly after.
At 1920 B/12, the destroyer Grecale joined the close escort of the Seventh Cruiser Division.
At 1925 B/12, the torpedo boat Ardito was given freedom of action and returned to La Spezia, arriving at 1400 B/13.

At 0048 B/13, Muzio Attendolo and Grecale left the formation and joined the Third Cruiser Division. At 0140 B/13, Vincenzo Gioberti signalled torpedo tracks. At 0240 B/13, the destroyers Maestrale and Vincenzo Gioberti assumed close escort to the Seventh Cruiser Division. Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli, screened by Maestrale, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani arrived in Naples at 1223 B/13-1313 B/13.

The cruisers Gorizia, Bolzano, Trieste and Muzio Attendolo, screened by the destroyers Camicia Nera, Ascari, Geniere, Aviere, Legionario, Grecale, Fuciliere and Corsaro, were returning to Messina at 22 knots, when at 0807 B/13, they were attacked by HMS P 42. Gorizia had just turned to port to launch a seaplane when Bolzano was hit by a torpedo (nine killed, twenty wounded), followed a few seconds later by Muzio Attendolo (three missing, two wounded) which lost her bow. After the torpedoing, Trieste and Gorizia, screened by the destroyer Camicia Nera, arrived in Messina at 1200 B/13-1320 B/13. The destroyers Aviere, Ascari and Geniere joined Muzio Attendolo to assist her. Aviere dropped depth charges and covered the cruiser with smoke. At 1000 B/13, the destroyer Ascari took Muzio Attendolo in tow, but the cable broke almost immediately. However, the light cruiser managed to proceed on her own power. At 1045 B/13-1159 B/13, the destroyer Freccia and the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Messina and joined them at 1340 B/13, followed at 1715 B/13 by the destroyers Legionario and Corsaro. Muzio Attendolo, screened by Aviere, Geniere, Freccia and Giuseppe Sirtori, arrived in Messina at 1745-2000 B/13. Corsaro returned to assist Bolzano, Legionario, and Ascari proceeded to Reggio Calabria, arriving at 1958-2103 B/13.

Bolzano was beached on the island of Panarea at 1200 B/13. The destroyer Fuciliere stood by her, before returning to Messina at 1145 B/14. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba had sailed from Messina at 2017 B/12 to do an A/S search area in the Catania area, then between Capo dell'Armi and Cape Spartivento. The following day, she was ordered to assist the stricken ships. She crossed Muzio Attendolo screened by three destroyers and went on to assist Bolzano. Admiral Parona took passage on her, and they arrived at Messina at 0044 B/14. Corsaro had also joined the protection of Bolzano after escorting Muzio Attendolo and returned to Messina at 1830 B/14. The destroyer Camicia Nera sailed from Messina at 1725 B/13, and the torpedo boats Centauro and Climene sailed from Trapani at 1155 B/13 to join Bolzano. Bolzano would remain at Panarea until 1615 B/15 September, when she was towed by tugs to Naples where she arrived at 2020 B/16.

0330 B/13 - Received the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 0320 B/13 to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) which contained the following; Enemy cruisers coming your way.
0442 B/13 - Reveived the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0411 B/13 to HMS P 42 which contained the following; Enemy cruiser off Cape Gallo at 0300 B/13 course east, speed 20 knots.
0730 B/13 - When in position 38°43'N, 14°57'E HE was heard bearing 230°.

0743 B/13 - Sighted a large number of ships bearing 230° steering straight towards. The centre column consisted of four large ships, two 8" cruisers and possibly two 6" cruisers. The escort consisted of 8 modern destroyers. Started attack.

0804 B/13 - Fired four torpedoes from yards at the nearest 8" cruiser. Two 6" cruisers were beyond the target and if torpedoes missed there was a good possibility of hitting the other ships beyond. P 42 went deep on firing and altered course 90° to starboard and increased speed for 5 minutes. It was thought two hits were obtained on the nearest 8" cruiser, and with luck the 'overs' may have hit one of the other cruisers.
0809 B/13 - Intensive depth charging started, went to 120 feet and crept away.

0900 B/13 - The 40th charge was dropped. By this time the enemy seemed to have lost contact, and to be drawing astern. However, depth charging continued for 8 hours and 31 minutes, the explosions becoming more distant and less frequent as time drew on.
1640 B/13 - The last depth charge was dropped at a considerable distance. The day's total came to 105. Only some superficial damage was sustained.
1900 B/13 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
2059 B/13 - Surfaced and charged 29 miles bearing 335° from the torpedo attack.

2130 B/13 - Received the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1227 B/13 and 1723 B/13 which contained the following orders for P 42; Return to Malta, pass position 38°25'N, 11°59'E, noon/15 then by reverse of the outward route to surface at the end of the searched channel at 1001 B/18 to make rendezvous with the minesweeper.

2320 B/13 - Passed our signal timed 0914 B/13 to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; At 0806 B/13th two 8-inch and possibly two smaller cruisers and eight destroyers in position 38°43'N, 14°57'E, course 070°, speed 25 knots. Two unseen hits claimed on 8-inch cruiser. (19)

14 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1100 B/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN); Your 0914 B/13. Reconnaissance confirms your nice work of hitting two cruisers. Anticipate HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) approximate position 2101 B/15 as 38°03'N, 11°39'E. (23)

15 Aug 1942 At 1717 B/15, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1625 B/15 which contained the following; One merchant ship and one destroyer in position 38°43'N, 12°56'E, course 205°, speed 15 knots at 1615 B/15. Aircraft may attack before 0200 B/16.

The convoy referred to was made up of the Italian transport Rosolino Pilo (8326 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Maestrale and Vincenzo Gioberti which sailed from Naples for Tripoli at 0615 B/15-0700 B/15. When it was learned that an important enemy convoy had sailed from Gibraltar (operation PEDESTAL), they were ordered to Trapani where they arrived at 0414 B/16-0605 B/16. They finally sailed from Trapani at 0530 B/17-0635 B/17. They were attacked by four Beaufort torpedo bombers from 86 Squadron (RAF), Rosolino Pilo was hit by a torpedo at 1550 B/17 in position 35°55'N, 11°49'E; Vincenzo Gioberti was machine-gunned and had about 50 wounded, including her Captain. She turned back and arrived at Trapani at 2200 B/17. Two MAS boats sailed from Pantelleria to assist the freighter. Maestrale was ordered to turn back and arrived at Trapani at 0316 B/18. The tug Montecristo had been ordered to take her in tow. Rosolino Pilo was finished off by a torpedo from HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) at 0009 B/18. There were no casualties. She carried 4000 tons of stores for the Germans with a civilian crew of 50 and a military crew of 32 Italians and 39 Germans, and 110 German passengers. She had already been abandoned by her passengers and crew before the submarine attack, except her captain and four crew. Members who had remained on board and were evacuated shortly before she sank. Montecristo picked up the survivors.
At 2110 B/15, HMS P 42 commenced the eastbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 [mined area].

At 2350 B/15, in position 241° - Marsala - 12.5 miles a destroyer was sighted. P 42 dived to avoid detection. HE indicated one destroyer and one merchant vessel steering approximately 180°. Having no torpedoes left, P 42 remained dived until all HE had faded.
Most probably this were ships from the Rosolino Pilo-convoy. (23)
16 Aug 1942 At 0104 B/16, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) surfaced.
At 0445 B/16, the eastbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) is completed. (19)

18 Aug 1942 At 1001 B/18, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) surfaced at the end of the searched channel and made rendezvous with HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which then swept the submarine into harbour where she ended her 5th war patrol (also 5th in the Mediterranean). (19)
31 Aug 1942 At 1730 B/31, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (also 6th in the Mediterranean).

She was ordered to patrol in area 'X', 36°40'N, 15°09'E - 320° - 62 - 180. She was also ordered to destroy a railway bridge to the north of Taormina, Sicily and to conduct a special operation off Crotone, Calabria, Italy.
P 42 was to proceed through position 35°22'N, 14°12'E at 2230 B/31.
She was also to proceed through position 37°11'N, 15°54'E.
HMS P 42 was sewpt out until 1930 B/31, by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (24)
31 Aug 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 42 during her 6th war patrol see the map below.
2 Sep 1942 At 1740 B/2, in position 36°55'N, 15°58'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) sighted an Italian hospital ship thought to be the Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) proceeding on course 130°.
At 2200 B/2, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) entered her patrol area. (19)

3 Sep 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) arrived off the Railway bridge she was to destroy by firing a torpedo at its base. This was however impossible as the river was dry and the bridge was 200 feet inland from the shore. Also if the river had not been dry the torpedo most likely would have been deflected by the current.
0254 B/3 - Surfaced and closed Taormina.

0341 B/3 - Received the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 0305 B/3 which contained the following; N.A.S. claim two hits on merchant ship in position 45° - Cape Spartivento - 15 miles. Ship almost stopped at 2345 B/2. Lt. Mars decided to go ahead with the mission to destroy the railway bridge as there would be ample time to attack the damaged ship in the afternoon in case it would make for Messina.
0457 B/3 - Dived.

1105 B/3 to 1330 B/3 - Reconnoitred the coast between Cape San Andrea and Botteghelle. The only possible objective was cleared seen to be the bridge over the river d'Argo which is a long metal trestle bridge. This bridge was closes to within 15 cables. On careful examination, it was seen that the bridge had low concrete piles under the metalwork. However these were set a good 200 yards back from the line of the beach and the river they spanned was quite dry. But even if the river had not been dry there is little chance that a torpedo would het the bridge as it would almost certainly be deflected by the current.
Proceeded to the eastward at 2.5 knots to see if the damaged ship reported could be seen near Cape dell'Armi.

1710 B/3 - The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1341 B/3 was received which contained the following; The Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 0305/3. Ship observed aground in position 38°06'N, 16°09'E with three destroyers in vicinity A.M./3. Take no action until completion of 'project A' [the special operation against Crotone harbour].

The ships aground was the Italian transport Monti (4301 GRT, built 1942) which had sailed from Messina at 1922 B/2-1930 B/2 for Piraeus escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Circe, Giuseppe Sirtori and Enrico Cosenz. At 2300 B/2, Enrico Cosenz left them and returned to Messina, arriving at 1830 B/3. At 2349 B/2, the convoy was attacked by torpedo bombers, and Monti was hit astern on the port side (thirteen wounded). At 0020 B/3, Giuseppe Sirtori attempted to take in tow Monti but the cable fouled the torpedo boat’s propeller. In the meantime, Circe picked up four survivors who had been thrown overboard by the explosion. At about 0100 B/3, Giuseppe Sirtori had finally succeeded in passing the tow cable and they proceeded to Messina. At 0130 B/3, Circe had obtained a sonar contact at 2500 metres. She remained behind to hunt the submarine and dropped depth charges. The destroyer Giovanni da Verazzano had sailed from Patras at 1400 B/2 for Messina and was instructed to go to the assistance of Monti. At 0420 B/3, she was sighted by Circe which was just being informed by Giuseppe Sirtori that Monti had run aground, without specifying the position. Circe informed Giovanni da Verazzano that this was probably north of Cape Spartivento. The torpedo remained in the area to look for the submarine until dawn and then rejoined Monti at 0600 B/3. The merchant ship was now protected by Giuseppe Sirtori, and they were soon reinforced by Giovanni da Verazzano and Enrico Cosenz. At 0735 B/3, Giovanni da Verazzano ordered Circe to escort a southbound German merchant vessel (could not find her identity). The torpedo boat reached her at 0759 B/3 but at 0836 B/3, she was relieved by the auxiliary Morosini, and Circe returned to her protective duty. At about 0935 B/3, the tugs Impero, Littorio and Nereo had arrived, but stores had to be removed from Monti so she could be towed away, and the tugs were sent home. Eventually, Circe left and arrived in Messina at 0605 B/5.
2028 B/3 - Surfaced and proceeded to a position 20 miles to the south of Cape Spartivento and then proceeded to the north-east to carry out 'project A' keeping about 20 miles from the coast. (25)
4 Sep 1942 At 0532 B/4, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) dived and commenced to patrol at periscope depth.

At 1152 B/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1043 B/4 was received which contained the following; Reference my 1015 B/4, one motor vessel and five destroyers in position 38°15'N, 16°20'E, course 000°, speed 10 at 0930 B/4.

This signal referred to the motorship Luciano Manara (8396 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Freccia, Giovanni da Verazzano, Corsaro and the torpedo boats Enrico Cosenz and Giuseppe Sirtori which sailed from Messina at 0345 B/4-0515 B/4 and arrived at Taranto at 2230 B/4-2344 B/4.
This signal referred on earlier signal timed 1015 B/4 which had not been received. Course was altered to close the coast.

At 1306 B/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1015 B/4, was received which contained the following orders; Make every endeavour to be in the vicinity of position 39°08'N, 17°22’E from 1601 B/4 until 2001 B/14. This takes priority over 'project A' about which further instructions later.

It was not possible to reach this position by 1601 B/4, not even by running on the surface. It was reluctantly concluded that an interception was not possible. Weather conditions were most favourable for aircraft and surface running could only have been accepted for a target of the very utmost value.
At 1330 B/4, HMS Unbroken resumed course to the north-eastward, with the intention of surfacing early and proceeding to the position ordered at full speed.
At 1710 B/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1613 B/4 was received which contained the following; My 1015/4. Aircraft may attack south-east of the position given after 2001 B/4.

From this signal it seemed evident that the convoy was expected to move to the south-east. Lt. Mars therefore decided to proceed to position 38°50'N, 17°35'E hoping to finish off any ship damaged by the aircraft attack.
0030 B/5, HMS P 42 arrived in position 38°50'N, 17°35'E.
Between 0105 B/5 and 0206 B/5, distant aircraft flares were seen to the north-east and to the north.

At 0225 B/5, an aircraft flare was dropped very close. P 42 remained on the surface hoping the aircraft was A.S.V. contact with the enemy. It was later considered that an aircraft report timed 0220 B/5, which stated 'am over enemy destroyer in position 39°05'N, 17°50'E, course 220°, speed 10 knots', may have referred to HMS P 42.
At 0300 B/5, HMS P 42 set course to the westward to close Crotone. (25)

4 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 2045 B/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information concering the special operation to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN); Reference plan on chart 198 and photograph supplied following reference position establish point 'A' red light at extremity outer arm point 'B' green light on harbour end of inner Mole. Four ships now in harbour. Two alongside each mole, position bow which all face seaward are; 1) 1800 feet point 'A', 153°, size 1000 tons. 2) 2100 feet point 'A', 154°, size 500 tons. 3) 550 feet point 'B', 194°, size 1200 tons. 4) 30 feet point 'B', 175°, size 2500 tons. Target '4' just arrived, probably for loading. This is a good target. It is 310 feet long.
Anti-torpedo boom supports on canisters about 3 feet apart stretch from 'B' to position 400 feet, 148° from 'A'.

This boom is in four equal sized sections, the central section bearest out arm forming the gate. Photograph enlargement show partially obstructed 50 foot gap in outer beakwater 420 feet 148° from 'A' and from which Folbot could pass trough it arriving inside boom if calm, if choppy passage of outer boom consider safer less [part of the signal missing] to meet jagged obstuctions. (26)

5 Sep 1942 During the night of 5/6 September 1942, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) landed a raiding party (Captain R. Wilson, D.S.O., R.A. and Bombardier Brittlebank) to use miniature torpedoes on shipping inside Crotone harbour. At 2340/5 the Folbot with the party was launched. At 0050 B/6 two flashes were observed inside the harbour. One miniature torpedo (actually a limpet with an electric motor) was released but the two men could not make their getaway in time and were captured. The experiment with miniature torpedo was not repeated. Soon afterwards a fast motorboat was heard approaching forcing P 42 to dive. The vessel was seen to be an 'E-boat' which then commenced a hunt. The enemy obtained contact but no depth charges were dropped. P 42 was therefore not on the surface to pick up the raiding party and they were not seen again. [Both members of the party were taken as PoWs.]
0500 B/5 - The day was spent off Crotone. A few fishing schooners were seen as were some bomber aircraft. There appears to be an aerodrome to the south of Crotone.
Some fishing schooners were seen near Cape Colonne during the afternoon.
In a signal timed 1537 B/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 42; Air reconnaissance at noon today shows no change from the information contained as in my signal timed 2045 B/4.

1845 B/5 - A ship of about 1200 to 1500 tons was seen leaving Crotone for the northward. She was escorted by a craft similar to a small motor torpedo boat. The ship was heavily laden and passed about 10000 yards from HMS P 42. This departure did not affect the special operation as the larges ship of 2500 tons could still be seen inside the harbour.
2100 B/5 - Surfaced 7.5 miles to the east of the Crotone breakwater lighthouse. Charged and ventilated.
2245 B/5 - Broke the charge, got the Folbot up and approached the breakwater trimmed down.

2330 B/5 - In position 090° - Crotone Breakwater Light - 2500 yards. Stopped bows on to the shore. The end of the breakwater could be seen through glasses. The night was at this time fairly clear with an offshore breeze force 2.

2340 B/5 - Launched the Folbot containing Capt. Wilson and Bombardier Brittlebank and their equipment. Capt. Wilson agreed the conditions for the operation were excellent. He expected to return in one hour and twenty minutes.

2342 B/5 - Withdrew astern to 3500 yards approximately and then turned bows to seaward taking up a final position 090° - End of the breakwater - 3000 yards. Before this manoeuvre was complete, smoke from the chemical (nitrate) factory pervaded the atmosphere cutting down visibility of objects to some 2000 yards or less. The outline of the hills could just be seen against the sky. These conditions prevailed until the submarine dived (see below).

0055 B/6 - Two flashes were observed inside the harbour, which might have been the explosions we were waiting for. The interval between the explosions was about 7 seconds. All waited for the sounds of the explosions but none were heard.
0118 B/6 - A fast motor bot was heard from the bridge and one minute later fast HE was picked up to starboard assessed as 320 revs.
Shortly afterwards a vessel was seen to be closing rapidly from the starboard quarter. This was thought to be a motor torpedo boat.
0120 B/6 - Dived to 60 feet. Set course to 110°.
0125 B/6 - Altered course to 085°.
0130 B/6 - The enemy started to hunt. It is throught two vessels were employed both equipped with Asdic.
0150 B/6 - The enemy was in contact but a turn stern on to 130° seem to made them to have lost contact.

0300 B/6 - All was quiet now. As hunting craft appeared to be working on a line 090° from the harbour it was decided to keep to southward for a while. It had been agreed with Capt. Wilson that should he make the dawn rendezvous in 090° - harbour entrance - 5 miles and not see the submarine he would steer 090° at slow speed.

0730 B/6 - P 42 was now in position 090° - harbour entrance - 10 miles steering 270°. In view of the good visibility it was decided not to surface, but a careful search was carried out with the high power periscope constantly in use.
0820 B/6 - Two naval auxiliary schooners were seen coming towards.

0830 B/5 - In position 090° - harbour entrance - 7.5 miles. Altered course to withdraw to the south-east. the schooners dropped a series of small charges about 5 at a time, but gave this up after about an hour. It was considered that Capt. Wilson still been free he would have, by this time, made the position 2.5 miles east of the rendezvous. The Commanding Officer is of the opinion that Capt. Wilson and his companion must have been detected about 0050 B/5 and that they most probably had been unable to leave the harbour.
2000 B/6 - Surfaced and proceeded to seaward.
2100 B/6 - Passed the signal timed 1832 B/6 which contained the events of the special operation. Course was altered to close the beached ship reported in position 38°06'N, 16°09'E.

2357 B/6 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2323 B/6 which stated that the grounded ship had already been sufficiently damaged. In this signal it was suggested to bombard the western siding of the railway track near the mouth of the Amendolea river.
Course was set to comply with this suggestion. (19)
7 Sep 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) bombarded a railway bridge across the mouth of the Amendolea River. 0550 B/7 - Dived and approached the Amendolea river mouth so as to arrive at sunset.
1315 B/7 to 1400 B/7 - Several distant explosions were heard.
1606 B/7 - In position 250° (?) - Cape Spartivento - 2 miles a trawler was seen proceeding on course 090°.
1857 B/7 - During the final approach to the mouth of the river Amandolea a torpedo boat was seen approaching. The enemy was proceeding along the coast from east to west.
P 42 altered course to evade and went deep thus delaying the operation.
This was perhaps the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz which sailed from Crotone at 1155 B/7, carried out an A/S sweep and arrived at Messina at 1023 B/8.

Later it proved impossible to see any railway tracks on the siding on the west bank of the river. Accordingly it was decided to bombard the bridge which makes a good target and had a road bridge just beyond.
2004 B/7 - Surfaced 2000 yards from the bridge.
2006 B/7 - Opened fire on the bridge.

2012 B/7 - Ceased fire after having fired 37 rounds of high explosive. Withdrew to the southward at full speed. Between 10 and 14 hits had been seen on the bridge. There was no opposition from the enemy. During the shoot, two smoke candles were fired to form a cloud from which behind the submarine could retire and also to mislead the enemy. These smoke candles were still burning 20 minutes later. While retiring from the coast two dummy bifocal periscopes were dropped in positions 37°52'N, 15°53'E and 37°40'N, 15°49'E.
2145 B/7 - Altered course to the eastward and set course for the Gulf of Squillace for another railway bombardment near Staletti Point. (19)

8 Sep 1942 In the evening, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) tried to bombard a railway viaduct near Starletti Point, Calabria, Italy. They tried to wait for a passing train but none showed up. While waiting P 42 had been set to the north and this had not been noticed. So when they tried to take the viaduct under fire they were out of position. Fire was then opened on a road but without success. [Italian sources reported no damage to the viaduct.]
0542 B/8 - Dived 20 miles to the south east of Punta Stilo and closed Staletti Point.

During the afternoon a careful study of the coast and railway was made through the periscope. It was decided to bombard a train on the open stretch of line between the tunnel of Staletti Point and another tunnel half a mile to the southward. This line is fairly low down, and about half its length runs over culverts and viaducts. It was hoped to knock a train off one of these. However, only one train had been observed during the afternoon. This had been a 35 coach goods train drawn by a steam engine.
At 1545 B/8, in position 38°42'N, 16°33'E, a motor fishing boat was seen proceeding southwards and entering Soverato harbour.
2017 B/8 - Surfaced and closed the coast to within a mile and waited for a train to approach. The night was very dark and still. The sounding machine was found to be defective.

2245 B/8 - By this time several cars had been observed passing along the road above the railway, but there were no trains. Lt. Mars decided that a longer wait was too risky, so an attempt to bombard the railway viaduct was made.

2254 B/8 - Opened fire with starshell. This revealed that P 42 had been offset to the north. She was now opposite the railway tunnel instead of the open line. The flares also illuminated a high single arched road culvert. For want of a railway target, fire was concentrated on this culvert but without success.
2259 B/8 - Ceased fire after eight rounds of starshell and fourteen rounds of high explosive had been fired. Withdrew from the coast at full speed.
Lt. Mars decided to patrol along the coast between Cape Colonne and Crotone the following day. Course was set accordingly. (19)

9 Sep 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) attacked an enemy convoy that had just left the harbour of Crotone. Two torpedoes were fired against a merchant vessel but no hits were obtained. These were Leonardo Palomba (1110 GRT, built 1899) and Loreto (1055 GRT, built 1912) on passage from Crotone to Messsina, escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori. The torpedo boat attempted to hunt the submarine but without success.
This convoy sailed from Crotone at 1810 B/9 and arrived at Messina at 1910 B/10.
0530 B/9 - Dived about 24 miles to the south of Cape Colonne.

1320 B/9 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1041 B/9 which contained the following orders for HMS P 42; Leave patrol on the 10th to make rendezvous with minesweepers at the end of the searched channel at 1001 B/13. Proceed through the following positions on passage, 37°11'N, 15°54'E, 35°22'N, 15°12'E, 35°22'N, 14°12'E and 35°32'N, 14°12'E.
1430 B/9 - Sighted an A/S patrol vessel passing southbound. She was searching with Asdic.
1700 B/9 - at least three small merchant vessels were seen inside Crotone harbour. As one had been seen leaving for the north on 5 September, it was decided to take up an intercepting position.
1822 B/9 - A ship was seen leaving the harbour seemingly for the north but the ship soon turned to the south and was seen to be escorted.

1840 B/9 - A second ship was seen leaving the harbour. This ship joined the first ship and it's escort. Both ships were of about 2000 tons and were laden. They were escorted by an older type torpedo-boat.
1922 B/9 - Fired two torpedoes (old Mark II type, due to torpedo shortage at Malta) from 4500 yards at the second ship. No hits were obtained.
1950 B/9 - The torpedo-boat commenced a hunt. Four depth charges were dropped at considerable intervals.
2115 B/9 - All was quiet now.
2147 B/9 - Surfaced and retired from the coast.

2238 B/9 - Passed an enemy report timed 1929 B/9 to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; One destroyer and a 2200 tons merchant ship in position 39°01'N, 17°14'E, course 180°, speed 6 knots. Probably coasting to the southwest.
The next day HMS P 42 left patrol in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1041 B/9. (19)
13 Sep 1942 Around 1230 B/13, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (also 6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0930 B/13. (19)

25 Sep 1942 At 1445 B/25, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (also 7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol near Misurata, Libya south of 33°00'N and between 14°20'E / 16°35'E.
She, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) were escorted out by until around 1700 B/25 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). (27)
25 Sep 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 42 during her 7th war patrol see the map below.

27 Sep 1942 At 0700 B/27, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) entered her patrol area in Longitude 15°31'E. Lt. Mars decided to make direct for a position about 15 miles 350° from Sirte and work to the north-west from there. (28)
29 Sep 1942 At 0610 B/29, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) dived 11 miles to the east of Ras Ramla and patrolled all day between 6 and 8 miles of Ras Khara.
At 1840 B/29, in approximate position 32°13'N, 15°29'E smoke was sighted bearing 300°. P 42 closed to investigate.

At 1852 B/29, a convoy of three ships could be made out in the poor light with a cloudy background. Obtaining a range was impossible, except for a rough estimate by eye. Enemy course was estimated as 130°, speed 6 knots.

This were most likely the Italian transports Anna Maria (1205 GRT, built 1912) and Amba Alagi (403 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the submarine chaser Selve which sailed from Tripoli at 1800 B/26 and arrived at Benghazi at 1230 B/1.
At 1930 B/29, the attack was broken off as the targets could no longer be seen. Nothing was heard on Asdic.

At 1945 B/29, HMS P 42 surfaced about 2 to 3 miles on the port quarter of the convoy. Due to a problem with the port propeller the speed was reduced to 8 knots. Lt. Mars decided to overtake the convoy to seaward and attack around midnight. The convoy consited of two ships of about 1500 tons and one smaller vessel, possibly the escort.
At 2100 B/29, HMS P 42 lost touch with the convoy in the bad visibility and a probable alteration of course of the convoy towards the shore.
At 2300 B/29, HMS P 42 altered course to 220° to close and carry out an attack. By this time the moon was fairly well up and the sky was still cloudy. Visibility was now about 3 to 4 miles.

At 0015 B/30, still no sign of the convoy. It seemed probable that convoy convoy was inshore of, and to the north-west of P 42 and might have anchored for the night. Set course for a patrol position to the west of Sirte where an interception could be made by day. Nothing was however sighted though. (28)
1 Oct 1942 At 0700 B/1, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) examined the Buerat anchorage from about 6 to 8 miles. No ships were seen and all hope of intercepting the convoy was now given up.
At 0820 B/1, in position 31°30'N, 15°52'E, sighted two small vessels bearing 100° at a range of about 6 miles. Commenced attack.

At 0840 B/1, identified one of the vessels as a small schooner of about 40 tons and the other as a 500 tons trawler. The trawler had a square stern and was painted grey. Seemed likely this was an auxiliary warship. Decided them too small for a torpedo and gun action was considered inadvisable. A gun could be seen on the forecastle of the schooner.

These were most probably the refrigeration vessel Ascianghi (403 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the auxiliary submarine chaser (schooner) Guiseppe Garibaldi (47 GRT, built 1939). They had sailed from Benghazi at 1700 B/29 and arrived at Tripoli at 0900 B/2.
At 1100 B/1, the schooner and trawler were out of sight. Their course and speed had been 330° at about 5 to 6 knots. (28)

2 Oct 1942 At 0610 B/2, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) dived to the north of Zreg (which is located in approximate position 32°26'N, 14°54'E), and patrolled to the west keeping about 4 miles from the shore.

At 1925 B/2, surfaced 5 miles from Zliten (which is located in approximate position 32°30'N, 14°35'E) and left patrol to return to Malta to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1037 B/1 which had contained the following orders for her; Leave patrols as necessary to make rendezvous at 1100 B/4. Proceed through position 35°01'N, 14°11'E on the way to Malta. (29)

4 Oct 1942 Around 1315 B/4, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) ended their 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) and 7th war patrol (also 7th in the Mediterranean) respectively at Malta.
They were escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1040 B/2. (30)

11 Oct 1942 At 1400 A/11, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (also 8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the east of Tripoli to the south of 33°00'N, and between 14°00'E / 15°50'E.
She was escorted out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) until 1625 B/11. (27)
11 Oct 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 42 during her 8th war patrol see the map below.

12 Oct 1942 At 2025 B/12, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1137 A/12 which contained the following orders; Patrol outside 100 fathom line in vicinity 15°00'E until further orders. (31)
13 Oct 1942 At 0300 A/13, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) entered her patrol area.
At 0518 A/13, HMS P 42 dived and closed position 32°34'N, 14°54'E.
From 1100 A/13, HMS P 42 patrolled within 5 miles of the ordered patrol position.
At 2353 A/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2314 A/13 was received which contained orders to patrol near position 32°50'N, 14°10'E. (31)
14 Oct 1942 At 0020 A/14, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) set course to proceed to the new patrol position as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2314 A/13.
At 0700 A/14, HMS P 42 commenced to patrol within 5 miles of the ordered patrol position (32°50'N, 14°10'E).
At 1140 A/14, HMS P 42 sighted very distant smoke to the north-west. This soon vanished and nothing further was seen.
At 1435 A/14, the smoke and topmast of a ship were sighted on bearing 340°. Closed at speed in an attempt to attack.
At 1506 A/14, the enemy vessel was in position 32°58'N, 14°18'E.

At 1515 A/14, after having run for 4 miles it was seen that the enemy ship was drawing away with the minimum range being about 11000 miles. Course of the enemy was 090° at 8 knots. The escorts topmast and smoke could be seen. There were two escorting aircraft present. The ship was thought to be of about 4000 tons but only the masts and funnel could be seen.

The vessels sighted were most probably the German steamer Trapani (1855 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Giacomo Medici. They had sailed from Tripoli at 0620 B/14-0700 B/14 and arrived at Benghazi at 1600 B/16-1610 B/16. They had come under air attack by three bombers at 1237 B/14 in position 200° - Ras Hallab - 6 miles. Two were shot down by the combined antiaircraft fire of Trapani and Giacomo Medici, the torpedo boat picked up two airmen from the second bomber. The third was shot down by German fighters covering the convoy.
Between 1624 A/14 and 1747 A/14, 37 distant explosions were heard in groups of 3 to 10. This may have been depth charging very far off.

At 1851 A/14, HMS P 42 passed an enemy report timed 1506 A/13 which contained the following; One 4000-ton merchant ship, one escort vessel, two aircraft in position 32°58'N, 14°18'E, course 090°, speed 8 knots.
At 2240 A/14, sighted distant flares bearing 095°. (31)

14 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1815 A/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN); Aircraft report one merchant ship and two destroyers in position 33°05'N, 16°35'E. Course 230° at 10 knots at 1515 A/14.
In a signal timed 2326 A/14, the following orders were sent by the Capt. S.10 to HMS P 42 and HMS P 44; On completion of attack or at noon/15, P 42 operating in area 'N', P 44 in area 'M'.

The reported westbound ship and two escorting destroyers referred to the following convoy; At 0531 B/5-0600 B/5 October, the Italian transport Amsterdam (8673 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Perseo and the patrol boats A.S. 211, A.S. 206 and A.S. 226 sailed from Naples and arrived at Messina at 0212 B/6. They sailed from Messina at 0415 B/7-0430 B/7, reinforced by the torpedo boat Circe (which took over from Perseo as the convoy leader). The three patrol boats were to scout ahead on an A/S search as far as Crotone until 1830 B/7. At 1117 B/8, an escorting Cant Z.501 dropped two bombs on a presumed submarine. Two minutes later, a German Junker 88 did the same. The freighter, averaging a speed of 9.5 knots, and the two torpedo boats arrived in Patras at 1906 B/8-1920 B/8. Initially, the convoy was to sail from Patras on 9 October with the torpedo boat Lince substituting for Perseo, they only sailed at 1615 B/12-1658 B/12. [The reason for the delay is not known to us]. At 0705 B/13, they were joined by the destroyer Bombardiere, which assumed command until 0804 B/14 when she left the convoy and returned to Navarino. Circe resumed duty as convoy leader. At 1715 B/14, Lince detected a submarine and attacked it with depth charges and searched the area before rejoining the convoy at 2045 B/14. At 2255 B/14, in position 034° - Misurata – 50 miles, a flare was observed, and a minute later an enemy bomber dropped a torpedo which passed between Amsterdam and Circe. At 0350 B/15, a bomber dropped two bombs near Amsterdam. At 0410 B/15, Amsterdam was hit by a torpedo from an enemy bomber in position 33°10'N, 15°02'E. She was abandoned by most of her crew, and Lince recovered the survivors. However, Amsterdam was still afloat, and Circe signalled Marina Tripoli to send a tug. In the meantime, Circe had managed to take her in tow and proceeded toward Homs. The tug Pronta (182 GRT, built 1913) escorted by the minesweeper RD 39 sailed from Tripoli at 0700 B/15 to their assistance and, at 1246 B/15, in 33°12'05"N, 15°01'E, the escort was reinforced by the arrival of the torpedo boat Calliope from Tobruk. Calliope was short of fuel and had to leave them at 1900 B/15 to refuel at Tripoli (0720 B/16-1310 B/16). She returned the following day at 1615 B. At 2120 B/15, Pronta had arrived and taken over the towing. In the meantime, Marina Tripoli had informed them that the trawler Scorfano (308 GRT, built 1924), the tug Porto Palo and the motor fishing vessel Sparviero (498 GRT, built 1915) were also dispatched to assist them, and they reached them at 1025 B/16. During the night of 15/16, Lince left them and arrived at Tripoli at 1125 B/16. At 1136 B/16, Porto Palo took Amsterdam in tow, and a few hours later Scorfano also helped with the towing. The tug Audax (115 GRT, built 1930) escorted by the minesweeper RD 31 had also sailed from Tripoli at 0930 B/16 and joined them at 1715 B/16, except RD 31 which had to turn back due to the rough seas. At 1828 B/16, Amsterdam was beached 600 metres from the Homs pier. Circe arrived in Tripoli at 0820 B/17 and Calliope at 0914 B/18. (32)

15 Oct 1942 At 0138 A/15, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) sighted several distant flares bearing 070°. She remained within five miles of the ordered patrol position (32°50'N, 14°10'E) for interception of a reported escorted westbound ship.
At 0605 A/15, in position 32°50'N, 14°08'E, a destroyer or torpedo boat was seen steering straight towards. An attack was started. The target was accompanied by aircraft.

This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Calliope on passage from Tobruk to Tripoli which had received order to join the escort of the Amsterdam-convoy. She may have expected to meet it near Khoms but she learned at 0450 B/15 that the Amsterdam had been torpedoed and was proceeding to Khoms and then turned to the NE to meet her. [For the details of the Amsterdam convoy see one of the events for 14 October 1942.]
