At 0625 B/15, the target turned 180° and made off along her own tracks. It was thought to be a Spica-class torpedo boat. She was using Asdic at a speed of about 14 knots. She had closed to about 5000 yards from HMS P 42 before she turned away.

During the morning and forenoon there was exceptional air A/S activity. The area was obviously being search for a submarine known to be present. This was considered due to HMS P 42 being D/F'd the previous night when the enemy report had been sent.
At 1003 A/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0932 A/15 was received. [For the text of the signal and the earlier signal it referred to see the other event for 15 October 1942.]
Noon/15, HMS P 42 acted in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2326 A/14 and set course to operate in area 'N'.
At 1201 A/15, Smoke was sighted bearing 257°.

At 1420 A/15, the smoke sighted earlier was seen to come from two small vessels steering 105° at 9 knots. These were investigated in position 32°46'N, 14°21'E and proved to be a tug of about 150 tons escorted by a small and decrepit looking gunboat which had turbines. Both were too small (and probably drew too little) for torpedo attack. Gun action was not attempted due to the aircraft patrols in the area. It was assumed these craft were going to assist the damaged merchant vessel.
This were the tug Pronta and RD 39 on their way to the Amsterdam-convoy.
At 1440 A/15, HMS P 42 set course to the east for her new patrol position.

At 1826 A/15, HMS P 42 surfaced on the 100 fatom line to the north-east of Khoms. Lt. Mars decided to withdraw 20 miles from the coast for the night on account of today's A/S activity and due to the bright moon.

At 1949 A/15, in position 32°53'N, 14°35'E a torpedo boat was sighted at a range of 5000 yards. The enemy was proceeding to the north-north-west at 15 knots. P 42 dived to periscope depth as the enemy had the advantage of the moon. The torpedo boat was heard to be carrying out an Asdic sweep. She altered course to the south and her HE and transmissions faded in that direction. Nothing could be seen through the periscope.
This was once more the Calliope.
At 2021 A/15, HMS P 42 surfaced. There was nothing in sight. (31)

16 Oct 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) patrolled in the vicinity of position 32°31'N, 14°48'E with the idea of intercepting the reported damaged enemy merchant vessel while she was being towed along the coast.

At 1530 A/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1259 A/16 was received which contained the following; Aircraft report large merchant ship stopped in position 32°54'N, 14°24'E at 0912 A/16 with two destroyers and air escort in company and tug approaching from Homs. If destroyers are towing suggest attacking them first but keep one torpedo for merchant ship.
Course was set to proceed submerged at 5 knots towards the western edge of the patrol area to intercept the damaged ship by moonlight tonight.
At 1820 A/16, HMS P 42 surfaced and proceeded to the western edge of the area but now at full surface speed.

At 1840 A/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1821 A/16 was received which contained the following; My 1259 A/16. Aircraft will endeavour to locate and drop a flare over target every half hour after moon set to assist you.
At 2330 A/16, HMS P 42 was in position 2 miles outside the western edge of the patrol area.
At 2333 A/16, a flare was sighted bearing 107° at a range of about 12 miles. Commenced to close that position. (31)
17 Oct 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) attacked the stranded and damaged Italian transport Amsterdam (10873 GRT, built 1921) off Khoms with three torpedoes. No hits were obtained.
In the evening orders were received to proceed to the Lampedusa area to intercept a southbound convoy.
0003 A/17 - A flare was dropped half a mile ahead and another one half a mile on the port beam. These flares illuminated HMS P 42.

0050 A/17 - When in position 32°52'N, 14°06'E, sighted a small vessel bearing 087° at fairly close range. The officer of the Watch considered it to be a motor torpedo boat but it was later thought to have been a torpedo boat.
0052 A/17 - Dived. Heard HE but not very distinct although persistant.
0233 A/17 - Returned to periscope depth and inspected the horizon with the help of a flare dropped at this time.
0236 A/17 - Surfaced. Nothing in sight. Set course to the eastward while keeping listening watch.
0500 A/17 - Dived in position 32°52'N, 14°01'E and proceeded eastwards to inspect Khoms at sunset.

1300 A/17 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1159 A/17 which contained the following orders; If practicable fire one torpedo at large vessel reported aground off Khoms and then comply with my 1251 A/17 which will be signalled later. [see the other event for 17 October 1942 for the text of this signal.]
1445 A/17 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat and several aircraft patrolling up and down the coast of Khoms.
1500 A/17 - Sighted the beached ship in approximate position 5 cables north-east of Khoms main lighthouse. Due to shallow water and the patrol it was not possible to close much further.
1533 A/17 - Fired a torpedo from 5000 yards. No result was heard or seen.
1538 A/17 - Fired a second torpedo. No track was seen and it is considered this torpedo failed to run.
1547 A/17 - Fired a third torpedo from a range of 4000 yards. Again no result was seen or heard.

1550 A/17 - The torpedo boat, which had been going away to the south-eastwards, now turned and closed Khoms. A schooner lying alongside the damaged ship cast of and joined two other small vessels standing by, one of which was a tug [This was most likely the Pronta (182 GRT, built 1913)].

The torpedo boat subsequently appeared to continue her patrol although her Asdic transmission interval was increased. Aircraft activity in the area remained the same. No counter attack developed during P 42's withdrawal to seaward.
This torpedo boat was the Italian Calliope.
1822 A/17 - Surfaced 9 miles from Khoms and proceeded to the northwards to operate on the shipping route along 32°57'N.

2120 A/17 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1251 A/17 which had previously been missed when HMS P 42 had to go deep for patrolling aircraft. Course was altered to comply with these new orders. (27)

17 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1251 A/17, Capt. S.10 sent the following submarines HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) the following orders; Previous orders for Utmost, P 37, P 42 and P 44 are cancelled. Proceed to be in following positions by dawn on the 19th. HMS Utmost to position 36°16'N, 11°58'E proceeding through position 36°47'N, 13°10'E en-route. HMS P 37 to position 35°45'N, 11°58'E proceeding through position 35°40'N, 12°54'E en-route. HMS P 42 to position 35°25'N, 12°21'E proceeding through position 33°20'N, 13°55'E en-route. HMS P 44 to position 35°12'N, 12°33'E proceeding through position 33°20'N, 14°35'E en-route. HMS P 44 if unable comply proceed through position 33°41'N, 13°03'E and steer 343° keeping south of 35°10'N. (32)

18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1017 A/18, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) of the following; HMS Utmost was to move her assigned patrol position 10 miles to the south. HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) leaves the Malta searched channel sunset 18th direct for position 36°18'N, 11°53'E.

In a signal timed 1736 A/18, Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines that an aircraft had repored a large tanker and three merchant ships escorted by seven destroyers in position 38°15'N, 12°39'E, course 200°, speed 8 knots at 1615 A/18.

The 'DELTA' convoy was made up of the transports Titania (Italian, 5397 GRT, built 1918), Beppe (Italian, 4859 GRT, built 1912) and Capo Orso (Italian, 3149 GRT, built 1916), screened by the destroyers Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 1526-1530 B/17. The destroyers Giovanni da Verazzano and Antonio Pigafetta (escort leader) sailed from Naples at 1730 B/17 and 1905 B/17 respectively and joined the convoy at 2040 B/17.

The tanker Saturno (Italian, 5022 GRT, built 1914) sailed from Cagliari at 1535-1600 B/17 escorted by the torpedo boat Nicola Fabrizi. The destroyers Antonio da Noli and AscariSaturno. At 0655 B/18, Antonio da Noli joined the convoy and two hours later so did the Ascari.

At 1212 B/18-1235 B/18, the Titania and Saturno sections merged at 1212 B/18-1235 B/18. The torpedo boat Centauro sailed from Trapani at 1910 B/18 to relieve Fabrizi, which went on to this harbour, arriving at 2040 B/18.

The torpedo boat Sagittario had sailed from Palermo at 2015 B/17 to do an A/S sweep off Pantelleria. At 0028 B/19, she was ordered by Marina Messina to join the convoy off Pantelleria, to replace the torpedo boat Centauro. She met the convoy at 0923 B/19 and took position astern after which the Centauro left the convoy.

At 1250 B/19, Beppe and Giovanni da Verazzano were torpedoed and sunk by HMS P 37. At the time of the attack, the convoy was formed of Beppe, Capo Orso, Titania and Saturno, screened by Vincenzo Gioberti, Antonio da Noli, Antonio Pigafetta, Alfredo Oriani, Giovanni da Verazzano, Ascari and Sagittario. Oriani and Gioberti were ordered to hunt the submarine. They dropped depth charges and then picked up the survivors. The two destroyers proceeded to Lampedusa where they transferred the survivors to a fishing vessel which came out of the port; they then rejoined the convoy at 2006 B/19.

At 1610 A/19, HMS P 42 attacked the convoy with four torpedoes but no hits were obtained. Following this failed attack, HMS P 42 was depth charged and sustained damage which eventually forced her to abandon her war patrol.

At 2330 B/19, Titania was hit by a torpedo bomber in position 34°45'N, 12°31'E. Alfredo Oriani, Ascari and Sagittario were detached to assist the stricken vessel. Sagittario took some seriously wounded on board and brought them to Trapani at 0725 B/20. Titania was finished off by HMS P 211 at 0720 B/20. The two destroyers picked up the survivors and were ordered to land them at Tripoli if their autonomy allowed it or turn back to Trapani. Alfredo Oriani continued to Tripoli, which was reached at 1436 B/20. Ascari turned back and reached Trapani at 2251 B/20.
At 0230 B/20, the torpedo boat Circe departed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy.
Saturno, Capo Orso, Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Vincenzo Gioberti and Circe arrived at Tripoli at 1206 B/20-1420 B/20.

In a signal timed 2349 A/18, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to the five submarines listed above; After passage of convoy on 19th submariens are to proceed as follows to arrive end of searched channel 1031 A on dates given: P 211 direct to arrive 20/10. Utmost through positions 35°46'N, 12°56'E and 35°40'N, 14°06'E and P 37 direct to position 35°32'N, 14°12'E both to arrive 21/10. P 42 and P 44 to spend daylight hours 20/10 vicinity Kuriat and position 35°24'N, 12°24'E respectively leaving as requisite, P 42 north of Lampedusa to position 35°41'N, 14°05'E and P 44 direct to position 35°31'N, 14°11'E to arrive 22/10. No submarine encountered is to be attacked unless definitely identified as hostile. (32)

19 Oct 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) attacked a convoy north-west of Tripoli, Libya in position 34°45'N 12°31'E. All the torpedoes missed. The target was the Italian tanker Saturno (5022 GRT, built 1914) which had the first two torpedoes missing under her and manoeuvred to avoid the next two. She was in company with the Italian transports Titania (5397 GRT, built 1918) and Capo Orso (3149 GRT, 1916), escorted by the destroyers Ascari, Antonio da Noli and Antonio Pigafetta. The Titania was sunk early the next day by HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) east of Tunisia in position 34°45'N, 12°31'E.
Following the attack the submarine was hunted by Antonio Pigafetta and Antonio da Noli.
0159 A/19 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2349 A/18. [See the event for 18 October 1942 for the text of this signal.]
0232 A/19 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0210 A/19. [See the other event for 19 October 1942 for the text of this signal.]
0537 A/19 - Dived in position 35°23'N, 12°15'E (D.R.).
1000 A/19 - Fixed position as being 161° - Lampion Island - 8.5 miles.
1224 A/19 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1130 A/19. [See the other event for 19 October 1942 for the text of this signal.]
1335 A/19 - Sighted a patrolling seaplane bearing 290°. Commenced to close on course 270°.
Received HMS Utmost's (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) contact report timed 0840 A/19 which contained the following; Convoy sighted in position 36°08'N, 12°01'E, course 180°, speed 8 knots.
1405 A/19 - Sighted smoke bearing 290°. Ran in to intercept.

1430 A/19 - The course of the convoy was estimated as 135°. Started attack. The convoy zigged twice first to 155° and shortly before firing to 175°. Three escorting aircraft were seen. The convoy consisted of one large merchant vessel of 7000 ton, a tanker of 8000 tons and a smaller merchant vessel. Escort was provided by fleet destroyers of which three (possibly four) were seen.

1510 A/19 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes. Two were aimed at the 7000 ton merchant, one at the tanker and one at the smaller merchant vessel. Range was estimated as being 8000 yards. Two explosions were heard 7m 10s and 7m 45s. These sounded like torpedo hits. P 42 went to 70 feet on firing and increased to full speed and altered course to get off the torpedo tracks.
1521 A/19 - An accurate and swift counter attack started. Twenty depth charges were dropped. All were close and caused serious damage to P 42.
1537 A/19 - The last depth charge was dropped.
1600 A/19 - The hunt by three destroyers continued although no depth charges were dropped.
1620 A/19 - No more HE or Asdic transmissions were heard.
1720 A/19 - Came to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.

1906 A/19 - Passed an enemy report timed 1844 A/19 which contained the following; Convoy of one tanker and two or three merchant ships escorted by three or four destroyers in position 35°21'N, 12°13'E, course 160°, speed 8 knots at 1510 A/19. Attacked with four torpedoes, result unknown, counter attack materialised.
1919 A/19 - Re-assessment of the damage made Lt. Mars decide to abandon the patrol and return to Malta. Course was set accordingly.

2017 A/19 - Passed the signal timed 1931 A/19 which contained the following; Many batteries damaged am returning to base through position 35°15'N, 12°10'E and 35°15'N, 12°40'E at full speed 1000 A/20 on surface by day Tuesday.

2334 A/19 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2230 A/19 which contained the following; Your 1931 A/19 acknowledged. Motor launch will rendezvous in position 252° - 40 miles from the end of the searched channel at 0601 A/20 and escort you in. Fighters will be available if air raid develops. (27)

19 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0210 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN); Convoy in position 37°24'N, 11°53'E, course 180°, speed 8 knots at 0058 A/19.

In a signal timed 1130 A/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information regarding the convoy to the above mentioned submarines; My 0210 A/19. Convoy, composition unknown, course 180° in position 310° - Lampedusa - 35 miles. (32)

20 Oct 1942 At 0038 A/20, to the south-east of Lampedusa, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) sighted HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) bearing 030° (approximately). Both submarines turned away and sight was lost.

At 0110 A/20, a submarine was sighted astern which made off at speed. The challenge was made but there came no reply. This was again HMS P 211 but the challenge was not recognised. An enemy submarine was also known to be in the area. [We can not place an enemy submarine in this area on this day though.]
At 0621 A/20, in position 252° - end of the searched channel - 46 miles, made rendezvous with HMML 121.
At 1045 A/20, HMML 135 joined company.
At 1200 A/20, rendezvous was made with HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) and HMS P 42 was then swept into harbour ending her 8th war patrol (also 8th in the Mediterranean) around 1400 A/20.
HMS P 42 was subsequently taken in hand for repairs and docking at the Malta Dockyard. (27)

15 Nov 1942 At 1200 A/15, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (also 9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Genoa but this was later changed to the south of the Gulf of Naples.
HMS P 42 was to pass through position 37°19'N, 12°49'E around 0600 A/16 and then pass submerged through Q.B.B. 255 [mined area].
Before proceeding to her patrol area to the south of the Gulf of Naples she was to patrol for 24 hours in position 37°52'N, 11°25'E.
She was escorted out until 1400 A/15 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). (27)
15 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 42 during her 9th war patrol see the map below.
16 Nov 1942 At 0605 A/16, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) dived in position 37°19'N, 12°49'E to make the submerged westbound passage through Q.B.B. 255 [mined area] at 80 feet.

At 1845 A/16, while deep in position 37°44'N, 12°04'E, HE was heard followed by Asdic transmissions on bearing 045°. Avoiding action was taken but P 42 was hunted for about an hour by what is thought to be an A/S trawler.
At 2000 A/16, all was quiet.
At 2026 A/16, HMS P 42 surfaced.

At 2120 A/16, HMS P 42 fixed her position as being 220° - Libeccio Point (Marettimo) - 10 miles which was three miles astern of the D.R. position. Set course to patrol to the west of Marettimo in position 37°52'N, 11°25'E. (27)

17 Nov 1942 At 0110 A/17, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) arrived in the patrol position (37°52'N, 11°25'E) and withdrew 10 miles 240° to allow the passage of HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 48 (Lt. M.E. Faber, RN).

This in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2358 A/15 which had contained the following orders; After gaining patrol position withdraw 10 miles 240° until 0500 A/17 to let HMS P 45 and HMS P 48 pass you.

At 0500 A/17, HMS P 42 dived and regained position. Kept patrol within 4 miles from the ordered patrol position but nothing was sighted. By noon keeping periscope depth patrol was getting difficult and by 1400 A/17 a moderate gale was blowing from the west-north-west.

At 1330 A/17, in position 37°54'N, 11°18'E a medium sized modern looking merchant vessel was sighted which was escorted by a destroyer or torpedo boat. Enemy course was 090° at about 11 knots. The ship was not attacked in accordance with the orders as it was bound for Italy so most likely empty. The state of the sea was such that further details of these ships could not be observed.

These were most probably the Italian naval auxiliaries Narenta (1362 GRT, built 1934) and Lago Zuai (783 GRT, built 1940), escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Cigno. They sailed from Bizerte at 0700 B/17 and arrived at Trapani at 1848 B/17. Perhaps due to the state of the sea, P 42 did not see one of the auxiliaries.
Between 1625 A/17 and 1730 A/17, many distant underwater explosions were heard which seemed to bear 240°.
At 1740 A/17, HMS P 42 surfaced and set course to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1352 A/17. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 17 November 1942.]

At 1840 A/17, HMS P 42 set course to proceed to position 38°15'N, 12°00'E in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1620 A/17 which had contained the following information / orders for P 42; Air reconnaissance shows three large merchant ships entered Palermo at 1000 hours today. Four destroyers arriving now. Consider ships will sail for Bizerta after dark, either two convoys of about 14 and 10 knots or all at 10 knots. Fastest convoy is the most important. Move with all despatch to position 38°15'N, 12°00'E. Submarines on enemy’s route have freedom of action from patrol positions at right angles to enemy's line of advance and may follow and repeat attack if speed allows. No submarine to be attacked. Target justifies firing a full salvo. HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, DSO, RN) passes from position 38°05'N, 11°55'E to position 'B' between 0100 A/18 and 0230 A18. Convoy vitally important. Good luck.
The four destroyers mentioned in the signal were the Mitragliere, Bombardiere, Legionario and Velite which had sailed from Bizerte at 0700 A/17-0728 A/17 and arrived at Palermo at 1940 A/17-2050 A/17

2230 A/17, HMS P 42 was estimated to be in the ordered patrol position but no sights or bearings had been obtained for 24 hours. Later it became apparent that HMS P 42 was about 8 miles to the west of the ordered patrol position. (33)

17 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1352 A/17, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN); After dark tonight P 42 move through position 38°06'N, 11°42'E to position 360° - Cape San Vito - 4 miles. At 0001 A/18 Parthian and P 37 move to establish patrol line in position 360° 7 and 14 miles respectively from P 42. (34)
18 Nov 1942 In the early morning it was necessary for HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) to heave to due to the north-westerly gale.

At 0630 A/18, it was finally possible to take a starsight which revealed P 42 was now about 10 miles to the west of the ordered and estimated position. She then dived and set course for the ordered patrol position (38°15'N, 12°00'E).

At 1602 A/18, the enemy reports timed 1233 A/18 and 1246 A/18 of HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) were received which gave the the following; One convoy in position 38°23'N, 12°41'E, course 270°, speed 13 knots.

At 1634 A/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1531 A/18 was received which contained the following; Seems probable convoy sighted by P 37 at 1233 A/18 is southbound to Tripoli and that Tunis bound shipping is still at Palermo. My 1620 A/17 is in force for tonight. [This was however not the case as the convoy sighted by HMS P 37 was proceeding from Palermo to Bizerta.]

At 1736 A/18, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°16'N, 11°45'E. The gale was still blowing in full force accompanied by severe rain squalls and thunderstorms which some bright moonlit periods. Set course for the patrol position.
At 2047 A/18, a fix was obtained in position 005° - Marettimo - 17.5 miles. P 42 then commenced to patrol within two miles of the ordered patrol position (38°15'N, 12°00'E). (33)

19 Nov 1942 At 0004 A/19, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) received the contact report timed 2041 A/18, from HMS Parthian (Lt.Cdr. D. St. Clair-Ford, RN) which stated the following; One large merchant ship and two destroyers in position 000° - Cape Gallo Light - 5 miles, course 280°, 12 knots at 2030 A/18th.

These were Italian transport Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1930) escorted by the destroyers Freccia and Velite which sailed from Palermo at 1840 A/18-1900 A/18 and arrived in Bizerte at 2200 A/19-2230 A/19.

At 0008 A/19, P 42 sighted a submarine fine on the port bow. The submarine was steering a south-westerly course. The other submarine soon dived. It was considered to have been HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) which had also sighted a submarine, at 0005 A/18.
At 0030 A/19, the visibility, which had been about 7 miles at midnight started to deteriorate again with rainsqualls setting in.

At 0130 A/19, the signal timed 2325 A/18 from HMS Parthian was received which contained the following; My 2041 A/18. Visibility under half a mile in heavy rain squalls. Enemy last sighted on course 270° at 2057 A/18. Nothing in sight for 6 miles at 2315 A/18 in position 38°27'N, 12°48'E. Consider enemy speed at least 15 knots. Delay caused by defective W/T apparatus.
At 0254 A/19, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0254 A/19 was received which was a summary of the report of HMS Parthian. The enemy had almost certainly passed P 42's position by this time.
At 0420 A/19, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0329 A/19 was received which contained the following orders; Establish patrol forthwith within 12 miles of Palermo Light.
At 0613 A/19, HMS P 42 dived in position 38°17'N, 12°22'E (D.R.) and proceeded at 70 feet, coming up for routines at the set times.
At 1421 A/19, a distant explosion was heard.
At 1546 A/19, another distant explosion was heard.
At 1810 A/19, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°18'N, 12°40'E. The weather had moderated. (33)
20 Nov 1942 At 0310 A/20, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) fixed her position as being 320° - Cape Gallo - 14 miles. Patrolled in this vicinity because of the bright moon.
At 0553 A/20, HMS P 42 dived and closed Cape Gallo.
At 0800 A/20, Lt. Mars chose to patrol in position 312° - Cape Gallo - 5 miles and remained within 3 miles of this position.
At 1437 A/20, smoke was sighted to the eastward, to the north-east of Cape Gallo.

At 1450 A/20, the source of the smoke was seen to be a small coastal steamer of under 1000 tons proceeding on course 275° at 5 knots in position 38°20'N, 13°10'E. It was not attacked in accordance with the patrol orders. A gun attack was out of the question due to the enemy air activity. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1803 A/20, HMS P 42 surfaced after having withdrawn about 8 miles from the coast and patrolled in this vicinity during the night. It was a bright moonlit night. (27)

21 Nov 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) attacked an enemy convoy to the north of Palermo, Sicily. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. The target was the transport Liv (Italian (former Norwegian), 3068 GRT, built 1896) in company with the transport Favorita (Italian, 3576 GRT, built 1916) escorted by the Italian auxiliary Cattaro (1275 GRT, built 1933). The next day, Favorita was hit by a Wellington torpedo bomber and finished off by the submarine HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN).

They had sailed from Palermo at 0600 A/21 for Cagliari, After the attack by HMS P 42, they were attacked by enemy bombers and Favorita was torpedoed at 2300 A/22 near Cape Carbonara. She was still afloat, but in imminent danger of sinking so, the crew abandoned ship and Cattaro picked them up. At 2355 A/22, HMS P 228 fished off Favorita. Cattaro rejoined Liv at 0100 A/23. At 0230 A/23, Liv was attacked by an aircraft and managed to avoid the torpedo. At 0305 A/23, it was Cattaro’s turn to avoid a torpedo, which missed 20 metres astern. They arrived in Cagliari at 1435 A/23. The destroyer Antonio da Noli and the tug Nereo (340 GRT, built 1936) were sent to assist Favorita, but they only found three empty lifeboats.
[Note: Maricotraf indicates the convoy sailed from Palermo at 0600 B/21 and again at 0600 B/22. It is possible that they returned to Palermo after the attack by P 42.]
0520 A/21 - In position 38°25'N, 13°18'E, sighted a destroyer / torpedo boat or patrol vessel to seaward and making for Cape Gallo / Palermo. No HE or A/S transmissions were heard.

Perhaps either the Italian torpedo boat Groppo or Perseo. The Italian transports Viminale (8657 GRT, built 1925) and Puccini (2422 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the torpedo boat Groppo had sailed from Bizerte at 0902 A/20. At 1655 A/20, the escort was reinforced by the torpedo boat Perseo from Tunis. They arrived in Palermo at 0902 A/21-0925 A/21.
0543 A/21 - Dived deep in position 315° - Cape Gallo - 9 miles and closed the coast.
0615 A/21 - Heard A/S impulses coming from the direction of the shore. Took evading action.
0650 A/21 - At periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
0756 A/21 - Sighted two CANT Z 501 aircraft.
0758 A/21 -Sighed smoke bearing 120°, closed.

0837 A/21 - A convoy of three ships was now in sight to the north-west. Started attack. Enemy course was 335°, speed 10 knots. The original plan of attack was spoiled by a zig (the first) of the leading ship, which turned out to be a small armed merchant cruiser.
0930 A/21 - In position 38°20'N, 13°20'E fired four torpedoes at the two cargo vessels of the convoy. Range was about 2000 yards. No results. These ships were 4000-5000 tons in displacement.
0936 A/21 - A counter attack was started. Fifteen depth charges were dropped, none was very close.
1103 A/21 - The last depth charge was dropped. P 42 meanwhile was withdrawing to the west.
1155 A/21 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1215 A/21 - Went deep for several Cant. Z 501's which were patrolling in the area.
1400 A/21 - Heard distant Asdic impulses.
1515 A/21 - Heard numerous distant explosions as if a depth charge attack was being carried out. No other submarines were in the area though. In half an hour 22 explosions were heard.
1555 A/21 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1606 A/21 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1245 A/21 which stated that P 42 had been sighted at 0940 A/21. This was no surprise as that was just after attacking the convoy.
1752 A/21 - Surfaced in position 38°29'N, 12°49'E and proceeded to the north-west.

1822 A/21 - Passed out signal timed 1741 A/21 which contained the following; At 0930 A/21 one medium armed merchant cruiser and two medium merchant ships in position 38°21'N, 13°13'E, course 330°, speed 10 knots, unsuccessfully attacked. Counter attack light. Palermo and vicinity considerable air and A/S air patrol activities. Am withdrawing to the vicinity of Ustica for 24 hours. 4 torpedoes and 200 rounds of ammunition remain.
2043 A/21 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1540 A/21 which contained the following orders; You have freedom of action within area bounded by 38° 46'N, 12°40'E and 38°46'N, 13°50'E. (33)
22 Nov 1942 At 0001 A/22, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) commenced to patrol on the surface 6 miles to the north-east of Ustica.
At 0553 A/22, HMS P 42 dived and patrolled on a line 4 miles to the east of Ustica.
At 0926 A/22, very distant smoke was sighted to the southwest at a range of about 15 to 20 miles. This smoke was not seen again.

At 1204 A/22, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1129 A/22 was received which contained the following; Two merchant ships and one escort vessel in position 360° - Cape Gallo - 15 miles, course 320°, speed 10 knots.
It appears this referred to the Favorita-convoy. [See the event of 21 November 1942.]
P 42 altered course to close and intercept but nothing materialised and the convoy must have altered course to the west or south-west.

1457 A/22 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1421 A/22 which indicated that P 42 had been sighted on the surface at 0530 A/22. In view of this, the full moon, and the constant A/S air patrol near Ustica, Lt. Mars decided to withdraw to the southwards and then to the west in order to spend the night in the extreme north-west corner of the patrol area and then close Cape San Vito before dawn.

At 2110 A/22, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1225 A/22 was received which contained the following orders; Proceed after dark today via north coast Sicily to the Gulf of Eufemia thence via Stromboli back to your present area by dawn 26th. Object to sink west or southbound shipping and to destroy by gunfire a train on the Messina - Palermo line and also one train on the Italian mainland.
These orders were considered to be most welcome and course was set for train wrecking in the vicinity of Cape Cefalu. (33)

23 Nov 1942 At 0447 A/23, north of Cefalù, Sicily, in position 38°14'N, 14°00'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sighted two ships proceeding on course 280° at 8 knots. Being on the wrong side of the moon, HMS P 42 dived for a periscope attack. Lt. Mars initially thought the enemy might be a submarine escorted by a motor torpedo boat.

This may have been the Italian landing ships MZ 705 and MZ 756. They had sailed from Reggio at 1000 A/21 for Palermo (arrival time not known to us). [They later sailed from Palermo at 0140 A/25 and arrived in Bizerte at 1200 A/26.]
At 0515 A/23, the range had come down to 1500 yards. The vessels were now identified as two large landing craft. Speed was now estimated to be 10 knots. They had diesel engines.

At 0745 A/23 - HMS P 42 fixed her position as being 025° - Cape Cefalu - 4 miles. Moved westwards during the day and chose the masonry bridge over the river Tusa (in 14°17'E) as a suitable spit for train gunning.
At 1530 A/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1357 A/23 was received. P 42 was ordered to patrol off Cape Rasocolmo until sunset/24. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.]

At 1720 A/23, HMS P 42 closed to within 1600 yards of the bridge selected as target with the intention of bombarding it at dusk. Four trains had been sighted during the day. However no trains arrived so it was decided to make a second attempt after dark.

After dark, HMS P 42 surfaced 2.5 miles from the shore. The night was cloudless and the full moon, bright from the east, caused Lt. Mars to abandon the gunning of a train in this area for the time being. Set course to patrol off Cape Rasocolmo. (27)
24 Nov 1942 At 0323 A/24, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°28'N, 15°19'E (315° - Cape Rasocolmo - 15 miles) and set course to close the shore while remaining deep.
At 0700 A/24, HMS P 42 came to periscope depth and commenced to patrol within two miles of position 310° - Cape Rasocolmo - 6 miles.

At 0910 A/24, the Swedish relief ships Eros (3152 GRT, built 1941), Formosa (7033 GRT, built 1921) and Camelia (1688 GRT, built 1941) and one ancient escort were sighted in position 38°20'N, 15°25'E. They were steering 090° bound for the Straits of Messina.
Two fishing trawlers, possibly naval auxiliaries and a couple of fishing smacks were observed during the forenoon.
At 1343 A/24, a distant explosion was heard. Visibility was good throughout the day and became excellent during the afternoon.
At 1700 A/24, it started to rain and visibility decreased. P 42 went deep and withdrew to the north.
At 1803 A/24, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°25'N, 15°25'E and set course for Cape Suvero for train bombardment. (27)

25 Nov 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) bombarded a railway viaduct immediately north of Cape Suvero lighthouse. A southbound train was just crossing this viaduct. Unfortunately, the gun jammed after the second round. The shoot had to be abandoned and HMS P 42 retired from the coast.

0250 A/25 - In position 1000 yards from the stone railway viaduct immediately to the north of the Cape Suvero lighthouse. Opened fire on a southbound electric train but the gun jammed after just firing two rounds. The defect could not be repaired and the shoot had to be abandoned. P 42 then withdrew from the coast.
0535 A/25 - Dived in position 38°50'N, 15°54'E and patrolled near Cape Gozzo making towards Strombili in the afternoon.

1742 A/25 - Surfaced in position 38°53'N, 15.34'E and set course to pass to the north of Stromboli. Attempts were made to clear the gun but these failed and the idea to bombard a train on the bridge over the river Tusa had to be given up. (27)
26 Nov 1942 At 0500 A/26, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°30'N, 13°50'E and closed Cape Zaffarano. Visibility was variable during the day due to rain.
At 1257 A/26, in position 38°10'N, 13°38'E, a destroyer was sighted proceeding towards Palermo on course 260° at 20 knots.
This was the Italian Corazziere, which sailed from Messina at 0618 A/26 and arrived in Palermo at 1349 A/26.

At 1620 A/26, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1226 A/26 was received. This signal contained the following orders; Establish patrol forthwith within 10 miles north of Cape Gallo. Reconnaissance indicates westbound traffic from Palermo may be expected very shortly.
At 1830 A/26, HMS P 42 surfaced and proceed to the newly ordered patrol position. (33)
27 Nov 1942 At 0100 A/27, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) commenced to patrol in position 310° - Cape Gallo - 10 miles.
At 0233 A/27, in position 38°14'N, 13°00'E, HMS P 42 sighted a convoy of two small vessels and one escort proceeding east along the coastal shipping route.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.]
At 0240 A/27, HMS P 42 dived in position 306° - Cape Gallo - 12 miles. The convoy was about 6 miles, 220° from HMS P 42.

At 0303 A/27, HMS P 42 surfaced. The enemy was still in sight hardly moving. An attack was not allowed as this traffic was eastbound. HMS P 42 withdrew 5 miles on the surface and then returned to the original patrol position.
At 0502 A/27, HMS P 42 dived deep and proceeded towards position 312° - Cape Gallo - 7.5 miles to patrol near that position during the day.

0640 A/27, HMS P 42 came to periscope depth. The two small ships of about 1000 tons were now seen to be clear of Cape Gallo to the east. The appeared to continue to the east and did not enter Palermo.

At 1906 A/27, HMS P 42 surfaced and patrolled near position 310° - Cape Gallo - 10 miles. During the night a small lighted hospital ship patrolled off Raisi Point and behaved in a suspicious manner. This vessel was in sight on and off all night.

Perhaps this was the small Italian hospital ship Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) which sailed from Trapani at 0450 A/27 and returned at 1415 A/28. [We have no information on the purpose of this sortie though, and we thus do not know if she sailed towards Cape Gallo.]
At 2016 A/27, in position 38°25'N, 13°10'E, HMS P 42 dived for two destroyers approaching at speed from the north-west steering 140° for Cape Gallo / Palermo.

Actually there were most likely three, not destroyer but torpedo boats, the Ciclone, Ardente and Procione, which sailed from Tunis at 0803 A/27-0805 A/27 and arrived in Palermo at 2320 A/27-2349 A/27.
At 2041 A/27, HMS P 42 surfaced and continued her patrol. (27)

28 Nov 1942 At 0503 A/28, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°20'N, 13°06'E for a fast approaching destroyer steering towards Cape Gallo. Faint A/S impulses were heard but these soon faded.
Most probably this was one out of the following; Ascari, Mitragliere or Grecale. They sailed from Bizerte at 1617 B/27-1650 B/27 and arrived in Palermo at 0700 A/28-0740 A/28.
At 0707 A/28, another destroyer was observed closing Cape Gallo from the westwards at 15 knots.
This was Camicia Nera which sailed from Bizerte at 1740 A/27 and arrived in Palermo at 0900 A/28.
At 0750 A/28, a hospital ship was sighted. This vessel looked like the Città di Trapani (Italian, 2467 GRT, built 1929). The vessel was steering eastwards 5 miles to the north of Femmine Island.
At 0830 A/28, the hospital ship reversed course and eventually disappeared towards Raisi Point.
This was most likely again the Italian Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) [see also the event for 27 November].

At 1140 A/28, in position 38°15'N, 13°11'E, an eastbound convoy (course 090°) was sighted very close inshore. It probably passed within one mile from Femmine Island. The convoy consisted of two 5000 to 7000 tons tankers and two schooners. They were escorted by a modern destroyer and an older three-funnel torpedo boat. One of the schooners remained off Femmine Island. The destroyer was seen to stop off Cape Gallo.

These were the Italian tanker Marte (2502 GRT, built 1892) and the Italian naval (water) tanker Istria (2900 GRT, built 1923) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardito and Giacomo Medici. They had sailed from Trapani at 0845 A/28 for Naples. At 1130 A/28, they met on an opposite course, eight schooners used as auxiliary minesweepers (probably two of them were the ones sighted by HMS Unbroken). The small Italian tanker Cocito (611 GRT, built 1917) was to sail from Palermo to join the convoy, but she turned back due to defects. The convoy arrived in Naples at 1010 B/29.
At 1215 A/28, and explosion was heard.
In the afternoon visibility decreased and observation towards the shore became very difficult. Until 1424 A/28, 21 depth charges were dropped, singly and small patterns.
At 1906 A/28, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°26'N, 13°09'E and commenced charging the battery.
At 1940 A/28, the signal timed 1737 A/28 from the Capt. S.10 was received. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 28 November 1942.] Course was set to comply with this signal. (27)

28 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1737 A/28, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN); Move to establish patrol line by 2359/29th. P 42 7 miles, Tribune 12 miles 350° from Cape San Vito. Tribune routed through position 38°42'N, 11°20'E and when well clear make brief report on fuel and torpedoes remaining. (34)
29 Nov 1942 At 0330 A/29, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) arrived in her new patrol position, 350° - Cape San Vito - 7 miles. She commenced to patrol within 2.5 miles from this spot.

At 0500 A/29, HMS P 42 dived. The hospital ship, believed to be the Città di Trapani (Italian, 2467 GRT, built 1929) again was patrolling in the area between 0510 A/29 and 0830 A/29 when it disappeared proceeding on course 265° at 10 knots.
Once again this was most probably the Italian Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) again. She sailed from Trapani at 0240 A/29 and returned there at 1125 A/29.
At 0730 A/29, a modern torpedo boat or destroyer was seen close inshore proceeding eastwards at speed.

This was the Italian torpedo boat Animoso. The Italian destroyer Maestrale sailed from Bizerte at 1215 A/28 and waited for the transport Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1930) which had been hit by a bomb and had a fire in her bow. It was finally extinguished and the motorship came out of the harbour at 1410 A/28, and they set out for Palermo. At 1750 A/28, they were reinforced by the torpedo boat Animoso, which had sailed at 1444 A/28 and at 2015 A/28 by the destroyer Folgore which had sailed at 1415 A/28. At 2240 A/28, in position 38°13’ N, 13°05’ E (north of Cape San Vito), Città di Napoli was hit by a torpedo or a mine [the latter was more logical as no submarine made a claim]. Folgore attempted to take her in tow, but the transport sank at 2330 A/28. Maestrale carried out a search for the presumed submarine without finding anything and it was later believed that Città di Napoli had been the victim of an Italian minefield. At 0150 A/29, Folgore signalled she had picked up the entire crew except for an antiaircraft gunner. The two destroyers arrived in Palermo at 0445 A/29. Animoso stayed behind to hunt the 'suspected submarine' and arrived in Palermo at 1013 A/29.
At 0830 A/29, in position 38°13'N, 13°10'E, an eastbound vessel was seen close inshore. It was thought to be possibly a small tanker. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
Between 1353 A/29 and 1516 A/29, thirteen distant explosions were heard.
At 1537 A/29, two motor torpedo boats passed about 6 miles off Cape San Vito on an easterly course.
These must have been the Italian MAS 543 and MAS 547 which had sailed from Palermo at 0730 A/29 for an A/S sweep and returned at 0140/30.
At 1905 A/29, HMS P 42 surfaced and commenced to charge the battery.

At 2130 A/29, HMS P 42 dived for two patrol craft, one of which had a fast diesel engine as such HE was picked up. Lt. Mars decided to stay submerged until 0246 A/30 when HMS P 42 surfaced to charge and ventilate.
Once again this must have been MAS 543 and MAS 547. (27)

30 Nov 1942 At 0314 A/30, after less than half an hour on the surface, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sighted a destroyer or torpedo boat up moon and inshore of the submarine. The enemy was moving at speed. HMS P 42 dived to avoid. Position was off Cape San Vito, in 38°14'N, 12°42'E.

Between 0330 and 0445 A/30, HMS P 42 was hunted by four Asdic equipped ships some of which had turbines. At one time the transmissions of two of these vessels were heard clearly throughout the submarine. Apparently P 42 had been detected while on the surface and lost contact when she dived. Asdic transmissions had commenced four minutes after diving.
The vessels encountered were most likely the Italian torpedo boats Partenope and Perseo which sailed from Trapani at 1626 A/29-1920 A/29 for an A/S sweep and returned there at 1146 A/30-1155 A/30.
At 0530 A/30, HMS P 42 withdrew to the northward as the patrol position was obviously compromised.
Lt. Mars decided to retired to the convoy routes from Naples to the south-west.
At 1650 A/30, distant explosions were heard.
At 1815 A/30, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°44'N, 12°37'E and proceeded to the west.
At 2123 A/30, gunfire and searchlights were seen in the direction of Trapani.

At 2330 A/30, HMS P 42 passed her signal timed 1557 A/30 which contained the following; Intensive and accurate anti-submarine activity in this area indicates either signals compromised or most efficient enemy R.D.F. and/or A.S.V. organization along the coast. Have withdrawn. Four torpedoes left. (33)

1 Dec 1942 Aircraft reports, although conflicting, revealed a convoy of three or four merchant ship and four destroyers south-westbound from Naples. Lt. Mars decided to try to intercept the convoy to the outside of Ustica Island.

Convoy 'B', made up of the Italian transports Arlesiana (5702 GRT, built 1915), Achille Lauro (5209 GRT, built 1921), Campania (5247 GRT, built 1918) and German transports Menes (5609 GRT, built 1926) and Lisboa (1799 GRT, built 1911) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio (convoy leader), Groppo, Pallade and Orione sailed from Naples at 1222 A/30-1320 A/30. At 0703 A/1, they were reinforced by the Italian torpedo boat Uragano, which had sailed from Trapani at 0015 A/1. At 1020 A/1, the convoy was in position 39°31'30"N, 11°53'E. At 1500 A/1, Pallade had to drop out of the formation due to a defective condenser and proceeded to Palermo, where she arrived at 0840 A/2. At 1845 A/1, the convoy was further reinforced by the Italian destroyers Maestrale (which took over command of the convoy), Ascari and Grecale (a fourth destroyer, Mitragliere, had gone directly to Trapani because of defects, arriving at 1617 A/1), which had just laid a minefield.

At 2015 A/1, many explosions and flares were seen, in what was thought to be the direction of convoy 'H', which was made up of the Italian transports Aventino (3794 GRT, built 1907), Aspromonte (976 GRT, built 1922), Puccini (2422 GRT, built 1928), Aspromonte (976 GRT, built 1922) and the German KT 1 (850 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Camicia Nera, Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Procione and Clio. This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 1015 A/1.

But actually, it is more likely that these were dropped near the Italian tanker Giorgio (4887 GRT, built 1907) escorted by the Italian destroyer Lampo and the Italian torpedo boat Climene, which had sailed from Palermo at 0900 A/1 and were attacked by torpedo bombers at 2215 A/1 in position 268° - Trapani – 44 miles. Giorgio was torpedoed and beached near Trapani.

Convoy 'B' was illuminated by flares at 2100 A/1-2203 A/1 but was not attacked. At 0100 A/2, Maestrale ordered the convoy to turn back to Palermo. At 0230 A/2, a torpedo boat was sighted which appeared to attack the convoy. Maestrale ordered all the escorts to move between the convoy and the enemy (Sirio reported four enemy destroyers, but she may have mistaken the Maestrale squadron for enemy). At 0635 A/2, Maestrale signalled the convoy that she had received the order from Supermarina to proceed to Trapani. They arrived there at 0927 A/2-1130 A/2, except Orione, which arrived at 1735 A/2.

Convoy 'B' sailed again from Trapani at 1700 A/2-1814 A/2, escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio (escort leader), Groppo, Uragano and Orione. At 0735 A/3, they were reinforced by two German S-boats (out of the following, S 58, S 60, S 56 and S 54, which had sailed from Bizerte at 2115 A/2). At 0230 A/3, the Italian torpedo boat Animoso sailed from Trapani to reinforce the escort. At 0830 A/3, she sighted the convoy and took her place in the screen. The attack on the convoy by HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) was noticed by a German aircraft, which fired machine-gun bursts at sea at 1118 A/3. Groppo was ordered to hunt the attacking submarine. At 1138 A/3, the Achille Lauro and Arlesiana, escorted by Orione and Animoso, were detached to Tunis. At 1000 A/3, the torpedo boat Antonio Mosto sailed from Tunis to join the escort of the Tunis section. At 1225 A/3, the Antonio Mosto was hit by a torpedo from HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) which failed to explode. Shortly after, she joined the convoy.

Campania and Menes, escorted by Sirio and Uragano, proceeded to Bizerte. At about the same time, the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori and two German S-boats were sighted and reinforced the Bizerte section. Giuseppe Sirtori had sailed from Bizerte at 0855 A/3 for minesweeping duty but was diverted to this new task. At 1235 A/3, Groppo rejoined them and was sent to escort the Tunis section, while Animoso was ordered to switch back to the Bizerte section. At 1330 A/3, Menes hit a mine and blew up. The four German S-boats remained in the area to pick up the survivors. Campania escorted by Sirio, Uragano and Animoso arrived in Bizerte at 1610 A/3-1703 A/3.
Achille Lauro and Arlesiana, escorted by Groppo, Orione, Antonio Mosto and Giuseppe Sirtori, arrived in Tunis at 1715 A/3-1737 A/3.

Convoy 'C', made up of the transports Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922), Veloce (5437 GRT, built 1911) and the Italian tanker Devoli (former Yugoslavian Perun, 3177 GRT, built 1939) escorted by Italian torpedo boats Lupo, Aretusa and Sagittario sailed from Naples at 2145 A/30-2320 A/30. At 0930 A/1, the Italian torpedo boat Ardente sailed from Palermo and joined the convoy off Trapani at 1630 A/1 and Devoli was detached to Trapani. At 1954 A/1, Sagittario was forced to leave the formation because of defects and arrived at Trapani at 0025 A/2. From 2130 A/1 to 0417 A/2, numerous flares were observed and several aircraft flew over the convoy without attacking it. At 1917 A/2, a single flare was seen near the convoy which began to make smoke. At about 2000 A/2, near Kerkennah, the convoy, which had split into two groups, Veloce and Lupo to the east of Chisone, Ardente and Aretusa was attacked by four Albacore torpedo bombers of 821 and 828 Squadron (FAA). Veloce was hit astern and set afire. At 2105 A/2, Lupo signalled that she would stand by her and ordered the other three to proceed. At 2346 A/2, Ardente observed gunfire from the direction of Lupo and assumed that she was being attacked by a naval force. Lupo and Veloce had been attacked by three or four enemy destroyers and sunk. Because, the vessels were laying smoke, Ardente lost sight of Chisone and Aretusa she increased speed to 24 knots to interfere with the enemy force should it try to deal with Chisone. At 0635 A/3, two German Junker 88 were observed and one of them released its bombs before landing in the sea. The torpedo boat picked up its crew. From 1208 A/3, survivors of Veloce and Lupo were seen, and Ardente began to pick them up. At 1528 A/3, an enemy bomber appeared. Antiaircraft was opened fire from a distance of 3000 metres, and the torpedo boat avoided a torpedo. At 1635 A/3, the last survivor was picked up; in all 29 were from Lupo and 72 from Veloce, there were 135 victims from Lupo and 63 from Veloce. Supermarina ordered Ardente to return to Trapani. Chisone and Aretusa arrived at Tripoli at 1830 A/3-1845 A/3.

Lupo had been sunk by the British destroyer HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, CB, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN or Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) which had departed Malta around 1400 A/2 and returned there around 0900 A/3.
At 0630 A/1, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°57'N, 12°34'E.
Between 0900 A/1 and 1100 A/1, several distant explosions were heard.
1145 A/1, in position 38°47'N, 12°40'E, HMS P 42 was caught at periscope depth by a low flying land plane, which dropped a bomb / depth charge but missed.
1300 A/1, as expected an aircraft commenced to patrol in the area. P 42 went deep and shortly afterwards HE and Asdic transmissions were heard.

1336 A/1, Lt. Mars considered that the HE and Asdic transmissions may also be coming from a convoy so P 42 returned to periscope depth. A patrolling aircraft, a large trawler and another warship, either a torpedo boat or a second trawler, were sighted. P 42 went deep (120 feet) again and remained there until no more HE was heard.
At 1420 A/1, a depth charge exploded within 5000 yards.

At 1600 A/1, HMS P 42 returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight. Later a patrolling aircraft was seen. Aircraft enemy reports now indicated that one convoy had passed 30 miles to the west and another 5 miles to the south-east of P 42 around 1430 A/1.

At 1840 A/1, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°58'N, 12°55'E and commenced charging. Lt. Mars decided to close Cape San Vito at speed in the morning, diving about 12 miles off in the absence of further instructions.

At 2100 A/1, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1150 A/1 was received which contained the following; Your 1557 A/30. The fury of the enemy is because you are in his way. Try 8 miles 340° from the ordered position. You will be recalled within 24 hours.
Course was subsequently set for position 345° - Cape San Vito - 15 miles.
At 2137 A/1, flares were seen to the south and west for the next half hour, most of these were very distant. (35)
2 Dec 1942 At 0349 A/2, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°20'N, 12°35'E and closed Cape San Vito.
At 0700 A/2, HMS P 42 commenced to patrol between position 345° - Cape San Vito - 15 miles and 4 miles to the south of it.
At 1843 A/2, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°27'N, 12°41'E and commenced charging. Nothing had been sighted all day.

At 2100 A/2, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1555 A/2, which contained the following orders; Leave patrol after dark tonight 2nd December, through position 38°28'N, 11°37'E to arrive in position 38°00'N, 11°25'E by 1900 A/3 thence following route and instructions given to TROOPER in my 0005 A/1, diving from positions 'J' to 'K' during daylight/4. Instructions for arrival at Malta will be signalled later.

The route and instructions given in the signal referred to above were as follows; Position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E). Position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E). Position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E). Position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E), thence direct to the Malta Sanctuary. (35)
3 Dec 1942 At 0626 A/3, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°06'N, 11°28'E (west of Marettimo) and patrolled in the vicinity of position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E).
At 0910 A/3, very distant smoke was sighted to the south-east but nothing developed.

At 1150 A/3, in position 37°57'N, 11°27'E, a destroyer, smoke and a seaplane were sighted to the north-east. The destroyer passed at a range of about 1000 yards using Asdic while proceeding at approximately 22 knots. Closed the smoke thinking the destroyer was part of the screen of a convoy. Commenced attack on masts and superstructure but this contact developed into a hospital ship. Course of the destroyer and hospital ship was approximately 240°.
At 1250 A/3, in position 37°58’N, 11°27’E the hospital ship stopped. Getting underway again soon. This vessel remained in the vicinity until dusk.
At 1400 A/3, another, hospital ship, similar in appearance was seen. Both were now ‘patrolling’ in the area.
At 1710 A/3, both hospital ships lit up and made off the the south-west.

At 1834 A/3, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 37°51'N, 11°23'E and set course to arrive in position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E) by midnight. Three hospital ships were in the vicinity. All passed out of sight to the north-east. They appeared to be steaming about, but remained near P 42's surfacing position.

The Italian hospital ships may have been the following; Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) sailed from Trapani at 2000 A/2 and returned at an unknown time [at least unknown to us]. Laurana (391 GRT, built 1939) sailed from Trapani at 1400 A/3 and returned at an unknown time [at least unknown to us]. It is possible that the Meta (243 GRT, built 1929) was in the area as she was later sent to pick up the survivors of the action off Kerkennah.
The 'destroyer' was perhaps the Italian torpedo boat Groppo, which was detached from convoy 'B' to hunt a 'presumed' submarine. [See the event for 1 December 1943 for more on this convoy.] (27)

4 Dec 1942 At 0006 A/4, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) arrived in position 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E) (D.R.) and entered Q.B.B. 255 (mined area). Later it was found out the D.R. [dead reckoning] position was off due to lack of sights and a defective log. Passage between positions 'H' and 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) was made 5 miles to the north-east of the ordered line.
At 0323 A/4, Pantelleria was sighted and the position was fixed at 0335 A/4 as being 37°07'N, 11°48'E.
At 0337 A/4, a small vessel was sighted, thought to possibly be a trawler. P 42 dived and set course for position 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E).
At 0715 A/4, HE was heard on bearing 028°.

At 0730 A/4, to the north-north-west of Pantelleria in position 37°00'N, 11°52'E, an attack was started on a ship steering 200° which was later seen to be a Citta di Trapani-class armed merchant cruiser painted light and dark grey. Her speed was 10.5 knots. Being off the ordered route, it was decided to attack this ship if the range gave a good chance of hitting her. Unfortunately during the end of the attack the target zigged way 25° to 175° putting HMS P 42 5000 yards off track and fire was withheld.
This was the Italian naval auxiliary Narenta (1362 GRT, built 1934), carrying stores and troops, which sailed from Trapani at 0150 A/4 and arrived in Pantelleria at 0845 A/4.
At 1030 A/4, a small northbound coaster was seen close inshore.
At 1630 A/4, a trawler was seen patrolling to the south-west of Pantellaria.
At 1842 A/4, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 36°32'N, 11°48'E and set course for Malta.

At 1850 A/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1114 A/4 was received which contained the following orders / information; My 1535 A/2. Proceed from position 'K' to make rendezvous with a motor launch at 0801 A/5 in position 36°00'N, 14°03'E whence you will be escorted into harbour. You are not to be south of 36°00'N, before rendezvous is made. Gozo Light north-west sector will be shown from 0400 A/5 until 0600 A/5.
At 1900 A/4, flares and AA gunfire were seen at great range to the westward. (35)
5 Dec 1942 Around 1235 A/5, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (also 9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMML 135 which had joined at 0725 A/5 in position 36°00'N, 14°03'E and then escorted the submarine in through the south-west searched channel. (27)
20 Dec 1942 Around 1315 A/20, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (also 10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Naples.

At 1545 A/20, sighted two destroyers proceeding to the south-west. This turned out to be HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, CB, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) which were to conduct an anti-shipping sweep near Djerba Island.
At 1638 A/20, HMS P 42 dived to pass through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area), passing to the west of Pantelleria. (27)
20 Dec 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 42 during her 10th war patrol see the map below.
21 Dec 1942 At 2205 A/21, in position 37°11'N, 11°25'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sighted a submarine on an opposite course. HMS P 42 dived to evade.
It was presumed this submarine was HMS Rorqual (Lt. L.W. Napier, RN) which was expected to be in the area.
HMS Rorqual reported nothing though but it is indeed very likely that it was her. (27)

21 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1225 A/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) and HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN); Move to establish patrol in position and by time given. After attack or at given time leave patrol or proceed in execution previous orders. P 212, patrol in position 38°14'N, 12°31'E from 1402 A/22 to 2359 A/22.M P 44, patrol in position 38°10'N, 11°50'E from 1758 A/22 to 0300 A/23. P 42, patrol in position 37°54'N, 11°17'E from 2256 A/22 to 0642 A/23.

These orders were issued to intercept the Italian transport Caterina Costa (8060 GRT, built 1942), escorted by the Italian destroyers Geniere and Camicia Nera, which sailed from Naples at 1430 A/22-1513 A/22. Geniere ran aground on the Skerki Bank (Keith’s Reef) and had to turn back, arriving in Palermo around 1000 A/24 (see below). The convoy was reinforced by the destroyer Bombardiere from Bizerte at 1220 A/23 in position 37°35'N, 10°39'E. They arrived in Bizerte at 1621 A/23-1700 A/23.

At 1220 A/23, Geniere sighted the destroyers Legionario and Grecale and asked the former to take her in tow. She did so at 1334 A/23 but the cable quickly broke, and towing was abandoned. The three destroyers proceeded to Trapani in company. At 1420 A/23, Marina Trapani informed them that the destroyer Saetta and the tug Tifeo had sailed to their assistance. For some unknown reason, Tifeo never found them. At 1658 A/23, Saetta joined to relieve Legionario which then proceeded alone and reached Palermo at 2325 A/23. At 1733 A/23, Grecale left them and arrived in Trapani at 2010 A/23. At 1902 A/23, Geniere received a signal from Supermarina ordering her to make for Palermo instead of Trapani. At 0055 A/24, off Trapani, they were reinforced by the torpedo boat Clio. At 1004 A/24-1015 A/24, Geniere, Saetta and Clio entered Palermo.
These patrol positions were based on information from ULTRA (codebreaking). [See files; DEFE 3/585 pages 892, 975 and DEFE 3/790 frames 123, 127.] (36)

21 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2307 A/21, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) of the following; One merchant ship, two destroyers and four barges in position 38°21'N, 12°18'E, course 260°, speed 8 knots.

This most probably referred to the Italian transport Etruria (2633 GRT, built 1906) and the German F-lighters F 478, F 482, F 483 and F 484, escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardito (convoy leader) and Angelo Bassini which sailed from Trapani at 1715 A/21-1726 A/21. At 0025 A/22, Ardito obtained a sonar contact in position 38°16'N, 11°49'E, and, ten minutes later, dropped three depth charges. At 0045 A/22, a torpedo wake was sighted and 0100 A/22, nine depth charges were released but, her sonar temporarily out of action, Ardito rejoined the convoy. At 0145 A/22, they came under air attack and opened fire with their machine guns. At 0230 A/22, there was a new air attack and Etruria was hit by a bomb and sank very quickly in position 38°08’ N, 11°36’ E. Angelo Bassini picked up the survivors and was ordered to Trapani where she arrived at 0925 A/22, while Ardito looked for the lighters which had dispersed during the air attack. They were found, and they all returned to Trapani where they arrived at 1031 A/22. (36)
22 Dec 1942 At 0010 A/22, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2307 A/21 [See one of the events for 21 December for the text of this signal.].

At 0026 A/22, an aircraft report was received which stated the reported convoy had changed course to 240°. The chances to intercept this convoy appeared good although with a very low battery [after passing through Q.B.B. 255, the previous day].

At 0242 A/22, HMS P 42 dived in position 37°40'N, 11°10'E, for a torpedo boat which was seen at a range of three miles steering a southerly course. The Capt. S.10 had already warned submarine operating or passing through this area for enemy A/S sweeps. This torpedo boat appeared to be alone.

The was perhaps the Italian Calliope. She had sailed with Sagittario from Trapani at 0720 A/21 for an A/S sweep. At 1418 A/21, Sagittario had to return to Trapani because of a defective sonar. At 0410 A/22, Calliope received the order to look for survivors of Etruria, but at 0515 A/22, she received the order of Marina Trapani to relieve the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini from escorting the four German F-lighters to Bizerte. However, at 1030 A/22, a new order arrived to return to Trapani which she did at 1158 A/22.
Another possibility is the torpedo boat Groppo. She sailed from Trapani at 0600 A/21 for an A/S sweep and arrived in Palermo at 1722 A/22.

At 0337 A/22, HMS P 42 surfaced 8 miles short of the reported convoy's probable line of advance and proceeded on a course normal to it. Speed was slow, as it now became essential to charge heavily due to the depleted battery.
At 0400 A/22, HMS P 42 arrived in the position Lt. Mars had selected and she commenced to patrol to the north and west towards Keith Reef.
At 0625 A/22, red and green very lights were seen to be dropped by aircraft to the southward.
At 0627 A/22, a large aircraft was sighted and HMS P 42 dived in position 37°53'N, 11°01'E.

At 0650 A/22, in position 37°53'N, 11°02'E, four torpedo boats and an aircraft appeared to conduct an A/S sweep but they did not appear to be transmitting on Asdic. After half an hour they disappeared in various directions.

There were no four destroyers or torpedo boats in this area. There was one destroyer and one torpedo boat in this area at this time; 1) The destroyer Granatiere, which had sailed from Palermo at 2200 A/21, carrying troops, and arrived in Bizerte at 1045 A/22. 2) The torpedo boat Groppo which had sailed from Trapani at 0600 A/21 for an A/S sweep and arrived in Palermo at 1722 A/22. We have no precise information on her movements during the period she was at sea.

At 0731 A/22, HMS P 42 commenced to patrol towards the position (37°54'N, 11°17'E) that had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1225 A/21. [See one of the events of 21 December 1942 for the full text of this signal.]

At 1310 A/22, HMS P 42 proceeded in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1153 A/22 which had contained the following orders for HMS P 42; Proceed forthwith through position 38°42'N, 12°44'E to patrol off Naples.
At 1818 A/22, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 38°02'N, 11°25'E and proceeded towards Naples via the route ordered.
At 1854 A/22, in position 38°05'N, 11°30'E, HMS P 42 dived for two destroyers or torpedo boats that were carrying out an A/S sweep while using Asdic. Enemy course was 050° at 16 knots.

Around the same time the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1810 A/22 was received which contained the following orders for all submarines on patrol; Clear out of area within 50 miles of Marettimo with all dispatch.
At 2014 A/22, HMS P 42 surfaced and proceeded on course 320°, away from Marettimo at 10 knots.

At 2020 A/22, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1902 A/22 was received which contained the following information relevant to HMS P 42; At 1730 A/22, one merchant vessel and two destroyers in position 40°23'N, 14°03'E, course 222°, speed 13 knots.
Lt. Mars decided to try to intercept this convoy in position 38°35'N, 11°56'E around 0430 A/23 while keeping about 40 miles away from Marettimo which was the maximum possible under the circumstances.
This signal referred to the Catarina Costa-convoy (see 21 December). (35)

23 Dec 1942 At 0116 A/23, to the north-east of Marettimo, in position 38°31'N, 11°39'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), had to dive for two approaching ships. These were thought to be either a torpedo boat and a motor torpedo boat or two torpedo boats. These two ships passed through the submarine's position from south to north on course 360°. They were not transmitting on Asdic.
Perhaps one of them was the torpedo boat Cigno which sailed from Trapani at 2126 A/22 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1702 A/23.
At 0248 A/23, HMS P 42 surfaced and continued her passage.
At 0430 A/23, HMS P 42 arrived on the expected route of the convoy.
By 0550 A/23, nothing had been seen. The convoy should have been visible from at least 6 miles.
At 0556 A/23, HMS P 42 dived in position 38°48'N, 12°04'E.
At 1100 A/23, the weather deteriorated making constant patrol at periscope depth impossible so P 42 went deep only coming up for W/T routines.
At 1145 A/23, about 30 miles west-north-west of Ustica, in position 38°59'N, 12°15'E, a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen proceeding on course 040° at 12 knots. She was sweeping with her Asdic.
Again, perhaps the torpedo boat Cigno (see above).

At 2100 A/23, about 40 miles north-north-west of Ustica, in position 39°27'N, 12°43'E, HMS P 42 had to dive to avoid two destroyers and another vessel, thought to be a motor torpedo boat, steering 200° at 18 knots. HE of two diesel engines and one turbine engine was picked up. One of the diesel engined ships was pinging. They passed directly over the submarine. On surfacing at 2202 A/23 they were seen laying stopped 2 to 3 miles away on the port quarter.
P 42 dived again and continued her passage surfacing again later in position 39°33'N, 12°41'E.

Perhaps the vessels sighted were some out of the following; The Italian transport Col di Lana (5891 GRT, built 1926) and the German KT 2 (834 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sirio and Perseo. We are however not sure they could have reached this area at the time. KT 2 and Sirio sailed from Naples at 0730 A/23-0800 A/23. Col di Lana was delayed and sailed only at 1036 A/23. At 1300 A/23, they were briefly reinforced by two MAS boats until 1500 A/23. Perseo joined at 1705 A/23. At 0753 A/24, they merged with the Italian transport Viminale (8657 GRT, built 1925) and the Italian destroyer Lampo which had sailed from Palermo at 2025 A/23. They arrived in Tunis at 2305 A/24. (27)

24 Dec 1942 At 0430 A/24, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) received an aircraft report of two destroyers, a motor torpedo boat and a 6000-tons merchant vessel in position 38°45'N, 12°06'E. It appears that this merchant vessel may have passed HMS P 42 on opposite courses during the night. However during the encounter at 2100 A/23 no merchant ship was seen or heard.
This most likely referred to the Col di Lana-convoy. [see 23 December for more info on this convoy].
Around noon, HMS P 42 commenced to patrol keeping within 4 miles of position 217° - Carena Point (Capri) - 14.5 miles.

At 2230 A/24, P 42 heard, and then sighted a medium sized merchant vessel in position 40°20'N, 14°00'E proceeding towards the Bocca Piccola. Enemy course was 040°, speed about 10 knots. The vessel was escorted by a destroyer or torpedo boat. An attack was not possible or only just possible from long range and as the ship was northbound Lt. Mars decided not to do so but to await a better opportunity.

The Italian transport Mario Roselli (6835 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Bombardiere and Camicia Nera sailed from Bizerte at 0630 A/24-0632 A/24 and arrived in Naples at 0317 A/25-0433 A/25. (27)

25 Dec 1942 At 1345 A/25, in position 40°20'N, 14°00'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) heard HE to the eastward. Smoke could be seen proceeding towards the Bocca Piccola. The source of the smoke was never sighted so an attack was not possible.

Possibilities: 1) The Italian transport Emma (7931 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Nicola Fabrizi sailed from La Spezia at 0925 A/24 and arrived in Naples at 1645 A/25. 2) The Italian torpedo boats FR 41 (former French Bombarde), FR 42 (former French La Pomone) and FR 43 (former French L’Iphigéniee) sailed from Palermo at 0615 A/25 and arrived in Naples at 1555 A/25-1600 A/25. Most likely this was Emma although she was coming from the north and not from the south but she may have approached Naples Bay from the south though. (27)

26 Dec 1942 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) attacked an enemy convoy south-west of Ischia Island. Four torpedoes were fired and the German transport Djebel Dira (former French, 2835 GRT, built 1930) was hit and damaged.

Besides the Djebel Dira, the convoy consisted of the German transport Jacques Schiaffino (former French, 1757 GRT, built 1930). The convoy was escorted by the Italian naval auxiliary Cattaro (1275 GRT, 1933), which sailed from Gaeta at 0620 A/26. Jacques Schiaffino and Cattaro arrived in Naples at 2400 A/26. The tug Titano (788 GRT, built 1913) sailed from Gaeta at 0130 A/27 and took the Djebel Dira in tow. They reached Naples at 0635 A/29.
0200 A/26 - Sighted patrol to a position off Imperatore Point, Ischia Island in the hope of intercepting enemy submarines bound for Naples coming from the north.
From dawn HMS P 42 patrolled in the vicinity of position 223° - Imperatore Point - 2.5 miles.

1130 A/26 - In position 40°41'N, 13°47'E, sighted smoke which soon turned out to be a convoy of two medium seized merchant ships, and one small merchant vessel, escorted by an armed merchant cruiser of about 2000 tons. Course of the convoy was 147° at 9 knots. Started attack.
1231 A/26 - In position 40°41'N, 13°47'E fired four torpedoes from yards. One hit was obtained on the leading merchant vessel which was of about 5000 tons. P 42 had gone deep on firing.
1240 A/26 - The counter attack of four depth charges was ineffective. On board P 42 they could hardly believe it, they usually got a much bigger pounding.

1310 A/26 - Returned to periscope depth and observed that the ship hit had about 50 feet of her bow missing and was stopped bow down. The AMC was escorting the other ship towards Naples while the third ship stood by the damaged one. The local schooner patrol had also arrived on the scene.

1430 A/26 - A look through the periscope revealed that a tug had arrived on the scene. The damaged ship had a ten degree down angle by the bow. It was not yet in tow. Wind and sea were increasing and Lt. Mars considered there would be a good chance that the enemy vessel would sink or had to be beached. He therefore decided to clear the area and attack the railway in the vicinity of Cape Palinuro. Set course for the south. (27)

27 Dec 1942 Just after sunset HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) surfaced to engage a rail viaduct over a deep ravine half a mile east of Pietra di Nante (position 40°07.5'N, 15°11'E). In 10 minutes 68 rounds were fired from 1000 yards. Twenty-three hits were observed on the viaduct or its supports. Seven hits were observed on the power house. Overhead wires were also brought down. In fact, the telegraph lines were cut, the bridge slightly damaged and a corporal was wounded.

0130 A/27 - In position 40°08'N, 14°27'E, HMS P 42 passed a lighted hospital ship steering 330°, so probably bound for Naples. It is thought to be the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which must have been correct as this vessel arrived at Naples at 0800 A/27.
0500 A/27 - Dived in latitude 40°10'N, about 15 miles from the coast and closed carrying out a reconnaissance when close in.
Rail traffic seemed light, possibly because it was a Sunday. Only four trains were observed in the afternoon but periscope visibility was very poor and there were probably twice that number.

1600 A/27 - A train was awaited for some time, but again one failed to turn up. P 42 was in a very good position to bombard the vulnerable viaduct. It was decided to take the opportunity before light would fail.

1648 A/27 - Just after sunset, surfaced 600 yards from the beach below the target. In 10 minutes, at a hitting range of 1000 yards, 68 rounds of 3" high explosive were fired without enemy opposition. 23 hits were obtained on the viaduct or its supports and also 7 on the power control house. All hits could be easily distinguished by the cloud of thick red brick dust thrown up. Overhead wires were brought down and electric flashes were seen on the line over the ravine and in the power house.
1658 A/27 - Fire was ceased.
1704 A/27 - Withdrew astern, turned and dived.
1802 A/27 - Surfaced 5 miles off shore and withdrew further from the coast. (27)

31 Dec 1942 At 1130 A/31, south of the Bocco Picolla, in position 40°26'N, 14°21'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sighted masts and smoke of a small merchant vessel through the Bocca Piccola in the Gulf of Naples. This vessel disappeared behind Capanella Point.
At 1755 A/31, HMS P 42 surfaced and left the Naples area to attack a train in the Gulf of Policastro.

At 2100 A/31, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1830 A/31 which contained the following; Happy birthday to you [Lt. Mars his birthday was on January, 1st]. Leave patrol tonight passing through position 39°56'N, 14°20'E thence direct to position 'G' '(38°00'N, 11°25'E) so as to arrive in position 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) at dawn/3 and make rendezvous with the escort at 0801 A/4. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) will not be relieving you in your sector. HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN) has freedom of action in the sector vacated by you from 0600 A/1.

Lt. Mars decided that a no train had been demolished yet to go ahead with the train wrecking mission as the place also did not interfere with friendly units. It was accepted that P 42 would arrive at Malta 24 hours late. (35)

1 Jan 1943 At 0502 A/1, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in estimated position 39°53'N, 15°29'E and closed the coast to reconnoitre the selected spot close to Dino Island. Visibility however was very bad, frequently even under half a mile due to heavy rain squalls. Lt. Mars decided to abandon the attempt to bombard a train.
At 1200 A/1, HMS P 42 set course for Malta, diving deep by day on account of the bad weather conditions which made constant periscope depth patrol impossible.

At 1800 A/1, HMS P 42 passed her signal timed 1721 A/1 to the Capt. S.10. This signal contained the following; Your l830A/31, thank you. P 42 delayed 24 hours. Noon 26th, one hit on 5000 ton ship 4 miles off Imperatore Point, probably sank. 30 hits on brick rail viaduct vicinity of 40°07'N. Four torpedoes left. (27)
2 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1045 A/2, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN; Your 1721 A/1 acknowledged, well done.

In a signal timed 1512 A/2, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 42 of the following; HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) may be met during next 24 hours on passage from position 'G' to a waiting position 15 miles north-north-west of Ustica. (37)

3 Jan 1943 At 0930 A/3, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0810 A/3, ordering her to patrol in position 38°04'N, 11°46'E (west-north-west of Marettimo) by 1600 A/3. Course and speed was adjusted to comply with these orders.
In a signal timed 1445 A/3, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 42 of the following; Three merchant ships and one destroyer at 1100 A/3 in position 37°29'N, 10°24'E, course 065°, speed 14 knots.
In a signal timed 2152 A/3, the Capt. S.10 cancelled his signal timed 1445 A/3 and ordered HMS P 42 to continue her passage to Malta. (38)

4 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 0117 A/4, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sent the following to the Capt. S.10; Your 2152 A/3. Intend waiting near position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) for the north-west gale to abate, sea 64. Expect me 0800 A/6 or or 0800 A/7.

At 1740 A/4, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 'G' and proceeded through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) to the east and north of the usual route as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0757 A/4. [for the text of this signal see the other event for 4 January 1943.] (38)
