4 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 0757 A/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN); Owing to delay of P 42 you will both be coming through Q.B.B. 255 together. P 42 keep east and Thunderbolt west of the normal route. (37)
6 Jan 1943 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (also 10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined at 1020 A/6. (27)
16 Jan 1943 At 1300 A/16, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (also 11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Gabes west of 11°30'E.
She was to pass through positions 36°05'N, 14°00'E and 36°05'N, 12°30'E.

17 Jan 1943 At 0107 A/17, in position 36°02'N, 13°21'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived for a submarine zig-zagging on an opposite mean course. This was presumed to be HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN).
HMS P 35 reported nothing at this time though. She did had altered course before to get of <>P 35's route but apparently this was thus not successful. (27)
18 Jan 1943 At 0635 A/18, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 34°11'N, 11°40'E and closed Ras Turgeuness (Taguermess) which was some 35 miles away.
At 1637 A/18, two distant explosions were heard.
At 1810 A/18, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 33°57'N, 11°21'E and sighted Ras Turgeuness lighthouse bearing 250° at a range of approximately 15 miles.

At 1820 A/18, in position 33°57’N, 11°21’E, smoke was sighted bearing 266° which was drawing to the southward. This was assumed to be a small ship previously reported by an aircraft. Set course to intercept at 10 knots.
At 1923 A/18, a small vessel was seen bearing 239° at a range of 4 miles. Only the masts were visible. Course of the vessel was about 150°.

At 1924 A/18, HMS P 42 dived for a torpedo boat which was seen approaching on bearing 160°. This torpedo boat was transmitting on Asdic. Evasive action was taken and the prey was thus lost. The torpedo boat closed to about about 1 mile and hunted for an hour with Asdic, alternated by listening. After an hour the torpedo boat make off at speed in a direction of 280°.
The vessel encountered was most probably the Italian submarine chaser Oriole which sailed from Sfax at 1300 A/18 and arrived in Tripoli at 1645 A/19.
At 2052 A/18, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 33°54'N, 11°14'E and patrolled up and down moon. (27)
19 Jan 1943 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Edda (6107 GRT, built 1924) near Djerba, Tunisia in position 33°45'N, 11°12'E.

Edda, escorted by the Italian torpedo boat San Martino and the Italian submarine chaser Eso sailed from Tripoli at 0020 A/19. At 0845 A/19, off Zuara, they joined up with the Italian tug Ciclope (840 GRT, built 1903) which was towing two lighters and was followed by a fishing vessel (Michelino). At 0917 A/19, they sighted the submarine chaser Oriole which was travelling on the opposite direction (See 18 January 1943). At 1752 A/19, Edda was hit by a torpedo astern in position 33°42.5'N, 11°14'E. San Martino ordered Eso to hunt the submarine while the torpedo boat dropped six depth charges for intimidation purposes. The crew had abandoned ship but the vessel was still afloat and they were ordered back. At 2130 A/19, Ciclope took Edda in tow while Eso did the same for the two lighters. At 2201 A/19, they came under air attack and by 2309 A/19, San Martino had avoided in all four torpedoes while she was covering Edda with smoke but Eso had been hit by a torpedo at 2142 A/19 and sank. Her survivors were picked up by Michelino and were later transferred to the torpedo boat. At 2245 A/19, Edda was hit by two torpedoes and capsized at 2310 A/19. At 1429 A/20, San Martino arrived at Sfax.
0621 A/19 - Dived in position 33°53'N, 11°17'E and closed the coast.
1300 A/19 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat proceeding from the south-east to the north-west keeping about 3.5 miles from the shore.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Lince which had sailed from Tripoli at 2035 A/18 and arrived in Sfax at 1835 A/19.

1410 A/19 [or 1450 A/19, two different times are mentioned in the patrol report] - In position 33°50'N, 11°06'E a three-masted schooner passed from the north-west to the south-east very close inshore.
1500 A/19 - Sighted smoke bearing 187°. This bearing grew to the westward, and the ship, which must have been quite small, passed very close inshore without being sighted.

1650 A/19 - Masts and smoke was visible bearing 150°. This was soon seen to be a convoy made up of a 5000 ton merchant ship and three schooners escorted by two escorts. Enemy course was 330°. Started attack.

1748 A/19 - In position 33°45'N, 11°12'E fired four torpedoes from yards. One hit was obtained. The counter attack was slight and started 10 minutes after firing. In 12 minutes 7 single depth charges were dropped but none were close.
1808 A/19 - A periscope look showed both escorts, one hunting and one standing by the damaged ship which was in a sinking condition with her stern down.
1814 A/19 - The escorts could still be seen but no transport. Went to 70 feet as a torpedo-boat was closing. Withdrew to the seaward.
2020 A/19 - Completed reloading the torpedo tubes.
2200 A/19 - Surfaced in position 34°42'N, 11°26'E and patrolled within 8 miles from this position. (39)
20 Jan 1943 At 0500 A/20, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 33°37'N, 11°28'E and closed the coast.
At 0711 A/20, in position 33°28'N, 11°24'E, a three masted schooner was sighted proceeding on course 120°.
At 1600 A/20, in position 33°28'N, 11°23'E, six F-boats were seen proceeding to the north-west at 9 knots.
These were most likely the Germans F 350, F 362, F 541 and the Italians MZ 708, MZ 726 and MZ 734 on passage from Tripoli to Zarzis.

At 1800 A/20, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 33°31'N, 11°25'E and set course for Ras Salakta to the north of the Kerkennah Bank. This in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1103 A/20. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 20 January 1943.]

At 1911 A/20, P 42 passed her signal timed 1746 A/20 to the Capt. S.10 which contained the following; At 1600 A/20 sighted 6 F-boats in position 33°28'N, 11°24'E, course 300°, 9 knots. At 1749 A/19th one hit on northbound 5000 ton transport believed sunk in position 33°45'N, 11°12'E. Am moving to the vicinity of position 35°22'N, 11°10'E in compliance with your 1103 A/20. (40)

20 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1103 A/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Establish patrol forthwith west of line 340° from Kerkennah as follows; 1) P 42 south of latitude 35°30'N. 2) P 45 north of P 42 and south of latitude 36°00'N. 3) P 46 north of P 45. (37)
21 Jan 1943 At 0645 A/21, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 35°19'N, 11°17'E (south-east of Ras Salakta) and closed the coast.
At 0800 A/21, HMS P 42 commenced to patrol about 4.5 miles from the shore near Ras Salakta.

At 1330 A/21, in position 35°27'N, 11°06'E, a Palestro-class torpedo boat passed northbound (course 010° at 20 knots) about 1.5 miles from the shore. She was sweeping on Asdic and slowed down once to listen.

This was the Italian torpedo boat San Martino which sailed from Sfax at 0530 A/21. She came under air attack at 1550 A/21 by four torpedo bombers but came out unscathed. She arrived off Sousse at 1650 A/21 and waited for permission to enter. She finally took her berth at 0020 A/22.

At 1828 A/21, HMS P 42 surfaced in position 35°22'N, 11°07'E and set course for Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1116 A/21. [For the text of this signal see the other event for 21 January 1943.] (27)

21 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1116 A/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); P 42 leave patrol after dark tonight and return to Malta through positions 35°01'N, 12°20'E and 35°02'N, 14°10'E to make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 0830 A/23. P 45 move forthwith north of latitude 36°00'N, keeping south of 36°15'N. P 46 keep north of 36°15'N. Three motor torpedo boats from Malta will be operating off Tunisian the coast between 35°00'N and 36°00'N. They are routed north of Linosa and have orders not to attack submarines.
The motor torpedo boats referred to in this signal were HMMTB 264 and HMMTB 307 (only two sailed instead of three). (37)
23 Jan 1943 At 1030 A/23, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (also 11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0815 A/23. (27)

25 Jan 1943 At 1600 A/25, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (also 12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to conduct special operation Felicity (or Felice) in the Gulf of Hammamet.
27 Jan 1943 At 0614 A/27, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 36°08'N, 10°37'E, for a small vessel sighted 210°.
At 0720 A/27, in position 36°14'N, 10°39'E three small tugs or trawlers were seen proceeding northwards keeping close inshore. These were later seen anchored in Hammamet roads.

In the afternoon a periscope reconnaissance of the coast between Hammamet at Bi'r al Buwaytah was carried out and a position for landing the commandos was established. On completion of the reconnaissance HMS P 42 retired to the centre of the Gulf of Hammamet for the night. (27)

28 Jan 1943 HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) landed a raiding party (Captain J. Eyre, Lieutenant P.H. Thomas of 4th battalion, the Bluffs, and six Fighting French commandos) that was to destroy a railway bridge near the town of Hammamet. The party was landed successfully but flares fired and gunfire on the shore made it obvious that they had been discovered. HMS P 42 wisely retired from the area. The commandos were all captured.
0600 A/28 - Dived and closed the coast.
1600 A/28 - Conducted a periscope reconnaissance of the landing position.
1730 A/28 - Obtained a firm fix 17 cables from the landing position and then retired from the coast.
1832 A/28 - Commenced to close the coast for the landing operation.
1847 A/28 - Surfaced in position 196° - Hammament flagstaff - 27 cables and closed the coast while getting the Folbots and equipment on the casing.
1901 A/28 - All gear was on the deck.
1912 A/28 - In the selected position for the landing operation.
1930 A/28 - The Folbots left the submarine.
1945 A/28 - Withdrew astern as it was now estimated that the boats had landed. P 42 took up a position, head to the sea, 10 cables from the landing position.

1958 A/28 - Three tracer shots were fired in the air from Hammament, 20 cables from the landing position and 17 cables from P 42. It is consided that these shots were just the clearing of a light AA gun and were not connected with the landing operation.
2032 A/28 - Three small explosions and a flash were seen in the rough direction of the landing position. It was thought these were small land mines or some grenades with which the party was armed.

2034 A/28 - Night lights and flares were fired from the same direction. These lit up the beach and Lt. Mars thought he could see the Folbots laying on it. P 42 was also illuminated but probably not enough for detection. However the alarm ashore had clearly been given and it was obvious the party would not be able to return so Lt. Mars decided to withdraw.
2036 A/28 - P 42 withdrew on course 156° at full speed.
2040 A/28 - P 42 fired starshell towards Hammamet to indicate her retreat to the landing party. This was a pre-arranged signal.
2050 A/28 - The enemy continued to fire flares from the same spot at irregular intervals.
2126 A/28 - P 42 dived in position 36°14'N, 10°40'E for a small craft that was seen to be approaching. This vessel was proceeding northwards.
2140 A/28 - Fast motor HE was picked up from three vessels. These were considered to be F-boats, thought to be northbound from Sousse. Could see nothing though.

Two possibilities; 1) The Italian transport Volta (1189 GRT, built 1919) escorted by VAS 202 and VAS 203 which had sailed from Sousse at 1815 A/28 and arrived in Pantelleria at 0615 A/29, departing immediately for Trapani where they arrived at 1515 A/29. 2) The Italian F boats MZ 708, MZ 726 and MZ 734 which had sailed from Sfax at 1700 A/27 and arrived in Pantelleria at 1500 A/29. They sailed from Pantelleria at 2400 A/29 and arrived in Trapani at 0930 A/30.

Given what is stated in the patrol report that the enemy were F boats the latter (the three MZ boats) were most likely the vessels heard. Given their low silhouette they were also difficult to make out.
2241 A/28 - Surfaced and set course for Malta by the reverse of the outward route.
2244 A/28 - A ten-minute show of flares was observed from the same direction as before. It seems likely that the landing party had been detected and did not accomplish its mission. (27)
30 Jan 1943 At 1110 A/30, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (also 12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (27)

12 Feb 1943 At 1500 A/12, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (also 13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kuriat and Hammamet passing to the south of Lampedusa en-route to her patrol area.
16 Feb 1943 At 0557 A/16, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 36°02'N, 10°42'E, and proceeded to patrol off Sousse.

Lt. Mars had deemed it probable that some ships of the convoy reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0355 A/16 may proceed to Sousse. He therefore had decided to patrol 5 miles from Sousse during daylight.

The signal timed 0355 A/16, had contained the following information; Aircraft sighted at 0245 A/16, three merchant ships and six destroyers 20 miles to the north of Pantellaria proceeding on course 190° at 10 knots.

The reported convoy consisted of the Italian transports Frosinone (former French Congo, 5202 GRT, built 1914), Chieti (former French Arizona, 5457 GRT, built 1925), Alcamo (former French St. François, former British Hermes, 6987 GRT, built 1916) and the German tanker Labor (510 GRT, built 1930), escorted by the Italian destroyer Augusto Riboty, the Italian torpedo boats Sirio (escort leader), Monsone and the Italian corvette Antilope. They had sailed from Palermo at 0930 A/15-1130 A/15. At 0540 A/15, the Italian corvette Gabbiano sailed from Trapani to join the convoy off Palermo. At 1925 A/15, Augusto Riboty was detached to Trapani due to boiler troubles. She arrived there at 2015 A/15.

At 2328 A/15, the convoy was attacked by three MTB's (actually five were involved, this were HMMTB 61, HMMTB 77, HMMTB 82, HMMTB 307 and HMMTB 315) without result. Frosinone had difficulty maintaining her station due to a drop in boiler pressure, and the convoy had to slow down to allow her to keep up with them. At 0050 A/16, the torpedo boat Clio joined the convoy in place of Augusto Riboty. She had sailed from Trapani at 2150 A/15. At 0130 A/16, the convoy received the order from Supermarina to proceed to Pantelleria. At 0137 A/16 and 0325 A/16, the convoy came under air attack, but all the bombs were avoided. At 0523 A/16, Pantelleria was sighted and course was altered to go through 37°26'N, 11°58'E. At 1050 A/16, Gabbiano and Antilope were detached to return to Trapani, and three motor torpedo boats joined the escort. The two corvettes reached Trapani at 1715 A/16-1820 A/16. At 1640 A/16, Frosinone was still having difficulties in keeping her station and could only do 5 knots. She was left behind with the torpedo boat Clio for escort. The convoy proceeded to Bizerte at 7.5 knots. At 1838 A/16, Chieti reported that her steering gear had broken down. She was left with the torpedo boat Monsone to stand by her. Alcamo and Labor escorted by Sirio continued to their destination, However, at 1855 A/16, Chioti reported her steering gear repaired and, with Monsone, reunited with the convoy. They arrived in Bizerte at 2315 A/16-0155 A/17.
At 0900 A/16, In position 35°55'N, 10°39'E, two motor torpedo boats passed. They were southbound for Sousse.

We can not positively identify these motor torpedo boats, At 2140 A/15, MAS 530 and MAS 532 had sailed from Pantelleria to protect a convoy (presumably the Frosinone-convoy ?) but turned back due to the bad weather and reached Pantelleria at 0225 A/16. Source is Marina Pantelleria, however the Supermarina Diaries state that they had sailed for Sousse, this is possibly an error. Supermarina Diaries also state that the submarine chasers A/S 203 and A/S 216 sailed from Pantelleria (date & time not given) and arrived in Sousse at 0830 A/16 [the time does not match with the sighting by HMS Unbroken but could be an error]. However, Marina Pantelleria makes no mention of their presence and departure.

At 1100 A/16, in position 35°55'N, 10°42'E, two masts were seen, thought to be of either a schooner or small merchant vessel very close inshore. These were presumably from a wreck as no movement at all was apparent.
At 1840 A/16, HMS Unbroken surfaced in position 35°56'N, 10°47'E and patrolled to the north of Sousse. Nothing was however sighted of the convoy reported in the morning. (41)
17 Feb 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) inspected the coast between the northern limit of her patrol area (36°10'N) and Sousse. Only two wrecks were seen.
Also a periscope reconnaissance of Sousse harbour was carried out. Two ships were present of which only masts and the funnel tops could be seen.

At 1800 A/17, in position 35°51'N, 10°48'E, a small convoy passed HMS Unbroken two miles to the north-east steering 335° to 345° at 7 knots. The convoy consisted of six F-boats, a motor torpedo boat and a schooner of about 100 tons. Torpedoes could not be fired as they had to run shallow which was not possible in the existing sea conditions. Gun action was not attempted given the combined firepower of the ships of the convoy.

The F-boats were the Italian MZ 714, MZ 724, MZ 733, MZ 739, MZ 751 and MZ 753 which had sailed from Sfax at 0900 A/16 and arrived in Monastir at 1330 A/17 and sailed from there at 1630 A/17 for Pantelleria. MZ 714 was lost in bad weather and MZ 739's engine broke down and had to be beached 3 miles north of Kelibia. The remainder reached Pantelleria at 2400 A/18. [We have been unable to identify the other two vessels.]

At 1846 A/17, HMS Unbroken surfaced and patrolled to the north of Sousse to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1535 A/17. In this signal HMS Unbroken was ordered to cover the approaches to this port as much as possible. (27)
18 Feb 1943 At 0300 A/18, in position 36°04'N, 10°44'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) encountered a convoy of five F-boats which were steering an approximate course of 200° to 250°.

The following units were in the area around this time; The German, F 349, F 350, F 361, F 541, F 542 and F 546 had departed Sousse around 2200 A/17 for Trapani via Pantelleria. They arrived at Pantelleria around 1500 A/18. F 349 grounded an entering and was heavily damaged. The Italian, MZ 713, MZ 720, MZ 748, MZ 754 and MZ 760 had departed Sfax around 1100 A/17 for Sicily. None of these were proceeding to the south-west though, but it is always possible they proceeded in that direction temporarily.
During the day, HMS Unbroken patrolled the approaches to Sousse, keeping 4 to 5 miles from the shore.
After dark she patrol the northern approaches to Sousse, about 12 miles from the coast. (27)

22 Feb 1943 During the forenoon HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) closed Sousse to a range of 4 miles. A pericope inspection of the harbour revealed one ship of about 3000 tons loading or unloading at the north quay and another ship of some 5000 tons at the east quay. The second ship appeared very low in the water. The smaller vessel was making smoke as though departure was imminent.
At 1857 A/22, HMS Unbroken surfaced to the north-east of Sousse, in position 35°52'N, 10°43'E, and patrolled about 5 miles from Sousse keeping listening watch with the engines stopped.
At 2020 A/22, HMS Unbroken moved further away from the coast and patrolled about 8 miles off due to the light (moon) conditions. (27)
24 Feb 1943 At 1844 A/24, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) surfaced in position 35°51'N, 10°44'E and proceeded to a new patrol position to the north-east of Mahedia. (27)
25 Feb 1943 At 0001 A/25, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) commenced patrol about 10 miles to the north-east of Mahedia.
At 0556 A/25, HMS Unbroken dived in position 35°36'N, 11°07'E and patrolled in the vicinity of Mahedia some 3.5 miles from the shore.

At 1845 A/25, HMS Unbroken surfaved and left patrol in compliance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1220 A/24 which had contained the following orders; Leave for patrol after dark on the 25th by by reverse of the outward route to arrive at Malta at 0830 A/27. (27)
27 Feb 1943 At 1040 A/27, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (also 13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. Only small vessels were sighted.
She was escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 0830 A/27. (27)
11 Mar 1943 At 1500 A/11, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (also 14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Hammamet.
En-route to her patrol area she was to pass to the south of Lampedusa.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unbroken during this patrol see the map below.
12 Mar 1943 At 0537 A/12, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 35°45'N, 12°32'E, in order to intercept any ship bound for Lampedusa.
At 1400 A/12, received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1227 A/12. This signal ordered her to concentrate her patrol off Sousse.
At 1853 A/12, HMS Unbroken surfaced in position 35°25'N, 12°24'E and continued her passage to her patrol area. (42)

15 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1147 A/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) and HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN); You have freedom of action in your respective areas. (43)

17 Mar 1943 At 0840 A/17, when about 4 miles to the north of Sousse, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sighted a small tug coming towards from the harbour entrance. She was later seen to be in company with an F-boat. Lt. Mars started an attack with the intention of torpedoing the F-boat and sinking the tug with gunfire. However the attack was frustrated when both stopped about a mile off and turned away. Before HMS Unbroken could close the tug laid a buoy following both vessels returned to harbour.

The vessels sighted were the tug Neptun 3 and the landing barge L 211. The tug had been tasked to reposition a buoy. Being covered by L 211 which had just before been equipped with a gun taken from a shore battery.

A periscope reconnaissance of the harbour showed a merchant ship of about 3000 tons. This is thought to be the same vessel as had been seen on the previous patrol but now in a different position. This vessels 'smoked' every evening. A vessel of about 1000 tons was seen heeled over apparently sunk. Two smaller vessels, probably tugs, which appeared intact. It is thought that the larger vessel seen on last patrol was still there but probably a complete wreck.
The German transport Skotfoss (former Norwegian, 1465 GRT, built 1917) and the Italian transport Orsolina Bottiglieri (883 GRT, built 1899) are known to have been in the harbour.

In the afternoon the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1245 A/17 was received which ordered HMS Unbroken to leave patrol and return to Malta. [for the text of this signal see the other event for 17 March 1943]. Lt. Mars decided, since the Gulf of Hammamet was now to be temporarily vacated by submarines to fire a torpedo at the shipping in the harbour.
At 1720 A/17, HMS Unbroken closed the entrance of the harbour.
At 1732 A/17, a Mk. II torpedo was fired into the harbour. The torpedo was set for surface running. After running for about 5400 yards the torpedo was seen to explode in the inner harbour entrance.

It is considered the torpedo hit the boom. No enemy activity followed although the explosion must have been seen. [The is no mention at all of a torpedo exploding in the harbour entrance in the KTB Seetransportstelle Sousse !!!]
At 1916 A/17, HMS Unbroken surfaced in position 35°44'N, 10°44'E and set course for Malta passing to the south of Lampedusa. (44)
19 Mar 1943 Around 1200 A/19, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (also 14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (27)

26 Mar 1943 At 1500 A/26, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (also 15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'Q', off the south coast of Italy between the southern entrance to the Straits of Messina and Crotone.
En-route to her patrol area she was to proceed through positions 35°10'N, 15°40'E, 38°15'N, 17°23'E.
28 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1119 A/28, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ; After 0001 A/30, include area 'M' in your patrol area. (45)
31 Mar 1943 At 0230 A/31, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) closed the coast near Punta Alice.
At 0430 A/31, HMS Unbroken dived in position 39°21'N, 17°24'E.
At 0800 A/31, HMS Unbroken was about 9 miles south-east of Punta Alice. She then patrolled to the southwards keeping some 5.5 miles out from the shore.
At 0937 A/31, in position 39°15'N, 17°12'E, two two-masted schooners were seen minesweeping north from Crotone about 3.5 miles from the shore.
At 1200 A/31, in position 39°10'N, 17°14'E, a two-masted auxiliary schooner was observed to be on A/S patrol some 8 to 10 miles to the eastward of Crotone.
At 1430 A/31, the harbour of Crotone was observed from about 5.5 miles off. No shipping of any size (over 1000 tons) was seen. The chemical (nitrate) factory seemed more active than ever.

At 1928 A/31, HMS Unbroken surfaced in position 38°57'N, 17°21'E. In a signal timed 1748 A/31, the Capt. S.10 reported the following; Aircraft reports one small unidentified vessel and one escort vessel in position 220° - Cape Spartivento - 3 miles, course 090°, 6 to 7 knots at 1415 A/31.

At 2252 A/31, in position 38°52'N, 17°38'E, a southbound ship was seen to seaward. It was at first thought to be a submarine. An attack was started. It was subsequently seen that the silhouette was too large for a submarine and that the enemy was almost certainly a destroyer or torpedo boat. Course of the enemy was approximately 210°. Speed must have been over 15 knots.
This was most probably the Italian destroyer Antonio Pigafetta which had sailed from Taranto at 1806 B/31 and arrived in Messina at 0821 A/1.
At 2315 A/31, the enemy vessel was lost from sight. (46)
2 Apr 1943 At 0431 A/2, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived in position 38°00'N, 16°22'E, and close the coast to patrol in position 070° - Cape Spartivento - 9 miles.
At 1400 A/2, in position 37°57'N, 16°15'E, a large motor torpedo boat passed the submarine at a range of one mile on course 235° at a speed of 14 knots. The enemy was using Asdic.
Perhaps this was the Italian VAS 227 which sailed from Messina at 0510 B/2 for a patrol and returned at 2010 B/2 [no clue where she patrolled exactly].
At 1957 A/2, HMS Unbroken surfaced in position 37°48'N, 16°13'E and moved away from the coast. The weather had meanwhile deteriorated.

At 2012 A/2, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1427 A/2 was received which contained the following information; Aircraft sighting at 1125 A/2, one merchant ship and one escort vessel in position 000° - Cape Colonne - 1 mile, course south, speed 5 knots.
Lt. Mars decided to try to intercept this convoy off Cape dell'Armi after daybreak. Course was set accordingly.

At 2020 A/2, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1911 A/2 was received which started that aircraft would be co-operating the the submarine tonight between 2130 and 0200 hours. The ship was too far away and also the weather conditions were unfavourable for this to be off much value. No enemy reports were picked up during the night and no flares were seen. Later it became known that the aircraft had returned to Malta without dropping flares due to the weather conditions.)
The merchant ship referred to may have been the Italian transport Gimma (1542 GRT, built 1897) escorted by VAS 225 and MZ 776, which sailed from Crotone at 1150 B/2 for Reggio Calabria. (47)

3 Apr 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) attacked the German destroyer ZG 3 / Hermes (former Greek Vasilefs Georgios) with torpedoes about 20 nautical miles south of Cape Spartivento in position 37°46'N, 15°38'E. Unfortunately, the torpedoes miss the target.

The German destroyer was unaware of the attack. There is no mention at all in her logbook. Not even a mention of the explosion heard by HMS Unbroken. At the time of the attack Hermes had an air escort of three German fighters.
The German destroyer was on passage from Salamis to Naples.
0405 A/3 - Dived in position 37°41'N, 15°47'E and closed the coast to maintail a patrol in position 160° - Cape dell'Armi - 9 miles.
1405 A/3 - Heard good HE bearing 110°. Nothing in sight although visibility was maximum.
1415 A/3 - Sighted masts of a warship on the bearing of the HE. Started attack. Enemy course was 262°. The target was thought to be an Italian Regolo-class light cruiser.
1501 A/3 - In position 37°48'N, 15°48'E fired a salvo of four torpedoes from long range, 6000 yards. No hits were obtained.
1505 A/3 - A periscope observation showed two fast aircraft bombing the torpedo tracks with smoke bombs. The aircraft had not been seen during the attack.
1507 A/3 - The target was seen, still on the same course. Went to 90 feet.
1510 A/3 - Heard a heavy explosion, not close. This was either a torpedo exploding or a large depth charge being dropped.
1512 A/3 - HE of the target started faded out and eight minutes later nothing could be heard anymore.
1530 A/3 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight but ten minutes later aircraft were searching overhead. Unbroken went to 85 feet.
1818 A/3 - Returned to periscope depth. All clear.
1920 A/3 - Surfaced in position 37°36'N, 15°47'E and made off to the south while charging heavily.
2010 A/3 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1824 A/3 which stated that aircraft would be cooperating in the area tonight.

2330 A/3 - Passed the signal timed 2101 A/3 to the Capt. S.10. This signal contained the following; At 1501 A# attacked one presumed Regolo-class cruiser in position 37°48'N, 15°38'E, course 347°. One heavy explosion but result unknown. (27)

4 Apr 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) torpedoed and damaged the German (former Norwegian) tanker Regina (9545 GRT, built 1937) off Punta Stilo, Sicily, Italy in position 38°15'N, 16°30'E.

The tanker Regina and the transport Carbonella A. (1593 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Climene (escort leader), Angelo Bassini, Antonio Mosto and the Italian destroyer Augusto Riboty, sailed from Brindisi at 1908 B/3-1936 B/3 for Messina.

At 0542 B/4, in position 39°18'N, 18°03'E, Antonio Mosto signalled that she could not maintain this course in the rough weather and had to alter course to 290° and left the convoy. She entered Taranto at 1845 B/4. At 1542 B/4, in position 38°13'N, 16°29'E, Regina was hit by two torpedoes and stopped. Some of her crew abandoned ship, and Angelo Bassini picked up eight survivors while Climene hunted the submarine. At 1550 B/4, she obtained a good echo on her sonar and dropped nine depth charges. In the meantime, Carbonella A. escorted by Augusto Riboty proceeded to Messina, arriving at 2226 B/4. Regina managed to start her engines and proceeded toward the coast. At 1702 B/4, Angelo Bassini signalled that she had a defective condenser of the forward engine and urgently needed to reach Crotone. She reached Crotone at 2358 B/4. Only Climene remained near Regina, and at 1740 B/4, she obtained a good echo at 1000 metres and, two minutes later, dropped nine depth charges set to depths from 70 to 90 metres. This was followed by eleven depth charges at 1801 B/4 set from 90 to 120 metres. At 1820 B/4, Climene left the scene, believing to have sunk the submarine and rejoined the stricken tanker. At 0046 B/, due to the rough weather, Regina dropped anchor in the bay west of Cape Rizzuto. Climene patrolled outside the anchorage to protect the tanker, and at 0645 B/5, they sailed again for Taranto. At 0915 B/5, they were reinforced by the torpedo boat Cigno which had sailed from Taranto at 0126 B/5. They arrived in Taranto at 2207 B/5.
The tug Vigoroso was sailed to assist her but the tanker had to be beached near Punta Stilo. The tug in the end never joined the tanker.
0325 A/4 - In position 37°52'N, 16°25'E, sighted a small craft thought to be a motor torpedo boat overtaking, possibly with the object of firing a torpedo.
0327 A/4 - Turned stern on and dived deep. Commenced to close the coast.
0413 A/4 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing was in sight except the land.
1415 A/4 - Heard A/S impulses. Diving stations were ordered and the boat prepared for depth charging.
1428 A/4 - By now it was obvious that several ships were in the vicinity, so Unbroken went to periscope depth in order to have a look at the opposition.

1429 A/4 - In a 10-second look through the small periscope. Lt. Mars was rather surprised to see two torpedo-boats on the starboard quarter and one on the port, three aircraft and right astern a very large tanker. Started attack.
1434 A/4 - Fired four torpedoes from 2500 yards. A torpedo explosion was heard 2m 24s after firing the first torpedo.
1436 A/4 - The counter attack started. The first depth charges were fairly close.
1457 A/4 - The counter attack was over. 25 depth charges in all had been dropped.
1530 A/4 - All HE had faded.
1554 A/4 - At periscope depth. Five aircraft were seen patrolling in the area of the attack. No surface craft were seen but the state of the sea made observation difficult.
1620 A/4 - Heard a heavy and prolonged explosion.
1630 A/4 - Made off at 80 feet at a speed of 5 knots.
1641 A/4 - A group of distant depth charges was heard to explode.
1702 A/4 - A larger group of depth charges, eight were counted exploded. These seemed closer.
1836 A/4 - At periscope depth. Visibility was low.
1910 A/4 - Surfaced in position 38°10'N, 16°45'E. Commenced charging.

1936 A/4 - Passed HMS Unbroken's signal timed 1921 A/4 which contained the following; At 1433 A/4 attacked a large southbound escorted tanker in position 38°15'N, 16°30'E, estimated one hit. Expect me by reverse of outward route arriving at the rendezvous at 0800 A/8.

1944 A/4 - Passed HMS Unbroken's signal (amplifying report) timed 1931 A/4 which contained the following; Indications are that ship in my 1921 A/4 is either stopped, in tow, or beached north of Cape Spartivento.
Set course for Malta at 10 knots. Later slowed down to charge the batteries well clear of the position in which the signals were transmitted. (27)

5 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1946 B/5, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) sent the following information to the Capt. S.10; My position 36°57'N, 16°29'E, course 208°. Will now make rendezvous at 0900 B/7. (48)
7 Apr 1943 At 0930 A/7, HMS Unbroken (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (also 15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined around 0730 A/7. (27)
18 Apr 1943 At 1700 B/18, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (also 16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Sicily.

18 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1228 A/18, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN); After passing position 'P' at 1500 A/20 proceed on course 35° to patrol in the southern half of of zone 'D'. You will be ordered to zone 'A' when HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) is clear. (49)
21 Apr 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) attacked an 'Italian submarine' with four torpedoes off Cape San Vito. No hits were obtained.

There were no submarines in this area, axis or allied, the attack was a bogus one. Perhaps Lt. Andrew sighted a landing craft of which the bridge sometimes could be mistaken for a submarine conning tower. However, we can't find any landing craft in the area either.
1052 B/21 - After having been deep for a while due to patrolling aircraft returned to periscope depth. Sighted a submarine proceeding close inshore round Cape San Vito. Started attack.
1100 B/21 - In position 38°15'N, 12°43'E fired four torpedoes at an Italian submarine from 5800 yards. No hits were obtained.
1230 B/21 - The submarine disappeared from sight round Cape Gallo.
1600 B/21 - Withdrew to the northward.
1611 B/21 - Sighted a small fast vessel making smoke, steering west and close inshore. This was considered to be a destroyer or torpedo boat.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
2045 B/21 - Surfaced. (27)

22 Apr 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian auxiliary minesweeper No. 17 / Milano (379 GRT, built 1919) off Cape San Vito, Sicily, Italy in position 38°11'N, 12°45'E. The torpedo missed but, while avoiding it, the minesweeper ran aground accidentally.
0322 B/22 - Dived to the north-north-east of Cape San Vito in position 38°30'N, 12°52'E for two dark objects.

0333 B/22 - Surfaced as these objects could not be seen through the periscope. Closed to attack but identified the objects as two destroyers / torpedo boats proceeding in line ahead on a course of approximately 255°. Dived.
0341 B/22 - Set course for the north-east.
0433 B/22 - Surfaced and turned to the south to close the coast.
0532 B/22 - Dived for the day.
1005 B/22 - In position 38°12'N, 12°45'E, sighted a large schooner steering east round Cape San Vito. This vessel later apparently anchored off Solanto Point just to the east of Cape San Vito.

1327 B/22 - While in position 38°14'N, 12°35'E, set course to close two small vessels close inshore steering 265°. These turned out to be two Siebel Ferries and a small lighter all heavily laden and well armed.
These were most probably the German landing vessels SF 94, SF 213 and L 61. They were on passage from Reggio Calabria to Marsala where they arrived around 2200 B/22.
1411 B/22 - Set course to close a large schooner seen during the morning.
1600 B/22 - In position 38°10'N, 12°46'E fired one torpedo from 900 yards. The torpedo hit and the target disintegrated. Unbroken then retired to the north.
2104 B/22 - Surfaced.
2158 B/22 - Set course for the eastern end of the patrol area. (50)
23 Apr 1943 At 0525 B/23, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for the day to patrol off Palermo Bay.
At 0700 B/23, In position 38°14'N, 13°18'E, ten small fishing boats were seen off Cape Gallo. These did not appear to be going outside the 100 fathom line.
At 0715 B/23, while in position 38°30'N, 13°23'E, a trawler or minesweeper escorting five motor torpedo boats in line ahead came out of Palermo and turned westwards round Cape Gallo.
At 0847 B/23, in position 38°14'N, 13°18'E, a small auxiliary naval vessel left Palermo and turned westward round Cape Gallo at a speed of about 15 knots.
At 1130 B/23, in position 38°08'N, 13°32'E, two two-masted auxiliary schooners were seen close inshore near Cape Zaffarano.
At 2102 B/23, HMS Unbroken surfaced to the east of Palermo Bay in position 38°11'N, 13°50'E.
At 2300 B/23, course was set to patrol in the western end of the patrol area. (27)
24 Apr 1943 At 0517 B/24, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for the day to patrol between Cape Gallo and Cape San Vito.
At 0840 B/24, In position 38°14'N, 13°07'E, several small fishing vessels were seen near Raisi Point.
At 1100 B/24, In position 38°14'N, 13°07'E, a small two-masted schooner was seen coast crawling westwards round Raisi Point.
At 2119 B/24, HMS Unbroken surfaced.
At 2139 B/24, to the north of Cape San Vito, in position 38°24'N, 12°45'E, HMS Unbroken dived to avoid a motor torpedo boat. HMS Unbroken surfaced again at 2259 B/24. (27)
25 Apr 1943 At 0039 B/25, in position 38°31'N, 13°29'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat surfacing again at 0130 B/25.

The Italian motor A/S boats VAS 212 and VAS 213 had sailed from Palermo at 0615 B/24 for an A/S patrol and returned at 1740 B/25. So probably the motor torpedo boats encountered at 2139 B/24 and / or at 0039 B/25 and was one of these two.
At 0523 B/25, HMS Unbroken dived for the day and commenced closing Raisi Point.
At 1615 B/25, in position 38°12'N, 13°03'E, four large landing vessels were seen coast crawling eastwards rounding Cape Gallo.
At 2114 B/25, HMS Unbroken surfaced. (27)
26 Apr 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) attacked but missed an Italian transport with three torpedoes off Palermo, no hits were obtained.
This was the Italian transport Anagni (former French El Mansour, 5818 GRT, built 1933) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Antares. They had just sailed from Palermo for Leghorn (Livorno).

Anagni, and her escort, the Italian destroyer Premuda had sailed from Leghorn at 1730 B/24-1850 B/24 for Palermo. The torpedo boat Antares sailed Naples at 0415 B/25 and relieved Premuda at 0914 B/25 from her escort duty. The destroyer returned to Leghorn at 1940 B/25. Anagni and Antares arrived in Palermo at 1903 B/25. At 1317-1400 B/26, Anagni escorted by Antares sailed from Palermo for Leghorn.
0107 B/26 - Dived in position 38°31'N, 13°29'E, for a possible motor torpedo boat or destroyer / torpedo boat.
The Italian motor A/S boat VAS 213 sailed from Palermo at 2100 B/25 and returned at 2200 B/26 and may have been the vessel sighted.
0135 B/26 - Surfaced.
0508 B/26 - Dived for the day and closed the Gulf of Palermo.

1130 B/26 - In position 38°08'N, 13°22'E, sighted a trawler or minesweeper coming out of Palermo, proceeding to the north-west for about 3 miles after which the vessel turned back. It is considered this ship was sweeping the channel.
The Italian corvette Euterpe sailed from Palermo at 1117 B/26 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1833 B/26. She may not have been sighted by the submarine or perhaps misidentified.
1550 B/26 - Sighted a 6000 ton merchant vessel escorted by a modern torpedo-boat and two aircraft leaving Palermo Bay. Started attack.
1617 B/26 - In position 38°13'N, 13°26'E fired the last three torpedoes from 5500 yards. Went deep after firing and retired to the north. No hits were obtained.
2112 B/26 - Surfaced.
2120 B/26 - Dived for the sound of motor torpedo boat engines.
2130 B/26 - Surfaced and proceeded westwards.

2330 B/26 - Altered course to 360° and passed Unbroken's signal timed 2307 B/26 which contained the following; Torpedoes expended. Returning to Malta through positions 'P', 'Q' and 'R', passing P at 0001 B/28. Possible hit on 6000-tons merchant ship off Palermo at 1617 B/26. (27)

27 Apr 1943 At 0057 B/27, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2108 A/26 which contained the following orders for her; Leave zone 'B' so as to pass position 'P' at 0500 A/28th thence Malta as ordered by patrol instructions.
At 0518 B/27, HMS Unbroken dived for the day.

At 1138 B/27, north of Marettimo, in position 38°15'N, 12°00'E, a large merchant vessel was seen which was closed. This was subsequently identified as a Sicilia-class hospital ship. Enemy course was 340° at 7 knots.

This was probably the small Italian hospital ship Laurana (391 GRT, built 1939) which had sailed from Lampedusa at 1000 B/26 via Pantelleria (she arrived there at 1900 B/26 and left at 2305 B/26) and arrived in Trapani at 1340 B/27. But it was certainly not a 'large' merchant vessel ?!?. Laurana was used to pick up airmen who had ditched into the sea.
At 1615 B/27, smoke was sighted to the southward. Also HE was picked up. Nothing developed though.
At 2117 B/27, HMS Unbroken surfaced.

At 2152 B/27, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1036 B/27 was received which contained the following; Pass position 'P' at 0500 B/28 thence through positions 'Q' and 'R' to arrive at Malta at 0830/30. You may meet HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) between positions 'Q' and 'R'. (51)
28 Apr 1943 At 0533 B/28, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for the day.

At 0916 B/28, well to the west of Marettimo, in position 37°56'N, 11°15'E, smoke was sighted astern. This is thought to be a merchant vessel on an approximate course of 040° and (according to the revolutions counted by Asdic) a speed of approximately 8 knots. Aircraft were patrolling over the smoke.

This was most likely the convoy made up of the German transports KT 5 (834 GRT, built 1943) and KT 14 (834 GRT, built 1943) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Climene. The two German ships had sailed from Tunis at 1400 B/27. Climene sailed from Trapani at 0515 B/28 to escort them. At 0910 B/28, she sighted 18 enemy bombers and shortly after heard several explosions. At 1000 B/28, Climene joined KT 5 and KT 14, the latter had engine defects and could only make 8 knots. At 1030 B/28, Climene sighted a torpedo wake at 500 metres on the port side (fired by HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN)). She turned hard to port, and the torpedo missed a few metres ahead. Shortly after, two torpedo wakes were seen. The torpedo boat increased to full speed, but a torpedo hit amidship, and the vessel immediately took a list of 40 to 50 degrees. The order to abandon ship was given, and she sank in less than three minutes. There were 54 victims. The two German ships dropped a few rubber boats and moved away, and reached Trapani at 1655 B/28. The small Italian hospital ship Laurana (391 GRT, built 1939) sailed from Trapani at 1245 B/28 to look for survivors. MS 34 and MS 24 sailed from Marsala at 1230 B/28, recovered 70 survivors and transferred them to Laurana. They returned to Marsala at 2030 B/28. Laurana lingered in the area and recovered more survivors before returning to Trapani at 0500 B/29. In all, there were 87 survivors.
At 0927 B/28, a series of small explosions like small bombs or cannon shells hitting the water were heard.
At 0950 B/28, the masts and funnels of a merchant vessel were sighted steering about 050°. Range was about 5 miles. (27)
29 Apr 1943 At 1945 B/29, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers proceeding westwards.

In a signal timed 1132 B/29, submarines, including HMS Unbroken had been warned that two destroyers from Malta would operate during the night of 29/30 April in the Marettimo area and that these may be encountered.
The destroyers in question were HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN). (51)
30 Apr 1943 At 1030 A/30, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (also 16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (27)

13 May 1943 At 1700 B/13, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (also 17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Sicily, exact orders would be signalled by the Capt. S.8 after leaving Malta.
HMS Unbroken was routed as follows; Trough positions 36°08'N, 14°22'E and 37°07'N, 13°15'E thence through position 'P' at 1200 B/15. She was to keep to the starboard side of the route.
She was escorted out by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) until 1850 B/13.

At 2001 B/13, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1332 A/13 was received which contained the following orders; After passing position 'P' at 1100 A/15 proceed to patrol in areas 'C', 'E' and 'F' east of 14°00'E. (52)
13 May 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unbroken during her 17th war patrol see the map below.

15 May 1943 At 0052 B/15, south-south-west of Marsala, in position 37°32'N, 12°20'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted vessel proceeding westwards. This was subsequently seen to be a hospital ship.
This was the small Italian hospital ship Meta (243 GRT, built 1929) which sailed from Pantelleria (date and time not given but probably ca. 1800 B/14) and arrived in Trapani at 0400 B/15.
At 0256 B/15, HE was picked up to starboard.
At 0306 B/15, HMS Unbroken turned away for a darkened vessel. This was thought to possibly be HMS Unison (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) which was on passage from patrol back to Malta.
HMS Unison reported nothing but she certainly was in the area.
At 0437 B/15, HMS Unbroken dived for the day surfacing at 2143 B/15. (27)

16 May 1943 At 1400 B/16, near Cape Gallo, in position 38°14'N, 13°20'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted four motor torpedo boat moored inshore and one on the stocks at the shipyard to the north of Mondello. (27)
17 May 1943 At 1530 B/17, off the Milazzo Peninsula, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a tank landing craft proceeding eastwards close inshore.

This was most probably the Italian MZ 774 which had sailed from Augusta and arrived at Milazzo at 1700 B/17 because of defects. It is likely that she was proceeding to Palermo and then turned back due to engine defects to go to Milazzo which could explain that she was going eastward. (27)

19 May 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian pilot vessel (tug) F 20 / Enrica (269 GRT, built 1913) three nautical miles bearing 260° off Pizzo Calabro in position 38°45'N, 16°00'E. It was towing the pontoon Titano from Messina to Salerno. An Italian seaplane alighted and rescued the eight survivors, thirteen were killed or missing. Three minesweepers were sent to the scene and Titano was taken in tow.
0441 B/19 - Dived for the day.
0710 B/19 - Sighted smoke bearing 320°. Closed to investigate.

0857 B/19 - Identified the target as a hospital ship of the Aquilea-class. This was the Aquilea (Italian, 9448 GRT, built 1914) which had sailed from Gaeta at 0615 B/18 and arrived in Naples at 1215 B/18. She sailed from Naples at 1830 B/18 and reached San Nicola (Crete) at 1740 B/22.
1500 B/19 - Sighted a floating sheer-legs towed by a large tug close inshore proceeding north. Started attack.

1547 B/19 - Fired two torpedoes from 3000 yards. 2 minutes and 6 seconds after firing a hit was heard followed swiftly by breaking up noises. On returning to periscope depth the tug had sunk. The sheer-legs appeared to be beached. The escorting seaplane landed and picked up the survivors.
1615 B/19 - A motor torpedo boat appeared on the scene. Went deep and retired westwards.
1658 B/19 - A distant depth charge was heard to explode.
2133 B/19 - Surfaced.

[On 30 October 2020 information was published in the newspaper Il Quotidiano Del Sud that the wreck of the Erica had been dived and positively identified. For the identification proces the divers had been assisted by Platon Alexiades, Lorenzo Colombo and Franco Vallone.] (27)

20 May 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) attacked a tug with two torpedoes north-west of Longobardi, Cosenza, Italy. No hits were obtained. This was the Italian tug Costante (100 GRT, built 1914) towing dockyard machinery. She opened fire on the periscope.
0433 B/20 - Dived for the day.
1030 B/20 - Sighted tug towing a large square object proceeding south close inshore. Closed.

1117 B/20 - In position 39°14'N, 16°01'E fired two torpedoes at the tug. These missed ahead. The tug's crew manned their gun forward and fired at the periscope so gun action was not considered practical.
1129 B/20 - Withdrew to the north-west.
2148 B/20 - Surfaced.

2205 B/20 - Received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1242 A/20 which contained the following orders for her; Leave patrol, pass south of Ustica so as to pass position 'P' at 0300 A/24. Further routing to Malta would be signalled by the Capt. S.10. (27)

21 May 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Bologna (former French Monaco, 5439 GRT, built 1917), 8 nautical miles bearing 210° of Cape Vaticano in position 38°34'N, 15°44'E.

The convoy, which besides the Bologna was made up of the the Italian transports Siena (former French Astrée, 2147 GRT, built 1921) and Polluce (1049 GRT, built 1917), escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Groppo, sailed from Naples at 1850 B/20. The torpedo boat Orione sailed from Messina at 0010 B/21 and joined the convoy at 0530 B/21 in position 39°51'30"N, 15°39'E. At 1750 B/21, Bologna was hit by a torpedo on the port side and immediately took a heavy list. At 1755 B/21, three explosions were heard which were probably torpedoes at the end of their course. After the sinking of Bologna, Siena and Polluce went on, while Orione remained behind to hunt the submarine. Bologna sank at 1757 B/21 and Groppo picked up the survivors, then rejoined the convoy, which arrived in Messina at 2035 B/21. Orione could not get a sonar contact and at 2300 B/21, abandoned the search and arrived in Messina at 0217 B/22. [No info on casualties and survivors available to us.]
0239 B/21 - Sighted a darkened vessel.
0244 B/21 - Dived and closed but the target passed rapidly out of range to the northward. It is considered it may have been a destroyer / torpedo boat.
This was probably the Italian torpedo boat Orione which had sailed from Messina at 0010 B/21 to proceed northwards and meet and escort the Bologna-convoy arriving from Naples.
0335 B/21 - Surfaced.
0438 B/21 - Dived for the day.

1045 B/21 - In position 38°42'N, 15°48'E, sighted two small motor minesweepers proceeding in line abreast close inshore on a southerly course. The enemy turned round abreast Cape Vaticano and swept back up the coast.
1517 B/21, In position 38°28'N, 15°48'E, two motor torpedo boats were sighted proceeding southwards close inshore.
This were most probably the Italian motor A/S boats VAS 204 and VAS 221, which sailed from Vibo Valentia at 1305 B/21 for a patrol and returned at 2035 B/21.
1605 B/21, In position 38°29'N, 15°48'E, sighted a tug proceeding northwards close inshore. An aircraft was seen overhead.
This was most probably the naval tug Polifemo (1034 tons) which sailed from Messina at 1305 B/21 and arrived at Vibo Valentia at 2015 B/21.
1655 B/21 - Sighted smoke of a convoy made up of three merchant ships and two destroyers. Closed.
1744 B/21 - Fired four torpedoes from about 3500 yards at a 4000 tons (the largest) merchant vessel. An explosion was heard 2m 21s after firing. Unbroken had gone to 120 feet on firing.
1753 B/21 - Depth charging commenced but they were dropped a good way off. Withdrew to the north-west.

1834 B/21 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw one of the destroyers stopped in the position of the attack, possibly picking up survivors. The other destroyer was hunting. The two smaller merchant vessels could still be seen but there was no sign of the larger one.
2203 B/21 - Surfaced and passed between Stromboli and Panarea.

22 May 1943 At 1940 B/22, well to the north-east of Palermo in position 38°45'N, 13°53'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted the italian repatriation ships Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) and Saturnia (23940 GRT, built 1927). They were westbound.
In a signal timed 1731 A/21 the Capt. S.8 had informed all submarines at sea that these ships would be proceeded from Trieste to Gibraltar and were not to be molested with. (53)
24 May 1943 At 0004 A/24, north-west of Marettimo, in position 38°10'N, 11°53'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for the sound of a motor torpedo boat.
At 0059 A/24, HMS Unbroken surfaced and continued her passage.
At 0445 A/24, HMS Unbroken dived for the day.

At 1705 A/24, in position 37.35'N, 12.15'E, an empty life boat with the name 'Monginevro - Genova' on it was seen on the stern. The number 3 was diplayed on the bow. [This was thus a life boat from the Italian transport Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940) which had been sunk by aircraft on 17 April 1943, while en-route from Bizerta to Trapani.]
At 2156 A/24, HMS Unbroken surfaced. (27)

25 May 1943 At 0200 B/25, south-west of Cape San Marco, in approximate position 37°15'N, 12°50'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted four motor torpedo boats proceeding northwards. Dived to evade.

This were most likely boats of the German 7th S-Boot Flotilla which was operating with six boats in the Pantelleria area during the night of 24/25 May. [No further exact details available for the moment. The KTB of the 7th S-Boot Flotilla for this period has not been digitised after the war by the British / Americans. It should however be available at the German BundesArchiv / MilitärArchiv at Freiburg (D).] The four boats sighted were most likely four out of the following six; S 151, S 152, S 155, S 156, S 157, S 158. [S 153 and S 154 were not serviceable due to defects.]

There were quite a few movements to and from Pantelleria during 24/25 May: 1) The landing vessels / lighters MZ 708 and MZ 790 sailed from Porto Empedocle at 2207 B/24 and arrived in Pantelleria at 0817 B/25.. 2) MAS 483, MS 24, MS 36 and MS 32, also sailed from Porto Empedocle escorting four F-lighters to Pantelleria where they arrived at 0700 B/25. [One of which was most likely the German F 483 another the Italian MZ 708.] 3) MZ 727 sailed from Trapani at 0500 B/24 and arrived in Pantelleria at 0817 B/25. 4) MAS 564 sailed from Mazara del Vallo ?/24 at and returned at 1200 B/25. 5) The hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) sailed from Pantelleria at 2115 B/24 with wounded and arrived in Trapani at 0750 B/25. 6) MAS 547, MAS 548 and MAS 557 sailed from Pantelleria and arrived at Mazara del Vallo.
At 0302 B/25, HMS Unbroken surfaced and proceeded.
At 0436 B/25, HMS Unbroken dived for the day.
At 2125 B/25, HMS Unbroken surfaced. (54)
26 May 1943 At 1115 A/26, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (also 17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0810 A/26. (27)

6 Jun 1943 Around 1720 A/6, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (also 18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Squillace and the Gulf of Taranto (Area 'Q').
En-route to the patrol area she was to pass through the following positions; 35°14'N, 15°35'E at 0800 B/7 and 38°24'N, 17°32'E at 0001 B/10.
10 Jun 1943 At 1159 B/10, in position 38°47'N, 17°38'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a Sicilia-class hospital ship proceeding on course 230°.
This was the Italian Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which sailed from Bari at 1050 B/9 and arrived in Naples at 0725 B/12. (27)
11 Jun 1943 At 0955 B/1, south-east of Cape Colonne, in position 38°57'N, 17°17'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted two A/S vessels passing inshore. (27)
12 Jun 1943 At 0005 B/12, in position 38°42'N, 17°27'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for two small vessels thought to be possibly A/S trawlers.
At 1530 B/12, while north of Crotone, smoke and upperworks of a small vessel was sighted. It passed out of range and entered Crotone.

At 1755 B/12, while east of Cape Colonne, in position 39°00'N, 17°27'E, the upperworks of a large vessel was seen proceeding northwards. This is thought to be one of the safe conduct vessels HMS Unbroken had been informed about. (27)

13 Jun 1943 At 0359 B/13, west of cape Santa Maria di Leuca, in position 39°49'N, 17°52'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a stopped A/S schooner. HMS Unbroken dived to avoid being detected.
At 1300 B/13, west of Gallipoli, in position 39°58'N, 17°34'E, an A/S schooner was seen proceeding on course 135°.

At 1900 B/13, it was discovered that HMS Unbroken had collected some extraneous matter round the port shaft. Efforts were made to clear it by going ahead and astern but the shaft remained extremely noisy at higher speeds.
At 2201 B/13, south-west of Cape Santa Maria di Leuca, in position 39°32'N, 18°07'E, HMS Unbroken sighted a westbound hospital ship.
At 2242 B/13, HMS Unbroken dived to avoid being sighted by the hospital ship. The submarine surfaced again after half an hour.
This was most probably the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which sailed from Bari at 0500 B/13 and arrived in Taranto at 0740 B/14. (27)

14 Jun 1943 At 1003 B/14, while of Cape Santa Maria di Leuca, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a minesweepers sweeping off the coast to the west of the cape. Course of the enemy vessel were 110°, 290° and 240°.
At 1020 B/14, to the south-east of the Cape, another minesweeper was seen proceeding on course 110°. (27)
15 Jun 1943 At 0915 B/15, while near Cape Santa Maria di Leuca, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats close inshore steering west.
At 0920 B/15, two distant explosions were heard.
At 0957 B/15, a minesweeper was sighted off Cape Santa Maria di Leuca proceeding on courses 110°, 290° and 240°, just like the day before.
At 2158 B/15, HMS Unbroken surfaced and left the area to patrol off Crotone. (27)
16 Jun 1943 In the morning, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) attacked an A/S schooner with one torpedo. It missed. This was the armed schooner Misuraca.

In the evening Unbroken attacked what was thought to be a medium seized tanker with four torpedoes off Crotone. No hits were obtained and it was later thought that the target was an A/S vessel instead of a tanker. Possibly this was the naval ammunition transport Vallelunga (1071 tons, built 1924) escorted by the torpedo boat Sagittario. They had just sailed from Crotone for Messina.
0400 B/16 - Dived.
0715 B/16 - In position 39°18'N, 17°20'E, sighted an A/S schooner. Closed.

0940 B/16 - The schooner was seen to be stopped in position 39°19'N, 17°21'E. Fired one torpedo from 850 yards. The torpedo ran under and exploded at the end of its run. The target was not considered worth another torpedo.
1943 B/16 - In position 39°01'N, 17°17'E, sighted smoke to the northwards.

2052 B/16 - Sighted a torpedo-boat (a modern one) steering south towards Crotone. About 3 nautical miles off the harbour stopped and was joined by what was thought to be a medium seized tanker. The torpedo-boat joined up ahead and both ships moved south towards Unbroken. Started attack.

2114 B/16 - In position 39°04'N, 17°17'E fired four torpedoes at the tanker from about 2500 yards. It was almost dark and the target was not very clear. No hits were obtained. Unbroken had gone deep and retired from the scene. After the attack it was thought that the target was not a tanker but a much smaller A/S vessel.
2300 B/16 - Surfaced. (27)

17 Jun 1943 In a signal timed 0150 B/17, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN); Leave patrol to pass through position 38°19'N, 17°19'E at 2300 B/17 thence through 36°26'N, 15°30'E at 1200 B/19 thence through position 36°26'N, 14°21'E to arrive in the Malta West Sanctuary rendezvous at 0830 B/20. (56)
20 Jun 1943 Around 1050 B/30, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (also 18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN), were escorted in by a minesweeper [not identified] which had joined around 0830 B/30. (27)
27 Jun 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) was docked in No.3 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard.
[It is currently not known to us when she was undocked.]

3 Jul 1943 Around 0020 B/3, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (also 19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Taranto as part of a patrol line of five submarines to provide cover during Operation Husky, the Allied landings on Sicily.

The other submarines in the patrol line were HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski), HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) and HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN). HMS Tactician was assigned to the patrol line temporarily until HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) could be sailed to take over.
For the daily positions of HMS Unbroken during this patrol see the map below.
6 Jul 1943 At 1230 B/6, in position 39°30'N, 17°41'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a two-funnelled hospital ship passing the north. The vessel was eastbound.
This was most probably the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which sailed from Taranto at 0500 B/6 and arrived in Bari at 0930 B/7. (27)
11 Jul 1943 At 1418 B/11, in approximate position 39°25'N, 17°24'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted smoke to the north but nothing materialised. (27)
13 Jul 1943 At 0015 B/13, in position 39°29'N, 17°40'E, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a small darkened vessel bearing 255°
At 0027 B/13, two small darkened vessels were seen bearing 290°. HMS Unbroken dived. Whilst dived the HE of three small diesel engined vessels was heard. They appeared to be hunting.
At 0248 B/13, HMS Unbroken surfaced.
At 0314 B/13, the HE was heard again so HMS Unbroken dived again and remained dived for the day.
At 0840 B/13, smoke was sighted to the westward apparently moving westwards along the coast.
At 2101 B/13, HMS Unbroken surfaced. (27)

13 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1401 B/13, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) to leave patrol P.M./15 routed along 18°E to 35°50'N passing this position at 1400 B/17. Thence course 270° to make rendezvous with HMS Swona (T/Lt. E.C. Davids, RNR) at the end of eastern swept channel at 1000 B/18. Surface passage on 17th and 18th as necessary. (57)

14 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1732 B/14, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN); After passing through position 'M' (44°00'N, 06°00'W) on route 'Gold' proceed direct to conduct an anti U-Boat patrol in zone 'J 9' bounded by lat. 45°40'N / 45°01'N and long. 05°20'W / 05°50'W. You are not to enter zone 'J 9' before 0800 A/17.<.br> You will probably be recalled to arrive off The Lizard A.M./25. (58)

15 Jul 1943 At 1840 B/15, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) heard a few distant explosions. These were later considered to be related to HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) sinking the Italian submarine Remo.
At 2100 B/15, HMS Unbroken surfaced and left patrol in accordance with the orders received in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1401 B/13. (27)
18 Jul 1943 Around 1400 B/18, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (also 19th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Beryl (T/Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR), which had joined around 1005 B/18. (27)
30 Jul 1943 At 1445 B/30, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. Passage, until Algiers, was made in a convoy.
HMS Unbroken was to proceed to the U.K. to refit.
For the daily positions of HMS Unbroken during this passage see the map below.

30 Jul 1943 A convoy, made up of, at least, the monitor HMS Abercrombie (A/Capt. G.V.B. Faulkner, RN), submarine HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN), landing ship HMS LST 321 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. C.H. Metcalfe, RNR) and the merchant vessels (ammunition carrier) Gudrun Maersk (British (former Danish), 2294 GRT, built 1937) and Abner Nash (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942) departed Malta for Algiers.
It is possible more ships were part of the convoy. [Little information on this convoy has been found.]

The convoy was escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Holcombe (Cdr. S.H. Pinchin, DSC, RN), HMS Atherstone (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Wood, DSC, RNVR), HMS Liddesdale (Lt.Cdr. A.A. Mackenzie, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Hyderabad (T/Lt. T. Cooper, RNR), HMS Rhododendron (T/Lt. O.B. Medley, RNVR) and HMS Coltsfoot (T/Lt. G.W. Rayner, RNVR).
Around 1130 B/31, HMS Abercrombie left the convoy to proceed to Bizerta. She took one of the escorts with her but this vessel apparently later rejoined the convoy.
Around 1930 B/31, HMS LST 321 parted company with the convoy to proceed to Bizerta.

At 0530 B/2, HMS Unbroken parted company with the convoy off Algiers Bay. She was to proceed to Gibraltar taking HMS Coltsfoot with her as escort. The remaining ships of the convoy entered Algiers. (59)
4 Aug 1943 Around 1230 B/4, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) and HMS Coltsfoot (T/Lt. G.W. Rayner, RNVR) arrived at Gibraltar. (60)
8 Aug 1943 Around 1120 B/8, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for passage to the U.K.

She was routed as follows; Position 'Q' (35°49'N, 06°06'W) at 1600 B/8. Position 'P' (35°53'N, 06°15'W) at 1700 B/8. Position 'R' (36°37'N, 07°24'W) at 0100 B/9. Position 'T' (36°51'N, 09°13'W) at 0030 B/10. Position 'U' (38°50'N, 10°01'W) at 0600 B/11. Position 'O' (43°01'N, 10°01'W) at 0600 B/14. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).
While en-route she was ordered to patrol off the north-west coast corner of Spain and later to conduct an anti U-boat patrol in the Bay of Biscay making this passage her 20th war patrol.

12 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1103 B/12, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN); Prom position 'O' (43°01'N, 10°01'E), proceed coastwise diving by day inside the 100 fathom line and rejoin route 'Gold' in longitude 07°00'W. Adjust speed to rendezvous with an escort as arranged by the Capt. S.5 in position 'X' (180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles) at 1400 A/20. (58)

15 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1233 A/15, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following information to HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN); My 1732 B/15. Zone 'J 9' is covered by submerged bombing restrictions. (58)

16 Aug 1943 At 0546 B/16, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) dived for a lighted fishing vessel with a small darkened vessel in company. The dark object was considered to be too large to be a submarine. This all looked very suspicious.
At 1200 B/16, HMS Unbroken was in position 44°03'N, 06°46'W. Several tunny fishing vessels were encountered near this position. All had Spanish markings.
More tunny fishing vessel would be sighted on the 17th and 18th.

At 1329 B/16, the signal timed 1121 A/16 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) was received. This signal contained the following orders; Cancel my 1732 B/14. Proceed direct to establish an anti U-Boat patrol in zone 'J 8' bounded by parallels 45°00'N and a line drawn 75° through 44°25'N, 05°00'W and longitudes 05°00'W and 03°00'W. You are covered by submerged bombing restrictions. (61)

19 Aug 1943 At 0420 B/19, in position 44°40'N, 04°25'W, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) heard distinct HE passing rapidly on the starboard side and fade away right ahead. The change of bearing was rapid. Nothing could be seen.
At 0628 B/19, HMS Unbroken dived for the day.
During the day a few tunny fishing vessels were sighted.

At 1711 B/19, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 1146 A/19 which contained the following orders; Leaves patrol P.M./21 so as to pass through position 'K.A.' (45°25'N, 06°01'W at 0800 A/22, thence via route 'Gold' to arrive in position 'Y.Y.' (180° - Bishop Rock - 12 miles at 0800 A/25 for onward escort to Portsmouth as will be arranged by the Capt. S.5.

At 2141 A/19, the F.O.S. signal timed 1609 A/19. In this signal the orders contained in the signal timed 1146 A/19 were amended. [We have been unable to find the text of the signal timed 1609 A/19.] (61)
21 Aug 1943 P.M. on this day, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) left patrol in accordance with the orders of the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.). (27)
22 Aug 1943 At 0532 B/22, in position 45°56'N, 06°01'W, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) sighted a darkened vessel fairly close.
At 0547 B/22, HMS Unbroken dived.

By 0717 B/22, HMS Unbroken had returned to the position of the sighting at 0532 B/22. A sailing ketch was seen which was probably the darkened vessel sighted that morning. HMS Unbroken then continued her passage to the U.K. (27)

25 Aug 1943 Around 0850 B/25, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) made rendezvous with FFS Rubis (Lt.Cdr. H.L.G. Rousselot) and the escort, the patrol vessel Chasseur 11. They then proceeded to Dartmouth where the arrived around 1730 B/25. (27)
26 Aug 1943 Around 0650 B/26, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Dartmouth for Portsmouth (Gosport) where she arrived around 1725 B/26 ending her 19th war patrol.
On the passage she was escorted by the patrol vessel Chasseur 11. (27)
15 Sep 1943 Around 1700 A/15, HMS Safari (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for the Clyde. They were escorted by Chasseur 5.
From around 2000 A/15 to around 0600 A/15, they paused in Yarmouth Roads before continuing their passage.

At 1930 A/16, rendezvous was made south of Eddystone with HMS Whalsay (T/Lt. F.J.S. Crawford, RNVR) and HMS Mangrove (T/Lt. J.K.M. Warde, RNVR) which took over the escort duties of the two submarines. (62)
18 Sep 1943 At 2300 A/18, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) arrived in Rothesay Bay. (27)
19 Sep 1943 Around 0715 A/19, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay Bay for Holy Loch where she arrived around 0900 A/19. (27)
21 Sep 1943 Around 1530 A/21, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) and HMS Viking (Lt. R. Bannar-Martin, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow.

Around 1730 A/21, they made rendezvous with HMS Stoic (Lt. P.B. Marriott, DSO, RN) and HMS Varangian (Lt. J. Nash, DSC, RN) which both were to proceed to Lerwick. Their escort, HMS La Capricieuse (Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) also joined. (27)
23 Sep 1943 Around 0845 A/23, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow from Holy Loch.
Around 1945 A/23 she departed Scapa Flow for Blyth. Passage was made together with HMS Uther (Lt. P.S. Beale, RN) that was to proceed to Dundee.
The submarines were escorted by HMS Scalby Wyke (A/Skr.Lt. C.A. Grimmer, RNR). (27)
24 Sep 1943 Around 2325 A/24, HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) arrived at Blyth where she was to refit. (27)
29 Sep 1943 HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) commenced refit at the Blyth Dry Dock Co.
8 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted post refit trials off Blyth.
9 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Blyth following which she conducted D/G trials.
12 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (63)
14 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (63)
24 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (63)
28 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (63)
29 Feb 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (63)
1 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
2 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
3 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
8 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
9 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
10 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
13 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
14 Mar 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (64)
16 Mar 1944 Around 0630 A/16, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) departed Blyth for Lerwick.

The Admiralty was under the impression that the damaged German battleship Tirpitz might be leaving Norway for Germany to effect repairs. So a whole lot of submarines were sent to patrol off Norway including several submarines from training flotillas. (27)

17 Mar 1944 At 0225 A/17, HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 0017 A/17. The signal was however a bit currupt. It did however contain the route and patrol position for HMS Unbroken. As the sailing orders had stated that the submarine may be diverted from the passage to Lerwick to proceed immediately on patrol it was thought this was meant to be the case and course was set to comply with the route given in the signal. [We have been unable to find the lat. / long. position for the coded route and patrol position mentioned in this signal.]
At 1805 A/17, the F.O.S. signal timed 1719 A/17 was received which ordered HMS Unbroken to proceed to Lerwick forthwith. Course was set to comply with this order.
Around 2045 A/17, HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) arrived at Lerwick.
Around 2300 A/17, she departed Lerwick for her 21st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the Norwegian coast to the north of Bergen.
She was routed through the following positions; 090° - Muckle Flugga - 15 miles and 61°01'N, 00°50'E to patrol off Utvaer.
19 Mar 1944 At 0847 A/19, in position 60°56'N, 04°37.1'E (entrance of Sognefjord), HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) struck bottom at a depth of 70 feet. No damage was sustained. (27)

21 Mar 1944 At 0145 A/21, HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) set course to return to Lerwick in accordance with the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2308 A/20. Around 1815 A/21, HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) ended her 21st war patrol at Lerwick. (27)

24 Mar 1944 Around 0030 A/24, HMS Unbending (Lt. J.D. Martin, DSC, RN), HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) and HMS Trusty (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Lerwick for Blyth or Dundee (HMS Trusty). They were escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (27)
25 Mar 1944 Around 1700 A/25, HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) arrived at Blyth from Lerwick. (27)
1 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
5 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
6 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
7 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
10 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
11 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
13 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
14 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
20 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
21 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners. (65)
25 Apr 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) conducted exercises off Blyth with a training class of new submariners.
[No log is available for May 1944, so some details for this month may be missing.] (65)
5 May 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) departed Blyth for Rosyth.
6 May 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) arrived at Rosyth.
30 May 1944 HMS Unbroken (Lt. P.L. Langley-Smith, RN) is decommissioned on this date and loaned to the U.S.S.R.
